report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared about your 83-year-old family member in Klickitat County — living in a family member's home, needing help with transferring, diagnosed with dementia, divorced, with Medicare coverage, income between $1,000–$2,999/month, and assets between $2,000–$85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. However, a few key pieces of information (like whether she's already on Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, how many daily activities she needs help with, whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, and any recent hospital stay) are still missing. Those answers make a real difference in which programs actually fit, so please treat this as a starting point for questions to ask, not a determination of what she'll receive. Nothing here means she does or doesn't qualify for any program — only the administering agency can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is covered by Medicare, needs help with an activity of daily living (transferring), and lives in a home setting rather than a nursing facility — all of which line up with the basic building blocks of this Medicare benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and home health aide help with things like walking and bathing, when ordered by a doctor and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
There is no cost to the beneficiary for the covered home health services themselves, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
Eligibility requires a homebound status finding and a face-to-face assessment and order from a health care provider.

nextSteps: Ask her doctor whether she meets the 'homebound' definition and whether a referral for a face-to-face assessment makes sense
If a referral is made, the provider can supply a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies in Klickitat County

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is 83 and needs help with transferring, which is one of the ADLs this program is built around, and her income and asset levels fall in ranges that can sometimes align with Medicaid-related programs. However, whether she is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and how many total ADLs she needs help with, are both unknown — and both are required pieces for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, eating, and mobility, plus related services such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and training for caregivers.

To keep receiving these services at a later review, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility is based on regular community Medicaid rules rather than institutional rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether she is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities (ADLs) she needs help with in total
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
If not already on Apple Health, look into applying for community Medicaid coverage alongside the CFC assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is covered by Medicare, which includes Part A, but this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and it isn't known whether she has had one recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical/occupational/speech therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period.

This coverage generally requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status) shortly before SNF admission, plus a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether she has had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask the hospital discharge team whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes

programs.whyThisMayApply: She is over 55, and her monthly income and countable assets both appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits as described in the program materials. What's unknown is whether she needs nursing-facility level of care (based on how many ADLs she needs help with) and whether she is already enrolled in community Medicaid, which would point elsewhere. It's also important to know that this program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits set well above regular Medicaid amounts.

As of the information reviewed, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, and new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (ADLs) she needs help with, to assess nursing-facility level of care
Whether she is currently enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid (CN/ABP), which would make TSOA not applicable
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status and functional screening
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: She has dementia and needs help with transferring, suggesting an ongoing care need, and she lives in a family member's home, which raises the possibility of an unpaid caregiver — but whether someone is providing regular unpaid care, and how many hours per week, is not yet known, and that detail matters for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) can arrange planned short-term care ranging from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility — along with caregiver training, support groups, and counseling; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid care for her, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite eligibility and current availability

followUpQuestions: Is she currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has she applied for community Medicaid coverage?
Besides transferring, does she need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around the house? About how many of these does she need help with?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid care for her, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has she had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently (as an admitted inpatient, not just observation)?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared — a 71-year-old in Wahkiakum County living at home with dementia, needing help with transferring, bathing, eating, and dressing, and already enrolled in both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) — the screening found five programs worth a closer look. Two (Medicaid Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit) line up closely with the information provided. Three others could be relevant but depend on details not yet collected, such as whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved or whether there's been a recent hospital stay. This report is an educational starting point only — it does not determine eligibility. Only the agency that runs each program can confirm that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program appears worth investigating because the person is already on Apple Health (Medicaid) and needs help with several daily activities — transferring, bathing, eating, and dressing — which is at least the level of need this program is designed to support.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can help pay for personal care support with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and toileting, plus related tasks such as meal prep or housekeeping when help with those is also needed.
It can also include relief (respite) care, a personal emergency response button, assistive technology, skills training, and training on how to manage a caregiver.

Services can be provided in the person's own home, and this program is generally available to people already on community Medicaid who have an assessed, ongoing need for help with three or more daily living activities.

nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment.
Ask whether combining this program with a waiver (CFC+COPES) could add more wraparound services if needed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program appears worth investigating because the person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs that could involve skilled nursing or aide-level help.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit can cover part-time skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as bathing and dressing) when this is ordered by a doctor and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered services generally cost $0 to the beneficiary, though durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a health care provider must do a face-to-face assessment and certify that the person is homebound and needs skilled care.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor about ordering a home health evaluation.
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies that serve Wahkiakum County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program could be relevant because the person is over 55, is enrolled in Apple Health, lives at home, and appears to need a level of care consistent with nursing-facility level of care — but it's not known whether there is an unpaid family caregiver, and the program is currently not accepting new enrollees.

whatItCovers.text: Medicaid Alternative Care supports unpaid family caregivers of people 55 and older who are on Apple Health and meet nursing facility level of care, offering caregiver assistance such as respite care, adult day services, home-delivered meals, training, and specialized equipment.
Enrollment in this program has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver, age 18 or older, who regularly helps with care.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status.
Clarify whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved in day-to-day care.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program could be worth investigating because the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A, but it's not yet known whether there has been a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which this benefit requires.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover a short stay in a skilled nursing facility — including room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days as an admitted inpatient.
Days 1–20 have no daily charge, but days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance, and the person pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (time spent under observation status doesn't count).
Whether a doctor has ordered or would order daily skilled nursing or therapy care after such a stay.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is being considered or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay.
Ask a doctor whether daily skilled care needs would meet Medicare's SNF criteria.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This could be worth investigating because ongoing care and supervision appear to be needed due to the dementia diagnosis and multiple ADL needs — but it's not yet known whether an unpaid caregiver is providing regular care, which these programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Washington has several respite options for unpaid caregivers, including Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (short-term in-home, adult day, or facility-based respite plus caregiver training and support groups), and DDA respite for people with a developmental disability.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40 or more hours per week of care, while the Family Caregiver Support Program serves caregivers of adults 18+ with eligibility and service levels set locally through a caregiver screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If there is a caregiver, note roughly how many hours per week they provide care, since this affects which respite option may fit.

followUpQuestions: Is there a family member or friend who provides unpaid, regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (not just for observation) recently?
Would it help to talk through how the reported countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) compare to the specific resource rules for each program?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven programs that Washington's rules engine flagged as possibly or likely relevant based on the details provided: 92 years old, living at home in Franklin County, needing help with transferring, a stroke diagnosis, single, monthly income under $1,000, countable assets under $2,000, and coverage through both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid). Several programs line up well on the financial and age criteria that were answered, but a few important pieces of information are still missing — mainly how many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, or moving around) require help, whether there is an unpaid caregiver in the picture, and whether there has been a recent hospital stay. Because those gaps affect most of the programs on this list, please treat this as a starting point rather than a set of recommendations, and use the follow-up questions below to gather more detail before contacting an agency. None of the programs described here can be confirmed as ones the family qualifies for — only the administering agency can make that determination.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has a reported need for help with transferring along with a stroke diagnosis, which suggests a possible skilled or personal care need. All the rule checks for this program came back as met based on the information given.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help with transferring, bathing, or walking) when it is provided alongside skilled nursing or therapy services.

To qualify, a person generally needs to be considered 'homebound' — meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended because of a medical condition — and a doctor must certify the need for skilled care after a face-to-face visit.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers the person 'homebound' under Medicare's definition.
Whether a doctor has ordered or would order skilled nursing, therapy, or aide services related to the stroke and transfer needs.
nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment and order for home health services.
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile shows an age of 92 (meeting the 65+ standard), a monthly income under $1,000 (within COPES's income limits), and countable assets under $2,000 (within the resource limit), and the person already has Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage. However, COPES also requires a functional assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and only one care need — help with transferring — was reported, so this part of the picture is not yet clear.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services on top of regular Medicaid personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, nursing services, and help transitioning to a new home setting.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find that the person needs a nursing-facility level of care — generally meaning unmet needs in several daily activities (like transferring, bathing, or eating) or supervision needs due to memory or thinking problems, plus an unmet daily-activity need.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person needs help with additional daily activities beyond transferring (this determines whether the nursing-facility level of care standard is met).
nextSteps: Contact the local Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment.
If not already connected to HCS, submit the Apple Health LTC financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), which is the financial pathway CFC uses for community-based clients. The reported need for help with transferring suggests some personal care need, but it isn't yet known whether the person has unmet needs in three or more daily activities, which CFC uses to determine ongoing eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and medication management, along with related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.
To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, based on a CARE assessment.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, or transferring) the person needs help with.
nextSteps: Since Apple Health coverage is already in place, ask HCS about scheduling just the functional CARE assessment.
Call the regional HCS intake line for Franklin County to start this process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 92 years old, has income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000 (both within this program's limits), and already has Apple Health coverage. Apple Health LTC also requires a functional finding of nursing-facility level of care, which has not yet been established from the information given.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care, and it also serves as the financial gateway to home-and-community-based waivers like COPES, PACE, and community programs such as CFC.

Eligibility includes meeting Apple Health's general rules, plus either being in a medical institution or receiving qualifying home/community-based services, along with income and functional requirements.

informationStillNeeded: Results of a functional (CARE) assessment showing whether a nursing-facility level of care is met.
nextSteps: Contact HCS to request a CARE assessment, since financial pieces (income and assets) already appear to align with the program's limits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare coverage, which is the base requirement for this benefit. However, the profile does not mention a recent hospital stay, and this program only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, so it's unclear whether this situation currently fits.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing facility stays — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services — for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

This is not a long-term custodial care benefit; it requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status) and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If there is or was a recent hospitalization, ask the hospital discharge planner whether the stay would qualify for Medicare SNF coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is over 55 and appears to have Apple Health coverage and lives at home, which are part of this program's requirements. However, it's not known whether the nursing-facility level of care standard is met, and — since the profile describes the person as single with no caregiver mentioned — it's unclear whether there is an unpaid family caregiver, which this program is built around supporting. This program is also currently paused statewide with only a waitlist available.

whatItCovers.text: MAC offers caregiver support benefits in steps — a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500/year, then (for those with greater needs) an amount around $928/month — for things like respite care, housekeeping help, adult day care, and caregiver training and equipment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person meets a nursing-facility level of care.
Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (age 18+) providing regular care.
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver involved in this person's care, contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The stroke diagnosis and need for help with transferring suggest ongoing care needs, but the profile describes the person as single, and it isn't known whether a family member or friend provides unpaid care — which is central to this program's eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs can offer short-term relief for unpaid caregivers, including Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year per household) and Family Caregiver Support Program services such as planned short-term in-home or facility care, counseling, and caregiver training.

Lifespan Respite generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care; the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults needing care, with services varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) provides regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver, contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options.

followUpQuestions: Beyond help with transferring, does the person need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, walking, or taking medication?
Is there any confusion, memory loss, or wandering that requires supervision?
Does a family member or friend provide regular unpaid care or supervision? If so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has the person had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently (as opposed to observation status)?
Is the person currently receiving any paid home care, adult day care, skilled nursing visits, or other in-home services?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a married 87-year-old living at home in Adams County who has had a stroke, needs help with toileting, is covered by Medicare, has monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range, and has countable assets under $2,000. Several important details are still unknown — such as how many daily activities need help with beyond toileting, whether there's any cognitive impairment, the exact income amount, whether the household is enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether there's an unpaid caregiver, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Because these missing pieces affect several programs, this report should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a conclusion about what the family qualifies for. The follow-up questions below can help sharpen the picture before contacting any agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family is already covered by Medicare and is living at home rather than in a facility, and the toileting help need combined with a stroke history suggests there may be a skilled or personal care need — the specific combination this benefit is built around.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only alongside skilled care), at no cost for the covered services, for people who are homebound and have care ordered by a provider.
It does not cover round-the-clock care at home, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only care needed.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment to document homebound status and the need for skilled care
If eligible, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 87 with a stroke diagnosis and a need for help with toileting, living at home in Adams County, and with countable assets already under the $2,000 Medicaid resource limit, this family fits the age and resource pieces the screening looked at for COPES. Whether it's a good fit further depends on details the screening doesn't have yet, like the full extent of daily-activity help needed and the exact monthly income.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home and community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, specialized equipment, and transportation — for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

To be considered, a person generally needs an assessment finding a nursing-facility level of care need — based on things like needing help with several activities of daily living (including toileting), needing nurse-level care, or needing supervision due to cognitive impairment.

Financially, COPES generally looks at income against a Special Income Level ($2,982/month in 2026) or a lower Medically Needy level, and at resources against a $2,000 limit for a single person (higher protections apply when there's a spouse still living in the community).

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (beyond toileting) need help with, to see whether the nursing-facility-level-of-care test might be met
The exact monthly household income, to compare against the program's income limits
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting form HCA 18-005
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: The stroke diagnosis and need for help with toileting suggest a personal-care need that CFC is designed around, and the family's low countable assets are consistent with Medicaid-linked programs. However, the screening doesn't yet know whether the family is enrolled in community Medicaid or how many activities of daily living need help with, both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help with daily tasks like toileting, bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver training, for people living at home or in a residential care setting.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and needs to be eligible for regular community Medicaid rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the family is currently enrolled in (or would be eligible for) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid
How many activities of daily living, beyond toileting, need regular help
nextSteps: If not already on Apple Health, submit the LTSS financial application
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being 87 years old and having countable assets under $2,000 line up with pieces of Apple Health LTC's age and resource rules, and this program is the financial gateway to services like COPES that could support toileting help at home. The nursing-facility-level-of-care question and the precise income amount still need to be worked out.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage that acts as the financial gateway to long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and community programs like COPES, once both financial and functional eligibility are established.

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared — a 69-year-old in Franklin County living in a family member's home, married, needing hands-on help with bathing and toileting, monthly income under $1,000, countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and Medicare coverage — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. The Medicaid-based programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) show promising signs on age, care need, and income, but the resource test can't be checked yet because the exact asset amount and some spousal financial details are still unknown. Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) lines up well with the income and asset figures given and doesn't require Medicaid, though it's important to know that statewide enrollment is currently paused and new applicants go on a waitlist. Medicare's home health benefit looks like a reasonable fit given the reported care needs and Medicare coverage, while the Medicare skilled nursing facility benefit is less likely to apply unless there's been a recent hospital stay. Respite care could help if there's an unpaid family caregiver, but that hasn't been confirmed. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency can determine that, and next steps involve reaching out to confirm the missing details.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time, intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with bathing, walking, and grooming — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
There's no cost for covered home health services, though 20% coinsurance applies to durable medical equipment.
To qualify, a doctor must certify the person is homebound and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary doctor about a referral for a home health evaluation.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, skilled nursing, and help transitioning to a new home.

To use COPES, a person generally needs to be age 65 or older (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed by a CARE evaluation as needing nursing-facility level of care, and have income at or below $2,982/month (2026 figure).
Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, though special rules can protect some resources for a spouse still living at home.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a request for a CARE assessment through the regional Home and Community Services intake line.

Two look like strong fits given the facts you provided (Medicare home health and Tailored Supports for Older Adults), though TSOA currently has a statewide enrollment pause and new applicants go on a waitlist. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, a few Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look, and a couple more need one or two more details before we can say much about them. You're 69, live in your own home in San Juan County, need help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, are covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid), and have income under $1,000/month with countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Two programs — Medicaid Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit — appear to align well with what you've told us so far. Three others (Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, Medicaid Alternative Care, and respite care programs) could be relevant, but they depend on information we don't have yet, such as whether you've had a recent hospital stay or whether someone provides you with regular unpaid care. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency can determine that. Please treat this as a starting point for questions to bring to a caseworker, not a guarantee of any benefit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 69 (meeting the 18+ age rule), live in your own home in San Juan County, and need help with dressing, transferring, and mobility — needs across three ADL areas, which lines up with CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs. You're also already covered by Apple Health (Medicaid), which is the financial pathway CFC uses.

whatItCovers.text: CFC can cover personal care help with ADLs such as dressing, bathing, transferring, bed mobility, and locomotion/walking, plus related IADL help like meal prep or housework when ADL help is also needed.

It can also include relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, a personal emergency response system, assistive technology, skills training, community transition help, and training on how to select and manage personal care providers.
Services can be delivered in your own home, and CFC can be combined with other waiver services (CFC+COPES) for people who need more support; it doesn't replace Medicare or other insurance.

Eligibility generally requires a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care and continuing to have an unmet need in at least three ADLs at reassessment, along with community Medicaid (CN/ABP) financial eligibility — people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage typically only need the functional assessment.

nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request or confirm a CARE assessment.
Ask whether your current Apple Health coverage is the Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan category CFC uses.
Apply or check status through Washington Connection (washingtonconnection.org) if you haven't already started an LTSS application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and live at home, and you've described needing help with dressing, transferring, and mobility — needs that can overlap with the kind of skilled or personal care this benefit is built around, especially if a doctor determines you need part-time skilled care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming — but aide help is only covered while you're also getting skilled nursing or therapy.
It also covers durable medical equipment and supplies for home use, and there's no cap on the number of visits while you continue to qualify.
Covered home health services cost $0; only Medicare-covered durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

Eligibility requires a homebound status finding, a face-to-face assessment by a provider, and a provider's order for care delivered by a Medicare-certified agency — this benefit does not cover 24-hour care or personal/custodial care alone.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or a nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and need for skilled care.
If a provider orders home health services, ask for a list of Medicare-certified agencies serving San Juan County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but it's not yet known whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor's determination that you need daily skilled care — this is a required trigger for the benefit that we don't have information about yet.

whatItCovers.text: When the hospital-stay requirement is met, this benefit can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, supplies, and certain ambulance transportation in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with no daily cost for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.
This is short-term rehabilitation coverage after a hospital stay, not a long-term custodial care benefit, and generally requires admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently.
Whether a doctor has determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care following that stay.
nextSteps: If you have a recent or upcoming hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health or Medicaid personal care options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 69 (over the program's age-55 minimum), already enrolled in Apple Health, live in your own home, and have described needs consistent with a nursing-facility level of care — but this program is designed to support an unpaid family caregiver, and we don't yet know whether you have one, which is a required piece of information. It's also worth knowing that new enrollment in this program has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with only a waitlist currently available.

whatItCovers.text: When available, MAC provides stepped support to an unpaid caregiver: a one-time $250 benefit, then up to $500/year, and eventually a monthly benefit (around $928/month, up to $5,568 per six-month period as of 7/1/2025–6/30/2026) after a TCARE/GetCare screening.

Covered categories include caregiver assistance services (respite, housekeeping, adult day care, home-delivered meals), training and education, specialized equipment and supplies, and health maintenance/therapy supports like counseling; it does not include personal assistance services for the care receiver.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services but no reopening date has been announced.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (age 18 or older) who regularly helps you and would be the one receiving this support.
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist and whether it makes sense to join it.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described ongoing care needs (help with dressing, transferring, and mobility), which is relevant to these programs, but they are built around supporting an unpaid caregiver, and it isn't yet known whether someone provides you with regular unpaid care — that's a required piece of missing information.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options), for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential setting) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with amounts varying by county/Area Agency on Aging.
A separate DDA respite pathway exists only if the elder has a developmental disability that generally originated before age 18.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they help.
nextSteps: If someone provides you with regular unpaid care, have them contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite vouchers.

followUpQuestions: Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights in a row recently, and did a doctor say you need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Is there a family member, friend, or other unpaid person who regularly helps you with care — and if so, are they 18 or older?
Is your Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage the 'Categorically Needy' or 'Alternative Benefit Plan' community coverage, or a different category?
Has a CARE assessment (functional/level-of-care assessment) been done through DSHS Home and Community Services yet?
Do you currently receive any paid personal care, home health, or respite services, or are you on any waitlist for these?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 67, live in your own home in San Juan County, need help with dressing, are married, are enrolled in Medicare, and have a monthly income between $1,000–$2,999 and countable assets between $2,000–$85,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs may be worth a closer look. However, many of the specific details these programs use to sort applicants (such as exactly how many daily activities you need help with, your precise income and asset numbers, whether you're already enrolled in Apple Health/Medicaid, any recent hospital stays, and whether a family member provides regular unpaid care) are not yet known. Because so many of these program-specific facts are still missing, this screening cannot say which programs you would actually qualify for — only an agency can make that determination. The best next step is to answer the follow-up questions below and then contact the agencies listed so a real assessment can happen.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, you live at home rather than in a facility, and you've told us you need help with dressing — Medicare's home health benefit covers part-time skilled care and aide help (including dressing assistance) for people in situations like this, at no cost for covered services.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (including bathing and dressing) when also receiving skilled care — at $0 cost to you for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person generally must need part-time or intermittent skilled services, be considered 'homebound,' have a face-to-face assessment by a health care provider, and have care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment to see if home health services and homebound status would apply
If appropriate, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving San Juan County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 (meeting the 65+ age rule), live in your own home in San Juan County, need help with dressing, are married, and are covered by Medicare — COPES is designed for people 65 and older who need help with daily activities and want to stay in the community rather than move to a nursing facility.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, operated by DSHS Home and Community Services, serving people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living facilities.

It adds services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, specialized equipment, transportation, and help transitioning into community living.

To be considered, an assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and financial rules look at whether income is at or below about $2,982/month and countable resources are at or below $2,000 for a single person (with higher protections for a spouse still living at home).

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities you need help with and whether an assessment would find a 'nursing facility level of care' need
Your exact monthly income compared to the $2,982 threshold
Your exact countable assets compared to the $2,000 (single) / $3,000 (couple) threshold
Details about your marital situation, since a spouse's income and assets can affect the numbers
nextSteps: Contact the Region 2 HCS intake line that serves San Juan County (800-780-7094) to ask about a CARE assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Ask about how a spouse's income and assets are treated under the spousal impoverishment rules

programs.whyThisMayApply: Community First Choice covers help with activities like dressing, which you've told us you need help with. Because it's a Medicaid state-plan benefit, it generally requires being enrolled in community Medicaid and needing help with several daily activities, and it's not yet known whether either of those applies to you.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is Washington's Medicaid state-plan personal care option for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, covering help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related household (IADL) tasks, relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional Medicaid rules).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health Medicaid, separate from Medicare
How many daily activities you need help with beyond dressing
nextSteps: Check with Health Care Authority or the local HCS office about your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document your care needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health LTC is the financial doorway to programs like COPES, and it uses the same age (65+), functional need, and income/asset rules that may apply to you given your age, home in San Juan County, dressing-related care need, marital status, and reported income and asset ranges.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, acting as the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and community long-term care, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Financial eligibility generally requires income at or below about $2,982/month (with a higher-income pathway available) and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with additional protections for a spouse still living at home.

For people in San Juan County, the functional (CARE) assessment is requested through the Region 2 HCS intake line, and the financial application can be submitted online, by mail, fax, phone, or in person.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an assessment would find a nursing-facility level of care need
Your exact monthly income and countable assets compared to program limits
Details about your marital situation for spousal financial rules
nextSteps: Call the Region 2 HCS intake line (800-780-7094, serving San Juan County) to request a CARE assessment
Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, mail, or fax

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, but this benefit only applies after a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care — it's not yet known whether you've had a qualifying hospital stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain transportation, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient responsibility after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare SNF coverage would apply
Keep documentation of any hospital stay length and discharge orders

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 (meeting the 55+ age rule), and your reported income and asset ranges appear to fall within TSOA's higher limits, and you're not reported as being on classic Medicaid — but note the program's current statewide enrollment pause is an important factor, and whether an assessment would find a qualifying care level is unknown.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need a nursing-facility level of care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid (income up to about $3,976/month; resources up to about $84,354 single or $156,883 married), no cost-sharing, and no estate recovery of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Program status: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and presumptive-eligibility screenings suspended; there is no announced reopening date.

Applications can be submitted online, by paper form, or through a local Area Agency on Aging, but during the pause, submitting an application mainly establishes a place on the waitlist rather than immediate services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an assessment would find a nursing-facility level of care need
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current TSOA waitlist and whether it makes sense to apply now
Ask about non-Medicaid respite options in the meantime, since TSOA services are paused for new applicants

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've told us you need ongoing help with dressing, and you're married, which raises the possibility that your spouse provides regular unpaid care — but it hasn't been confirmed whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which these programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief ('respite') for unpaid family caregivers through channels such as Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which can include in-home respite, adult day services, caregiver training, support groups, and counseling.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or waclc.org to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program screening
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours a week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers

followUpQuestions: Besides dressing, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, eating, toileting, transferring (getting in/out of bed or a chair), walking, or taking medications?
Is there any memory loss, confusion, or need for supervision because of disorientation or wandering?
What is your exact monthly household income (you told us it's somewhere between $1,000 and $2,999)?
What is your exact countable asset total (you told us it's somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000)?
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), in addition to Medicare?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and if so, did a doctor say you need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Does your spouse or another family member provide regular unpaid care or supervision for you, and if so, about how many hours a week?
What are your spouse's income and assets, since some programs have special rules for married couples?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that might be worth exploring for a 79-year-old widowed individual in Walla Walla County who needs help with bathing, eating, and transferring after a stroke, has Medicare coverage, income under $1,000/month, and assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Two programs (Medicare home health and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) look likely relevant based on the details given. Three others (Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) could also be worth investigating, but the screening is missing a few key facts — whether Medicaid is already in place, whether there was a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening more useful. Nothing here is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is already in place, and the described needs — help with transferring, eating, and bathing after a stroke — suggest a possible skilled or personal care need while living at home, which lines up with what this benefit is designed to address.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology, along with medical social services.

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming can be included, but only while skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy is also being received — it isn't available as personal care alone.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a doctor or qualifying provider must certify homebound status and order the care, and services must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or health care provider about scheduling a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and the need for skilled care.
If the assessment supports it, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Walla Walla County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 79 years old with income under $1,000/month and assets between $2,000 and $85,000, the reported financial situation falls within this program's higher income and resource limits, and the described needs with bathing, eating, and transferring align with a nursing-facility level of care standard this program uses. The profile doesn't indicate current Medicaid enrollment, which matches this program's target group.

whatItCovers.text: This program supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, using income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and it also serves people without an unpaid caregiver.
Financial limits (as of 1/1/2026) allow income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single person — both above regular Medicaid limits.

Functional eligibility is based on meeting a nursing-facility level of care standard, and there is no client cost-sharing or estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Important: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants currently go on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services, with no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org, by paper form (HCA 18-008), or through a local Home & Community Services office, understanding that this currently places the application on a statewide waitlist.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the waitlist process and any interim options.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes needing help with transferring, eating, and bathing — three ADLs — which matches the substantial ADL need this program looks for. However, it's not yet known whether Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage is in place or obtainable, which is a required piece for this specific program.

whatItCovers.text: This program can cover personal care help with ADLs like bathing, eating, dressing, transferring, and toileting, along with related IADL help (like meal prep or housework) when ADL help is also needed, plus health-related tasks.

It can also include relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, community transition help, and training on managing personal care providers.
To use this program, a person generally needs to already be eligible for community Medicaid (CN or ABP coverage) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.
Continuing to receive these services at reassessment requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, alongside a finding of nursing-facility level of care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the individual is currently enrolled in, or could enroll in, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN or ABP coverage).
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask whether a Medicaid (Apple Health) financial application is needed, or whether existing coverage already qualifies for the assessment-only path.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage appears to be in place, but this program only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and it isn't yet known whether such a stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: When it applies, this coverage can include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transport.

It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with $0/day for days 1–20, coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and whether a doctor has indicated a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it meets the 3-day inpatient (not observation) requirement.
Ask the hospital or doctor whether Medicare would cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility before any admission.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living in a family member's home while needing ongoing help with several ADLs suggests there may be an unpaid caregiver involved, which these respite programs are designed to support — but it isn't yet confirmed whether such a caregiver is providing regular care.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver to live in Washington, and an inability to otherwise afford respite; they provide up to $1,000 per household per year.

FCSP serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ needing care, with services and amounts tailored by local Area Agencies on Aging through a caregiver assessment; there's no statewide dollar cap, and support can include short-term in-home or facility-based care plus counseling and training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week that care involves.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP screening and available respite options in Walla Walla County.
If a caregiver is providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington voucher eligibility through wapave.org or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), including any Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) coverage?
Has there been a recent hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row as an inpatient (not just under observation) connected to the stroke or other health needs?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a CARE assessment or similar functional assessment been done by DSHS Home and Community Services, and if so, what was the result regarding level of care?

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is over 55, is enrolled in Apple Health, lives at home rather than in a licensed residential facility, and has ADL needs consistent with nursing-facility level of care — all factors this program's design considers. However, it's not known whether an unpaid family caregiver (age 18+) is involved, which is a core requirement, and the program has been paused for new enrollment since December 1, 2025.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports unpaid family caregivers of people 55+ who are on Apple Health and meet nursing-facility level of care but have chosen not to use other Medicaid long-term care programs.

Benefits are structured in steps: a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500/year, then a higher benefit (an average of about $928/month, up to $5,568 over six months as of the 2025–26 rate period) for things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, home-delivered meals, training, equipment, and counseling.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services, but no reopening date has been announced.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver, age 18 or older, currently providing care for this person.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status and options counseling.
If a caregiver is identified, ask about how the pause affects timing for this specific situation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The dementia diagnosis and needs with transferring, eating, and toileting suggest an ongoing need for care or supervision, which is one factor these programs consider. However, it isn't yet known whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is central to eligibility for these respite options.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care, subject to available funding.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment; amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging and there's no statewide dollar cap.
DDA waiver respite only applies if the person has a developmental disability that generally originated before age 18, which does not appear to match this situation based on the information given.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program available through the local Area Agency on Aging.
If applicable, a TCARE caregiver screening can help determine what level of respite support might be available locally.

followUpQuestions: Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (as an admitted inpatient, not just under observation) recently, especially if a stay in a skilled nursing facility is being considered?
Is there a family member or friend who provides regular unpaid care or supervision? If so, about how many hours per week do they help?
Has a doctor indicated that leaving home is difficult or not recommended (sometimes called 'homebound' status), which is relevant to some Medicare home care benefits?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 81, live in Adams County, WA, in your own home, are married, have Medicare, monthly income under $1,000, countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and need help with dressing and transferring after a stroke — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look with an official caseworker. None of this is an eligibility decision; only the administering agency (DSHS/HCA or Medicare) can confirm that. A few key pieces of information are still missing (your exact countable asset amount, whether you're already enrolled in Apple Health/Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether a family member acts as an unpaid caregiver), so some of these results are more tentative than others. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, and your need for help with dressing and transferring after a stroke suggests a personal or skilled care need, while living at home rather than in a facility — all of which line up with this benefit's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, dressing, walking) when also receiving skilled care — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person generally needs a documented skilled care need, homebound status confirmed by a provider, and care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Durable medical equipment for home use is also covered, though a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible may apply.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and skilled care needs.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County.
Have your doctor or a home health agency help set up a care plan (CMS-485).

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55 (age 81), your monthly income under $1,000 is well below the program's $3,976 limit, and your countable assets (reported between $2,000 and $85,000) appear to fall within the $84,354 single-person resource limit. Your reported need for help with dressing and transferring after a stroke also matches the kind of functional need this program looks for. Importantly, this program's enrollment has been paused statewide, which affects how soon services could actually start even if the basic criteria seem to align.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed to support people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers if there is one; it can also serve people who have no unpaid caregiver.

As of the most recent information, enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; people already enrolled or approved before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist process.
Submit the TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish your place on the waitlist if you want to pursue this option.
Ask the AAA about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 81 years old and need help with dressing and transferring after a stroke, which lines up with the kind of daily-living needs this waiver's assessment looks at. Your monthly income under $1,000 is well within the program's income guideline. Because your countable assets fall somewhere in a wide range ($2,000–$85,000) and you're married, it isn't yet clear whether you'd be within the resource limits used for this program.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services on top of basic personal care, such as adult day care and adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.
It's Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, meant for people who could otherwise need nursing-facility-level care but want to stay in the community.
Eligibility involves an assessment of needs like help with dressing, transferring, bathing, and similar daily activities, along with age and income tests.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable asset total, so it can be compared to the Medicaid resource limits
Details about your marital status and how assets are divided between you and your spouse, since married couples have different resource rules
nextSteps: Contact your local DSHS Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment.
Gather documentation of your and your spouse's assets and income to clarify where you stand relative to program limits.
Ask HCS about spousal impoverishment protections, which may affect how your assets are counted.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with dressing and transferring after a stroke reflects the kind of personal-care needs this program is built around, and you're an adult over 18 in Washington. However, it isn't yet clear whether you're currently enrolled in community Medicaid coverage, and continuing eligibility generally looks for help needed in three or more daily activities, so this is uncertain based on what's known so far.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help such as dressing, bathing, transferring, and other daily living tasks, plus supports like personal emergency response systems, relief care, and training for choosing and managing caregivers.
Services can be provided in your own home or other community settings, and CFC can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage
The full list of daily activities you need help with, since staying on this program generally requires needing help with three or more
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health or HCS about your current Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake to document your care needs in detail.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 81, needing help with dressing and transferring after a stroke, and with income under $1,000/month, several pieces of this program's criteria seem to line up. As with the related COPES waiver, whether your specific countable assets and marital financial details fit within the program's limits is still unclear.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid pathway for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to nursing facility care and home-based waiver programs like COPES.
Applying involves both a financial application and a functional (CARE) assessment, with financial and functional eligibility determined together.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable asset total, to compare against Medicaid resource limits
Details about your marital status and spousal asset arrangements
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application online, by mail, fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call HCS central intake for your region to schedule a CARE assessment.
Gather asset and income documentation ahead of the required financial interview.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, it isn't known whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more consecutive days, which is a required trigger for this coverage — without that, this benefit likely wouldn't apply right now.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a short-term stay in a skilled nursing facility — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and whether that stay was formally classified as 'inpatient' rather than 'observation'
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies you for SNF coverage.
If there's no recent qualifying stay, ask your doctor about other options like Medicare home health or Medicaid-funded care.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your stroke-related need for help with dressing and transferring suggests ongoing care needs, and being married raises the possibility that your spouse could be an unpaid caregiver — a key factor for these programs. However, it isn't yet known whether your spouse (or anyone else) provides unpaid care and how many hours per week, so it's unclear which respite option, if any, would fit.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies; the Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus support groups, training, and consultation, with amounts varying by county Area Agency on Aging.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, while the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ needing care, with eligibility details varying by community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver, such as a spouse, providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide it
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program screening.
If your spouse provides regular unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers as well.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact total of countable assets? (You mentioned a range between $2,000 and $85,000, but the specific number matters for Medicaid limits.)
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage, or only Medicare?
Besides dressing and transferring, do you need help with any other daily activities, like bathing, eating, or toileting?
Do you have an unpaid caregiver, such as your spouse, who regularly helps you, and about how many hours a week do they provide care?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay (not just observation) of 3 or more consecutive days in recent weeks or months?
Since you're married, is your spouse also applying for benefits, or are you the sole applicant? This affects how income and assets are counted.
