report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared, this 70-year-old in Skamania County has a stroke diagnosis and needs help with several daily activities (mobility, dressing, toileting, transferring), is married, has monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000, and has Medicare coverage. These facts line up closely with several Washington long-term-care Medicaid programs and the Medicare home health benefit, so those are worth exploring with the administering agencies. A few other programs (Community First Choice, the Medicare skilled nursing facility benefit, and respite care programs) could also be relevant, but the screening is missing some specific details — like current Medicaid enrollment status, any recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved — so those need follow-up before they can be prioritized. No program listed here has been confirmed as something this family qualifies for; only the administering agency can make that determination after a full application and assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 70 with help needed for mobility, dressing, toileting, and transferring, and with monthly income under $1,000 (within the program's $2,982 Special Income Level) and countable assets under $2,000 (within the $2,000 resource limit), the facts shared line up with COPES' age, functional, income, and resource criteria.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and specialized medical equipment and supplies.
Services can be provided in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and cannot duplicate or replace Medicare or other insurance benefits.
COPES is Washington's largest Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and typically works together with Community First Choice personal care ("CFC+COPES").

nextSteps: Submit the financial application (form HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment to document the level of care needed.
Gather documentation such as the signed Acknowledgment of Services (DSHS 14-225) and records of any asset transfers or gifts in the past 5 years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 70, the described ADL needs, monthly income under $1,000 (within the $2,982 Special Income Level), and assets under $2,000 (within the resource limit) match the general eligibility framework for Apple Health long-term care, which also serves as the financial gateway to waivers like COPES.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial pathway into home-and-community waivers such as COPES, PACE, and institutional hospice.

Eligibility involves a nursing-facility level of care assessment plus income and resource limits — a Special Income Level of $2,982/month and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026).

nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit paper form HCA 18-005 by mail, fax, or phone.
Complete the required financial interview with an HCS financial specialist.
Call the Region 3 HCS intake line (800-786-3799), which covers southwest Washington including Skamania County, to schedule the in-person CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is confirmed, the person lives at home (in a family member's home, not a nursing facility), and the described needs — help with mobility, dressing, toileting, and transferring following a stroke — suggest a possible skilled or personal care need that this benefit is designed to address.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (only when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy) at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To use this benefit, a doctor must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, order the services, and have them delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor to complete a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and need for skilled care.
Obtain a written care order and choose a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Skamania County.
Review the agency's written explanation of what Medicare will and won't cover before services start.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 70 (55+), income under $1,000/month (within the $3,976 TSOA limit), and assets under $2,000 (within TSOA's higher resource limits) line up with TSOA's financial and age criteria, and the described ADL needs suggest a possible nursing-facility level of care. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, which affects how soon services could start.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not enrolled in classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using income and asset limits well above regular Medicaid.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving immediate services, though people already enrolled keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply via washingtonconnection.org to be placed on the current statewide waitlist.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting, since these don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.
Confirm current Medicaid enrollment status, since TSOA is unavailable to those already enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described help needed with mobility, dressing, toileting, and transferring suggests a substantial ADL need, which matches CFC's functional criteria. whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs — bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, mobility, and eating — plus related IADL help like meal prep and housework, along with relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
CFC services can be delivered at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living, and can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is currently enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage.
nextSteps: Confirm current enrollment status in Washington Apple Health Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage.
If already enrolled in qualifying Apple Health coverage, request a CARE assessment through HCS.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed, but it isn't yet known whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with a doctor-documented need for daily skilled care, which this benefit requires.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a skilled nursing facility stay — including room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

These programs are intended for unpaid caregivers providing regular care and generally do not stack with other publicly funded respite programs.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 91-year-old family member in Stevens County who needs help with dressing, bathing, mobility, and toileting, has heart disease, is on Medicare, and has income under $1,000 a month with assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — five programs from Washington's aging and Medicare systems look worth a closer look. A few key facts are still missing (current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment, any recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing regular care), so some of these need more information before you'll know whether they're worth pursuing. None of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency (DSHS, HCS, or Medicare) can decide that. Think of this as a starting map for conversations with those agencies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home (not in a nursing facility), and has care needs (help with bathing, dressing, mobility, and toileting) that suggest a possible skilled or personal care need — all three factors the rules engine checked came back as a match.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare-certified home health agencies can provide part-time skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language therapy at no cost for the covered services.

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — it is not a stand-alone personal care benefit.

To qualify, a doctor or nurse practitioner must certify the person is homebound (has difficulty leaving home without help, and leaving takes considerable effort) and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care.
Durable medical equipment for home use is also covered, though it carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor whether a face-to-face homebound and skilled-care assessment would be appropriate.
If a skilled need is identified, ask the doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 91 (well above the 55+ age requirement), has monthly income under $1,000 (within the $3,976/month limit), and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 (within the $84,354 single-person resource limit), and needs help with several ADLs consistent with a nursing-facility level of care need. The rules engine also found they are not currently enrolled in classic Medicaid, which fits this program's design for people above regular Medicaid limits.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA does not require Medicaid enrollment and allows income and resources above regular Medicaid limits, up to $3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person (current as of 1/1/2026).
Eligible participants get a 12-month certification with no cost-sharing and no estate recovery of the kind tied to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Important: statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025. New applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments happen only when called from that waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or the paper HCA 18-008 form to get placed on the statewide waitlist, since applying now only secures a place in line.
While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 91 and needs help with four daily activities — dressing, bathing, mobility, and toileting — which meets the 3+ ADL need this program looks for at reassessment [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility]. However, whether they are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is not yet known, and that financial/coverage piece is a required eligibility building block for this program [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, toileting, mobility, and eating, plus related IADL help such as meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief (respite) care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family members who manage caregivers.
Services can be provided in a family member's home, an adult family home, or assisted living, among other settings.

To qualify functionally, a person generally needs a nursing-facility level of care finding and, to continue services, an unmet or partly met need in at least three ADLs — a need your family member appears to meet based on the ADLs described.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in, or could enroll in, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP).
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a financial application and a CARE assessment.
Ask HCS specifically how CFC eligibility connects to your family member's current Medicaid status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, it's not yet known whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a qualifying hospital stay is a required condition, and this piece of information is currently missing.

whatItCovers.text: Following a qualifying 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Days 1–20 have no daily charge; days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100 the patient pays all costs.
Time spent under hospital 'observation status' does not count toward the required 3-day stay, and admission to the SNF generally must happen within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has said ongoing daily skilled care is needed.
nextSteps: If a hospitalization occurs or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether the stay qualifies for SNF coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options), for caregivers providing 40+ hours a week who could not otherwise afford respite.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, at an adult day center, or in residential care) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with services and amounts tailored locally through a caregiver specialist's assessment.

These programs generally do not stack with each other — for example, Lifespan vouchers exclude people already receiving respite through another publicly funded program, with a limited waitlist exception.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 78, you meet the age requirement for this program, and your reported needs with eating, mobility, and transferring line up with the kind of assistance-level needs used to decide whether someone needs nursing-facility-level care. Your reported monthly income (under $1,000) is below the program's income limit, and your countable assets (under $2,000) fall within the resource limit for a single person.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, transportation, environmental modifications, and help transitioning to a home setting.
Services can be provided in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and are meant to add to — not replace — Medicare or other insurance benefits.

To be considered, a person generally must be age 65+ (or younger with an SSI-level disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care based on unmet needs in ADLs like eating, transferring, or mobility, and meet income and resource limits (income up to $2,982/month and resources up to $2,000 for a single person, as of 1/1/2026).

nextSteps: Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at a Home and Community Services (HCS) office.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment, which determines functional (care-need) eligibility.
Ask about the DSHS 14-225 Acknowledgment of Services form and any documentation needed about assets or transfers in the last 5 years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your age (78), reported income under $1,000/month, countable assets under $2,000, and care needs involving eating, mobility, and transferring align with the general financial and functional gateway criteria this program uses for long-term-care Medicaid coverage.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS home-and-community-based waivers such as COPES, as well as nursing facility care and other community LTSS options.

Eligibility generally requires meeting nursing-facility-level-of-care functional criteria and either being within the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or qualifying through the Medically Needy pathway, along with resource limits.

nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org, by paper form HCA 18-005, by phone, or in person at an HCS office.
Call HCS central intake for your region (Franklin County is in Region 1 — Eastern WA — reachable at 509-568-3767 or 866-323-9409) to request the CARE functional assessment.
Be prepared for a financial interview with an HCS financial specialist as part of the process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You reported being covered by Medicare, and your described needs — help with eating, mobility, and transferring — suggest a need for skilled or personal care support at home, which this benefit is designed to help with.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help (bathing, feeding, walking assistance) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at no cost to you for covered services.
To qualify, a provider must certify a need for part-time/intermittent skilled care and confirm 'homebound' status — meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended without help.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled home care.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 78, with reported care needs matching a nursing-facility level of care, income under $1,000/month, and assets under $2,000, your numbers fall within TSOA's more generous financial limits. However, this program is currently paused statewide, which affects how soon services could start even if the screening looks favorable.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not enrolled in classic Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid limits, and no client cost-sharing.

As of the most recent information available, enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: If interested, submit an application (online at washingtonconnection.org or paper form HCA 18-008) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about other non-Medicaid support options available while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported needs with eating, mobility, and transferring suggest a substantial level of ADL need, which is one of the factors this program looks at. However, whether this applies also depends on being enrolled in (or eligible for) regular community Medicaid, which is not yet known from your profile.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related IADL help like meal preparation and shopping, along with relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

It's a Medicaid state plan benefit (not capped by waiver slots) available to people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or would be eligible for) regular community Medicaid coverage (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan).
nextSteps: Check your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status, or ask HCS whether you'd qualify for community Medicaid.
If already on qualifying Apple Health coverage, ask HCS about scheduling just the CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You reported having Medicare coverage, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program specifically requires a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay followed by a need for daily skilled care, and that information wasn't provided.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical/occupational/speech therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

This coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to a Medicare-certified SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, followed by an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify as a 'qualifying stay' for SNF coverage.
If there was no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported care needs (help with eating, mobility, and transferring after a stroke) suggest ongoing supervision needs, which is one factor these programs consider. However, whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — a key requirement for these programs — was not part of your profile.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care to someone in Washington who can't otherwise afford respite.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (from a few hours to a few days) plus caregiver training, counseling, and support groups, with amounts and rules varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family member or friend regularly providing care or supervision for you, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver, contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment.
Ask about current Lifespan Respite voucher availability, since awards are limited by funding.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (community Medicaid), either Categorically Needy or an Alternative Benefit Plan?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past, followed by an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is there a family member, friend, or other unpaid person who regularly provides care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor certified that leaving home is difficult or not recommended (sometimes called 'homebound status')?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven possible programs for a 78-year-old in Jefferson County, WA who lives at home, is married, has Medicare, and needs help with toileting, with income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000. A few things line up well with certain programs — age, income, and asset levels fit within Medicaid long-term-care and TSOA limits, and Medicare enrollment plus a home-based care need fit the Medicare home health benefit closely. However, several important pieces of information are still missing: how many daily activities (not just toileting) need help, whether any skilled nursing supervision is needed, whether the person is currently enrolled in Apple Health/Medicaid, whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) provides regular care, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Because these unknowns affect several programs, this screening should be treated as a starting point — a follow-up conversation with a Home and Community Services (HCS) or Area Agency on Aging professional is needed to get real answers, not a determination of eligibility for any program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare and needs help with a daily activity (toileting) while living at home in Jefferson County — matching the conditions the screening already confirmed: Medicare enrollment, an indicated care need, and living at home rather than in a facility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help such as bathing, walking, and feeding — but aide help is only covered while the person is also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.

To qualify, a doctor must certify the person is 'homebound' (has real difficulty leaving home without help, or leaving isn't advised) and needs skilled, part-time or intermittent care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible. Custodial or personal care alone, without an accompanying skilled-care need, is not covered.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person meets Medicare's definition of 'homebound'
Whether there's a skilled nursing or therapy need alongside the toileting help, since personal care alone doesn't qualify for this benefit
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 78, lives in Jefferson County, WA, and has monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000 — figures that appear to fit within COPES' age, income, and resource limits. The noted need for help with toileting is one of the activities of daily living used to assess whether someone needs nursing-facility level of care, but it isn't yet known whether there are enough ADL needs, or a nursing need, to meet that functional requirement.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, skilled nursing, and specialized medical equipment, for people in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must find the person needs nursing-facility level of care — based on daily nursing supervision needs, needing help with two or three (or more) specific ADLs at certain levels, or cognitive impairment combined with an ADL need.
Financially, COPES uses a Special Income Level of $2,982/month and a resource limit of $2,000 (single) / $3,000 (couple), with additional protections for a spouse who continues living at home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether daily help is needed with activities beyond toileting, such as bathing, eating, transferring, mobility, or medication management
Whether a nurse or skilled professional's supervision is needed on a daily basis
The results of a CARE functional assessment by DSHS
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Discuss the toileting need and any other daily-living needs with an HCS social worker to help clarify functional eligibility

programs.whyThisMayApply: CFC provides Medicaid-funded personal care, including help with toileting, for people living in the community who meet an institutional level of care. This family's noted toileting need matches one of CFC's covered activities, but the screening does not know whether this person is currently enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid coverage, or whether help is needed with three or more ADLs, which CFC requires to continue services after the first review.

whatItCovers.text: CFC provides personal care help with activities like toileting, bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus help with related household tasks, relief care, and personal emergency response systems, for people living at home or in certain care settings.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must find nursing-facility level of care, and the person generally must already be eligible for Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, or SSI-related Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or would qualify for) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid community coverage
How many activities of daily living need ongoing help, beyond toileting
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status, or apply through washingtonconnection.org
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the Medicaid financial gateway to long-term care services, including COPES and CFC. The person's age, low income, and low assets appear to fit within its income and resource standards, but whether the toileting need alone (or additional unreported needs) meets the required nursing-facility level of care is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care covers nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (like COPES), PACE, and other community long-term services, once a CARE assessment establishes nursing-facility level of care.

Income limits include a Special Income Level of $2,982/month for the standard pathway, with a Medically Needy pathway for people above that; the resource limit is $2,000 single / $3,000 couple, with protections for a spouse who stays in the community.
Applying requires a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus an in-person CARE assessment; the standard processing time is 45 days (60 days if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: Whether a CARE assessment would find nursing-facility level of care, based on ADL needs or nursing supervision needs
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or in person
Call regional HCS central intake to schedule the required financial interview and CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed by the profile, but the screening does not know of a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, which is required to trigger this short-term skilled nursing facility benefit. Nothing in the profile currently points to a recent hospitalization.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

It covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, and facility medications and supplies; days 1–20 have no daily charge, days 21–100 carry a $217/day coinsurance (2026), and it is not meant for long-term custodial care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
Whether a doctor has determined daily skilled care is needed following such a stay
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a SNF stay before admission
If there is no recent hospital stay, this benefit likely does not apply to the current at-home care situation

programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 78 fits TSOA's 55-and-older requirement, and the reported income and assets are well within TSOA's higher limits; the screening also found this person not currently enrolled in Medicaid, matching TSOA's non-Medicaid requirement. However, functional need (nursing-facility level of care) is unknown, and importantly, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility level of care, along with their unpaid caregivers, without requiring Medicaid enrollment, using income and resource limits that are higher than regular Medicaid's.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date, while people already enrolled or approved before the pause keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a functional assessment would find nursing-facility level of care
Whether joining the current waitlist makes sense given the family's timeline, since new intake is paused
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections / the local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the TSOA waitlist and current status
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while the TSOA waitlist is in effect

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program supports unpaid caregivers of adults who need ongoing care or supervision. The profile notes the person is married and needs help with toileting, but it does not say whether a spouse or anyone else is providing regular unpaid caregiving, which is required to qualify for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40 or more hours per week of care, if the family can't otherwise afford respite and isn't already receiving respite through another program.

The Family Caregiver Support Program offers free or low-cost planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling, with services tailored through a TCARE assessment; amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging.
DDA respite only applies when the elder has a developmental disability that generally began before age 18, so it likely would not apply here.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver, such as a spouse, is providing regular care and about how many hours per week
Whether the household could not otherwise afford respite care on its own
Whether respite is already being received through another program
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging / Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility and a TCARE assessment
If a spouse or family member provides 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, ask about the Lifespan Respite Washington voucher

followUpQuestions: Besides toileting, does the person need regular help with other daily activities like bathing, eating, dressing, transferring, walking, or managing medications?
Does a nurse or skilled professional currently need to supervise or provide daily care, or is there a cognitive impairment involved (like memory problems or confusion)?
Is the person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application been submitted?
Does the person have difficulty leaving home without help, or is leaving home not recommended by a doctor (this is called being 'homebound')?
Does the spouse, or anyone else, regularly provide unpaid care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past?
Has a CARE functional assessment ever been completed by DSHS/HCS for this person?

It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared about your 83-year-old family member in Benton County — who has Parkinson's, needs help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, is married, and is already enrolled in both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) — several Washington programs look worth a closer look. Two programs (Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit) line up well with the care needs and coverage you've described. Others, like the COPES waiver and Apple Health long-term care, could be strong fits too, but we'd need more specific numbers on monthly income, countable assets, and spousal resources to know whether the financial rules are likely to line up. A few programs (Medicaid Alternative Care and respite care programs) depend on whether there is an unpaid family caregiver — often a spouse — providing regular care, which wasn't stated. The Medicare skilled nursing facility benefit depends on a recent 3-day hospital stay, which also wasn't mentioned. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below will help sharpen this screening.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), and the described needs with mobility, transferring, and dressing suggest a substantial level of ADL assistance — which matches CFC's requirement of unmet or partially met needs in three or more activities of daily living.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.
To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, confirmed through a CARE assessment.
People already active on Apple Health CN/ABP coverage generally don't need to submit a separate financial application for CFC — only the functional (CARE) assessment.

nextSteps: Contact regional HCS central intake to request a CARE assessment, since a financial application may not be needed given existing Apple Health enrollment
Ask how CFC services could be combined with COPES waiver services if additional needs are found

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is enrolled in Medicare and lives at home, and the described needs (mobility, transferring, dressing, and a Parkinson's diagnosis) suggest there may be a need for skilled care services that this benefit is designed to cover.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (when also receiving one of the skilled services above), plus durable medical equipment.
To qualify, a person generally needs to be considered homebound and require part-time or intermittent skilled care, confirmed through a face-to-face assessment and provider order.
Covered home health services generally cost $0, though durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment for homebound status and skilled care needs
If eligible, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies in Benton County

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 83 (meeting the age 65+ standard) and has Parkinson's along with needs in mobility, transferring, and dressing, which may point toward the nursing-facility level of care this waiver requires. However, whether the specific income and asset amounts fit within COPES' financial rules isn't yet clear from the income and asset ranges provided, and marital status (married) means spousal resource rules would need to be applied precisely.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of core Medicaid personal care (CFC), including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and transportation, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.
It is generally available to people age 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability) whose assessed care needs meet Washington's nursing-facility level of care standard.

Financially, it generally requires monthly income at or below about $2,982 (2026 Special Income Level) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with protections that can allow a spouse living at home to keep more income and assets.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
The exact countable asset total, to compare against the program's resource limit
Marital and spousal financial details, since special rules apply when a spouse remains in the home
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment
Complete and submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person
Gather documentation such as any recent asset transfers, gifts, or property sales from the past 5 years

programs.whyThisMayApply: As the age-65+, Medicare-and-Medicaid-enrolled status and described ADL needs suggest a possible nursing-facility level of care, Apple Health long-term care could serve as the financial gateway to additional long-term services. As with COPES, whether the specific income and asset figures fit the program's limits isn't yet clear from the ranges given.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like the COPES waiver, along with nursing facility care and other community LTSS options.

Financially, it generally uses the same income and resource standards as COPES — income at or below about $2,982/month (2026 Special Income Level) and resources at or below $2,000 single/$3,000 couple, with spousal protections available.
Applying involves both a financial application and a functional CARE assessment, which are processed together, generally within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
The exact countable asset total, to compare against the program's resource limit
Spousal financial details, since special protections may apply
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or in person
Call the appropriate regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days needing daily skilled care, and it isn't mentioned whether such a hospital stay has occurred or is expected.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit covers short-term skilled nursing facility care after a qualifying hospital stay, including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Costs include $0 per day for days 1–20, $217 per day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026), and full cost after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was recommended afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up stay in a skilled nursing facility
Ask about the difference between inpatient and observation status, since only inpatient time counts toward the 3-day requirement

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is over 55, is already enrolled in Apple Health, and lives at home, and the described ADL needs may indicate a nursing-facility level of care — all factors this program considers. However, MAC specifically supports an unpaid family caregiver, and whether one (such as a spouse) is providing regular care wasn't stated. It's also worth knowing that this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with only a waitlist currently available.

whatItCovers.text: MAC offers stepped caregiver support benefits — a one-time $250 amount, up to $500/year in Step 2, and roughly $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months) in Step 3 for 2025–2026 — covering things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, home-delivered meals, training, and specialized equipment for the caregiver's benefit.

To qualify, the care receiver must be 55+, already enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health, meet nursing facility level of care, live at home, and have an unpaid caregiver age 18 or older.
Program enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) age 18 or older providing regular care
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections to ask about the current waitlist and options counseling

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described needs — help with mobility, transferring, and dressing due to Parkinson's — suggest ongoing care or supervision may be needed, which is a basic condition for these respite programs. Whether there is an unpaid caregiver, such as a spouse, providing that regular care wasn't stated, and that detail affects which specific respite option might be worth exploring.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year to help pay for respite care such as in-home care or adult day services.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility), along with support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
Lifespan Respite generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care; FCSP is available to unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with details varying by community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and TCARE screening
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher

followUpQuestions: What is the exact monthly income for your family member (and spouse, if combined), rather than just the range?
What is the exact total of countable assets (bank accounts, investments, etc.), rather than just the range?
Does your spouse have separate income or assets that should be considered under Medicaid's spousal protection rules?
Is there an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse or adult child) age 18 or older who regularly provides care or supervision, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has your family member had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, or is one expected soon?
Has a doctor indicated that your family member has trouble leaving home without help, or needs skilled nursing or therapy services (not just personal care)?

report.overallSummary: Based on what was shared — a 66-year-old living in their own home in Kittitas County, WA, married, covered by Medicare, with a monthly income under $1,000, countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and needing help with bathing, mobility, and toileting — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening, not an eligibility decision; the administering agencies (DSHS/HCA for Medicaid programs, Medicare for federal benefits) make the final call. A few key details are still missing — the exact countable asset amount, whether Medicaid is already in place, whether there's an unpaid caregiver, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay — and those answers would sharpen which programs are the best fit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage was reported, along with needs for help with mobility, bathing, and toileting that could point to a skilled care need. Living at home also fits this benefit's setting requirement. Whether it currently applies would depend on a doctor certifying homebound status and a skilled-care need.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare pays in full for part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency.
Home health aide help with bathing, walking, and grooming is covered, but only while skilled nursing or therapy is also being received — it is not a stand-alone personal care benefit.
To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and order the care after a face-to-face assessment.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor whether a face-to-face assessment supports certifying homebound status and a skilled care need.
If certified, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 66, with reported needs for help bathing, moving around, and using the toilet suggesting a nursing-facility level of care, an income under $1,000/month, and countable assets reported between $2,000 and $85,000, this profile fits within TSOA's income and resource ranges for either a single or married applicant, and there's no indication of active Medicaid enrollment.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers stepped benefits — up to $250 one-time, then up to $500/year, then an average of about $928/month (up to $5,568 per six months) — for services like personal care, home-delivered meals, and specialized equipment, without requiring Medicaid enrollment.
Eligibility fits people 55+ who need a nursing-facility level of care, with income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 single or $156,883 married.
As of the most recent update, new applications are placed on a statewide waitlist rather than processed for immediate services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply via washingtonconnection.org, noting that new applications currently go on a statewide waitlist.
Ask the Area Agency on Aging about interim options such as Family Caregiver Support Program respite while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 66 with reported needs for help bathing, moving around, and using the toilet, this profile suggests a nursing-facility level of care, which is one of COPES's core requirements. The reported monthly income under $1,000 is well below the program's income limit, and Medicare coverage often works alongside COPES. Because the exact countable asset amount and how assets are shared between spouses are still unclear, it isn't yet known whether the resource limit is met.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add extra home- and community-based services on top of personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.
Qualifying functionally generally means having an unmet or partially met need in three or more daily activities such as bathing, toileting, and mobility.
Countable resources generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with special rules that can let a spouse keep more of the couple's resources.

informationStillNeeded: The exact dollar amount of countable assets, to compare against the Medicaid resource limit.
How assets are divided between spouses, since Medicaid has special rules for married couples.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to complete a financial application (HCA 18-005) and request a CARE assessment.
Ask HCS how spousal impoverishment rules would apply given the marriage and current asset picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with bathing, mobility, and toileting — three activities of daily living — matches CFC's standard for ongoing eligibility. Whether this program applies also depends on already being enrolled in, or able to enroll in, community Medicaid, which hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help with activities like bathing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care.
To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, which lines up with the bathing, mobility, and toileting needs described.
CFC eligibility depends on already being covered by regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), not institutional financial rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), or eligible to enroll.
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with HCA or DSHS.
If not enrolled, ask HCS about applying for community Medicaid alongside a CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to Medicaid long-term care waivers. The reported age (66), care needs (bathing, mobility, toileting), and income under $1,000/month line up with several of its requirements, though the exact countable asset amount and marital resource details are still needed to know whether the resource limit is met.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like COPES and other home-and-community-based waivers.
Eligibility requires meeting a nursing-facility level of care and having income at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (2026), which fits an income under $1,000/month.
Resource limits are generally $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with extra protections that can let a spouse keep more resources.

informationStillNeeded: The exact dollar amount of countable assets, to compare against the resource limit.
How assets are divided between spouses, since special spousal rules may apply.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office to start the financial and functional review.
Gather documentation of current assets and any transfers made within the last five years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage was reported, but there's no information yet about a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more consecutive days, which is required before this short-term skilled nursing facility benefit can apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a short-term stay in a skilled nursing facility, including skilled nursing, therapy, meals, and medical supplies, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs such as Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program offer short breaks for unpaid caregivers, including in-home respite and adult day services.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 71, live alone in your own home in Yakima County, have heart disease, need help with bathing, are covered by Medicare, and have income and savings in the moderate range — five programs came up as worth learning more about. programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home in Yakima County, and you have a noted care need (help with bathing) along with heart disease, which fits the kind of situation this Medicare benefit is meant to help with.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row as an inpatient, Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility — including nursing care, therapy, meals, and a semi-private room — for up to 100 days per benefit period.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared — you're 84, live alone in your own home in Clallam County, need help with mobility and toileting, have Medicare coverage, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — this screening found five programs worth learning more about. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility for any program, and only the administering agency can make that determination. A few key pieces of information are still missing (such as your Medicaid enrollment status, your full list of daily-activity needs, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stay), so some of these results are more solid than others. We've flagged the follow-up questions that would sharpen this picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and live at home in Clallam County, and you mentioned needing help with mobility. Medicare's home health benefit is designed for people in your situation when the care need is tied to a skilled nursing or therapy need certified by a doctor.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a doctor must certify that you have trouble leaving home without help and that you need skilled care; a home health agency then coordinates the care plan with your doctor.

Home health aide help (like assistance with mobility or bathing) is only covered while you're also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy — it doesn't cover custodial or personal care on its own.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers you homebound
Whether a doctor has identified a specific skilled nursing or therapy need
nextSteps: Ask your primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 84, you meet TSOA's age requirement, and your monthly income under $1,000 and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 both appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits for a single person ($3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources). You also mentioned needing help with mobility and toileting, which relates to the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard TSOA uses. Note that TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, so new applicants go on a waitlist rather than starting services right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with financial limits set well above regular Medicaid.
There is no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure for TSOA, and eligibility is certified for 12 months at a time.

As of the information gathered, new TSOA enrollment is paused statewide since December 1, 2025, and new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than starting services immediately.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to be added to the current TSOA waitlist
Ask about non-Medicaid respite funding options in the meantime, since these don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 84, live in your own home in Clallam County, and mentioned needing help with mobility and toileting. Community First Choice is built for people with personal care needs like these, so it may be worth investigating — but we don't yet know whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid or how many total daily activities you need help with, and both matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care assistance with daily activities such as toileting, bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus extras like personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training.
To continue receiving CFC over time, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three of these daily activities.
CFC requires being eligible for community Medicaid (the regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan rules), not the financial rules used for nursing home stays.
Services can be provided in your own home, among other settings.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're already enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage
The full list of daily activities you need help with, since ongoing CFC eligibility generally requires needing help with at least three
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Check with HCS or a caseworker about your current Medicaid enrollment status if you're unsure

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic building block for this benefit, but skilled nursing facility coverage only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive nights, and we don't yet know whether you've had one recently.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive nights
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay afterward

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned needing ongoing help with mobility and toileting, which is the kind of need respite programs are built to support — but these programs are meant to give relief to an unpaid caregiver, and since you noted you're single, it's unclear whether you currently have someone providing that regular unpaid care.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing at least 40 hours per week of unpaid care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers short-term planned respite care — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility — along with caregiver counseling, training, and support groups, with amounts varying by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver, such as a family member or friend, who regularly provides your care
nextSteps: If you have an unpaid caregiver, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options available in Clallam County

Do you have an unpaid caregiver — a family member or friend — who regularly helps with your care, and if so, about how many hours a week?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive nights recently?
Has a doctor said you have trouble leaving home without help, or that you need skilled nursing or therapy care?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 67-year-old veteran in Columbia County living in a family member's home, needing help with toileting and mobility, managing heart disease, covered by Medicare, with income under $1,000/month and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. A few, like the Medicare home health benefit and Tailored Supports for Older Adults, line up closely with the facts you've given. Others, especially the VA programs, look promising but are missing one key piece of information (discharge status). Some programs need more detail before it's clear whether they're worth pursuing. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the VA, DSHS/HCS, or Medicare can make that call. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, living at home (not in a nursing facility), and have described needing help with mobility and toileting, which fits the kind of skilled and personal care need this benefit is built around.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language services, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at $0 for covered services.

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — it does not cover 24-hour care or personal care as the only service.
To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and order the care through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation from a doctor of homebound status and a certified need for skilled care (needed to start services, not something the screening can determine)
nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a home health referral and face-to-face assessment make sense for your situation.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Columbia County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 67, with monthly income under $1,000 (below the $3,976 limit) and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 (below the $84,354 single-person limit), and needing help with toileting and mobility, your situation looks financially and functionally similar to what TSOA is designed for, and you don't appear to be currently on Medicaid CN/ABP coverage. However, TSOA's statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, so new applicants currently go on a waitlist rather than receiving services right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, and no cost-sharing or estate recovery.

As of December 1, 2025, enrollment is paused statewide; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments happen once called from the waitlist. People already enrolled or approved before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008 or via washingtonconnection.org) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist.
Ask your Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting, since these don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've shared that you need help with toileting and mobility and that your monthly income is under $1,000, which is in the range CFC's financial rules consider (the SSI-related income standard is $994/month for a single person). CFC is a Washington Medicaid program for people who need this kind of personal care help, so it may be worth investigating further, but two key facts about your situation — whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid and exactly how many daily activities you need help with — aren't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around (transfers, walking), plus related supports like meal prep and housework once ADL help is already needed.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not the higher institutional income limits) — SSI-related limits are around $994/month income and $2,000 in resources for a single person.
To keep receiving CFC after the first assessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid community coverage
How many daily activities (ADLs) you need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE assessment.
Ask about applying for Washington Apple Health if you're not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran who needs help with daily activities like toileting and mobility, and with net worth reported within the VA's limit, most of the pieces the VA looks at for Aid & Attendance line up with what you've shared. The one thing not yet confirmed is your discharge status, which the VA requires to be anything other than dishonorable.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year, effective December 1, 2025 (versus $17,441 without it) — the actual payment is based on the gap between countable income and this limit.
Aid & Attendance and the VA Housebound benefit cannot both be received at once — a claimant gets one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your military discharge was anything other than dishonorable
nextSteps: Gather discharge paperwork (such as a DD-214) showing discharge status.
Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680.
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to discuss filing VA Form 21P-527EZ.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with net worth reported within the VA's pension limit, some of the Housebound benefit's requirements appear to match your situation. However, Housebound and Aid & Attendance can't both be paid at the same time, and Aid & Attendance's daily-activity criterion may be a closer fit given the toileting and mobility help you need. Discharge status also still needs to be confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313/year, effective December 1, 2025 (versus $17,441 standard).
Housebound and Aid & Attendance cannot be received together — only one applies at a time.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your military discharge was anything other than dishonorable
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your daily-activity needs.
Gather the same discharge and pension documentation used for Aid & Attendance.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, but this benefit is specifically for short-term skilled nursing facility care following a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, and it isn't yet known whether you've had such a stay. Given that you're currently living in a family member's home, this program may be less relevant right now.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing facility care, including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and medical supplies, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Costs include $0/day for days 1–20 and $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 figures); after day 100 the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: Keep this program in mind if a hospital stay occurs in the future.
Ask a hospital discharge planner about SNF coverage if hospitalization happens.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're living in a family member's home and need ongoing help with toileting and mobility, which suggests there may be a family caregiver involved, though this hasn't been confirmed. Washington's respite programs are built to support unpaid caregivers like this.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only if the care receiver has a developmental disability).

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, generally for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP respite amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, with no statewide dollar cap published.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP respite options.
If a family member provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers.

followUpQuestions: Was your discharge from military service anything other than dishonorable? This affects both VA Aid & Attendance and VA Housebound.
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied?
About how many daily activities do you need help with in total — for example, bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, or toileting — beyond toileting and mobility?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more days in a row recently, or is one expected?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 75-year-old WWII-era veteran in San Juan County with heart disease, mobility challenges, very low income and assets, Medicare coverage, and no listed Medicaid enrollment — several programs look worth a closer look with the right agency. The VA benefits and Medicare's home health benefit line up well with the facts you've given. A group of Washington Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC, and TSOA) could also be relevant, but the state's CARE functional assessment (which measures how much help someone needs with daily activities) hasn't been done yet, so we can't tell yet whether the functional requirement would be met. A few programs need more specific facts — like recent hospital stays or whether a family member is providing regular unpaid care — before it's clear whether they're worth pursuing. None of this is a decision about eligibility; only the administering agencies (VA, DSHS/HCA, Medicare, or the Area Agency on Aging) can make that call. Answering the follow-up questions below, and reaching out to Home and Community Services and a Veterans Service Officer, are good next moves.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a veteran with WWII-era service, a discharge that isn't dishonorable, need for help with daily activities like mobility, and income/assets that appear to fall within VA limits, this benefit closely matches the facts you've shared, and the rules engine found every checked criterion to pass.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing; the money can be used at the household's discretion.
For a veteran with no dependents, the added pension amount can reach up to about $29,093 per year as of December 1, 2025, depending on other income.

The underlying VA pension requires a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, service during a recognized wartime period, and income/net worth within limits set by Congress — WWII service from December 7, 1941 to December 31, 1946 counts as a recognized wartime period.

nextSteps: Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to file VA Form 21P-527EZ for pension with Aid & Attendance.
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting the need for help with daily activities.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The same veteran status, wartime service, and mobility limitations that support looking into Aid & Attendance also line up with Housebound's criteria, and the rules engine found every checked criterion to pass — though a veteran can only receive one of these two benefits, not both.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
It cannot be paid at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a veteran receives one benefit or the other, not both.
For a veteran with no dependents, the added amount is about $21,313 per year as of December 1, 2025.

nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits the described mobility and home-confinement situation, since only one can be paid.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is confirmed, the person lives at home rather than in a facility, and the described need for help with mobility suggests a possible skilled or personal care need, matching the criteria the rules engine passed for this benefit.

Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; Medicare-covered durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a doctor must certify the person is homebound (has trouble leaving home without help, such as a cane or walker) and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility.
Ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving San Juan County.
