whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra in-home and community services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To move forward, a CARE assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care — generally shown by needing significant help with several daily activities like bathing, transferring, or moving around, or needing supervision for memory-related issues.
The income and resource rules described (up to about $2,982/month income and $2,000 in resources for a single person) are current as of January 2026 figures in the source material.

Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment.

It also includes relief care, personal emergency response systems, and training for family members who help manage caregivers.

To use CFC, someone generally needs to be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) and have a CARE assessment showing significant help needed with three or more daily activities to continue.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to Washington's long-term-care Medicaid services, and the age, low income, and low assets you've described fit the type of situation this program is designed for — though the required functional (CARE) assessment for nursing-facility level of care hasn't been done yet.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared — an 82-year-old Korean-era veteran in Skamania County living in a family member's home, needing help with mobility and toileting, with income between $1,000–$2,999/month, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage — several programs look worth a closer look, especially the VA pension programs and Medicare's home health benefit. A few Washington Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Apple Health LTC, Community First Choice) also look potentially relevant, but the rules engine couldn't finish checking them because your exact monthly income, Medicaid enrollment status, and full count of daily-activity needs weren't provided. Two other programs (Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care) also have open questions about a recent hospital stay and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency (DSHS, the Health Care Authority, the VA, or Medicare) can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korean era, which is one of the recognized wartime periods for VA pension purposes, and you've described needing help with mobility and toileting, matching the Aid & Attendance clinical criterion of needing another person's help with daily activities.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

To get the underlying pension, a veteran needs a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period (the Korean Conflict, 6/27/1950–1/31/1955, is one of these), and net worth under $163,699.
The maximum payment for a veteran with no dependents, with Aid & Attendance, is $29,093/year as of December 1, 2025.

nextSteps: Complete VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting the need for help with daily activities
Gather income, asset, and military service records for the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korean era wartime period and appear to meet the general VA pension requirements. This benefit is for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, which is a different clinical standard than the daily-activity help described for you — and it cannot be paid at the same time as Aid & Attendance.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.

It uses the same underlying pension rules as Aid & Attendance — discharge status, wartime service, and the $163,699 net worth limit — but only one of the two benefits, Aid & Attendance or Housebound, can be paid at a time.
The maximum payment for a veteran with no dependents is $21,313/year as of December 1, 2025 — lower than the Aid & Attendance maximum.

nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer or the VA which benefit — Aid & Attendance or Housebound — better fits current needs, since only one can be paid
If pursuing this option instead of Aid & Attendance, complete VA Form 21-2680 for the housebound clinical determination

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and have described needing help with mobility and toileting, which points toward a possible personal-care or skilled-care need that this benefit is designed to help with, as long as a doctor certifies a homebound status.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time skilled nursing, physical/occupational therapy, and home health aide help (such as bathing, walking, and grooming) delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency, at no cost for covered services.

To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and order the care after a face-to-face assessment; home health aide help is only covered alongside skilled nursing or therapy, not as stand-alone personal care.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and skilled care needs
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving the Skamania County area

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55, your income (between $1,000–$2,999/month) is well under TSOA's $3,976/month limit, and your assets under $2,000 are well under its $84,354 resource limit — and you've described needing help with mobility and toileting, consistent with the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard this program uses. However, TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid — $3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person.
It has no cost-sharing and no estate recovery, and eligibility is reviewed every 12 months.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go onto a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than receiving services right away, and there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about joining the TSOA waitlist
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 82 and living in Washington, and you've described needing help with mobility and toileting — both are activities of daily living that COPES' nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment looks at. Your countable assets are under $2,000, which matches the resource limit described for this program. However, your monthly income falls in a bracket ($1,000–$2,999) that straddles the $2,982 special income limit, so it isn't yet clear which financial pathway would apply.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home and community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care — things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing services.
It's run by DSHS Home and Community Services and can be used in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To qualify, an assessment must show a need for nursing-facility level of care, and income/asset rules apply — a special income limit of $2,982/month for one pathway (or a lower $994 medically needy limit), and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income amount, so it can be compared against the $2,982/month special income limit or the lower $994 medically needy limit
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment
Ask a caseworker to review your exact income against COPES' financial limits

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility and toileting, which are activities of daily living relevant to this program, but it isn't known whether you're currently enrolled in community Medicaid or how many total daily activities you need help with, both of which are required to determine possible relevance.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice pays for help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, mobility, and meal preparation for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are enrolled in community Medicaid.
It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems, and training for family caregivers on how to manage paid providers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage
How many daily activities (ADLs) you need help with, since continuing CFC requires an unmet need in at least three
nextSteps: Check your current Medicaid/Apple Health enrollment status
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document your full ADL needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 82, live in Washington, and have described needing help with mobility and toileting; your assets are under $2,000, matching the resource limit mentioned for this program. whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is the Medicaid coverage and financial gateway that supports nursing facility care and waiver programs like COPES for people who need long-term services and supports.

Financial applications can be filed online, by mail or fax, or in person, and people 65+ or on Medicare are directed to washingtonconnection.org; a CARE assessment determines whether someone needs nursing-facility level of care.
Income is compared to a special income limit ($2,982/month) or a medically needy limit ($994/month), and resources must be at or below $2,000 for a single applicant.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against the $2,982/month special income limit
nextSteps: File the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, the pathway noted for people 65+ or on Medicare
Call the HCS regional intake line covering southwest Washington to request a CARE assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but it isn't known whether there has been a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, which is required before Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy, with no cost for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance of $217/day (2026) for days 21–100.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing ongoing help with mobility and toileting while living in a family member's home, which suggests there may be an unpaid family caregiver involved, but this hasn't been confirmed, and these programs are generally aimed at supporting that caregiver.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs give unpaid family caregivers short-term relief through Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which offers planned short-term care, counseling, and caregiver training.
FCSP amounts and rules vary by county/Area Agency on Aging, while Lifespan Respite generally requires the caregiver to provide 40+ hours a week of unpaid care.

Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or in a Medicare Savings Program?
Besides help with mobility and toileting, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 80, live in your own home in Lincoln County, WA, get help with dressing and mobility, have heart disease, are a Gulf War-era veteran, are married, have monthly income under $1,000, have countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000, and are covered by Medicare — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. A few important details are still missing (the exact amount of countable assets, whether you're already enrolled in any Medicaid program, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether a family member or friend provides regular unpaid care). Because those pieces are missing, this screening can only point you toward programs that seem worth investigating with an agency — it cannot say whether you or your spouse actually qualify for any of them. Please treat every item below as a starting point for a conversation with the listed agency, not as an approval or denial.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, you need help with dressing and mobility, and you have heart disease — all of which point toward a possible need for skilled or personal care at home, and you live in your own home rather than a facility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.
To qualify, a provider must certify homebound status and order the care, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency; covered services generally cost $0.
Personal or custodial care alone (without an accompanying skilled need) is not covered under this benefit.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor or nurse practitioner about whether you meet the homebound criteria and skilled-care need.
Ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 80 years old (well above the 55+ age requirement), your reported monthly income is far below the program's limit, your reported asset range appears to fit within the program's resource limits, and your need for help with dressing and mobility suggests you could meet the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard this program uses.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA does not require Medicaid enrollment, and income/resources can be above regular Medicaid amounts — the income limit is $3,976/month and the resource limit is $84,354 for a single person or $156,883 for a couple with a community spouse.
Eligibility also requires meeting nursing-facility level of care, and the program offers a 12-month certification with no cost-sharing and no estate recovery.

As of the most recent update, new applications are being placed on a statewide waitlist rather than processed immediately, though people already enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or by submitting form HCA 18-008.
Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about current waitlist status.
Ask about non-Medicaid respite options through your AAA while any waitlist is in effect.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 80 years old and need help with dressing and mobility, which are activities the program's assessment looks at when deciding if someone needs nursing-facility-level care. Your reported monthly income (under $1,000) is well within the program's income limit. However, the program generally requires countable assets at or below $2,000 for a single person (or $3,000 for a couple), and your reported asset range ($2,000–$85,000) straddles that line, so this can't be judged yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and help with meaningful goods/services or community transition costs.
Services can be provided in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility, and may not duplicate what Medicare or other insurance already covers.

To be considered, an assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care, based on things like needing help with 2 or more ADLs at higher assistance levels, or 3 or more ADLs, among other criteria.
The asset limit is $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with special protections for a spouse who continues living in the home.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount, to see whether it falls within the program's resource limit.
Confirmation of marital status details (already noted as married), since the asset limit differs for a couple versus a single applicant.
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to request a CARE assessment.
Submit financial application HCA 18-005 through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person.
Ask an HCS financial specialist to review your specific asset amount against the $2,000/$3,000 limit.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with dressing and mobility, which are the kinds of personal-care tasks this program covers, and you're already on Medicare. Whether this program is a fit also depends on whether you're enrolled in a qualifying community Medicaid program and how many activities of daily living you need help with — both currently unknown.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, medication management, transferring, and mobility, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
Services can be delivered at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living settings, and cannot replace what Medicare or other insurance already covers.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP).
How many daily activities you need help with, since ongoing CFC generally requires need in three or more.
nextSteps: Contact HCS central intake to request a CARE assessment.
Ask HCA or DSHS whether you currently qualify for community Medicaid coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As the financial gateway to long-term-care Medicaid programs, this may be relevant given your age (80), your reported help needs with dressing and mobility, and your low monthly income. As with COPES, the open question is whether your countable assets fall within the required resource limit.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid pathway that opens the door to nursing facility care and HCS waiver programs (like COPES), PACE, and institutional hospice.

Eligibility generally requires meeting nursing-facility level of care through a CARE assessment, and financial rules that include an income test (Special Income Level, $2,982/month) and, for SSI-related coverage, an asset limit of $2,000 single/$3,000 couple.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a phone/in-person interview and a CARE functional assessment; processing is generally within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: The exact countable asset amount compared to the $2,000/$3,000 resource limit.
Confirmation of how marital status affects which resource limit and spousal protections would apply.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application.
Call HCS central intake for your region to schedule a CARE assessment.
Ask about spousal impoverishment protections given your marital status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, which is the basic requirement for this benefit, but this coverage only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and it's not known whether you've recently had one.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.
Covered items include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, medications used in the facility, and certain ambulance transport.

A qualifying stay means at least 3 consecutive days as an inpatient (observation status doesn't count), with admission to the SNF generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and whether daily skilled care was ordered.
nextSteps: If you're hospitalized in the future, ask the hospital discharge staff whether your stay would qualify you for SNF coverage.
If you haven't had a qualifying hospital stay, ask your doctor about other options such as Medicare home health or Medicaid.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need ongoing help with dressing and mobility, and as a married person, it's possible your spouse or another family member provides unpaid caregiving — though this hasn't been confirmed. Respite programs are designed to support unpaid caregivers, so this may be worth exploring if that's the case.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for respite care such as in-home care or adult day services, awarded based on available funding.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers planned short-term respite care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver training, counseling, and support groups; amounts vary by county.
Applying involves either an online/phone/email application for Lifespan Respite, or contacting the local Area Agency on Aging for FCSP, which requires a caregiver screening/assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member, friend, or other unpaid caregiver regularly provides care, and how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about respite options.
If a spouse or family member provides regular care, ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment through the AAA.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact total in countable assets (bank accounts, investments, etc.), rather than a range?
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, which category?
How many daily activities (such as bathing, toileting, eating, transferring, or walking) do you need help with, and how much help is needed with each?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — being 85, living in your own home in Jefferson County, needing help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, having heart disease, being a peacetime-era veteran, being single, having Medicare, and having income between $1,000–$2,999 a month with assets under $2,000 — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. However, a few key pieces of information are still missing (your exact monthly income, whether you're already enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether anyone provides you unpaid caregiving support). Because these missing pieces affect several programs, the follow-up questions below matter a lot for making this screening more useful. Nothing here is a decision about what you'll receive — only the actual program office can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, live at home rather than in a facility, and need help with dressing, bathing, and transferring — needs that can overlap with the kind of skilled or aide-level care this benefit covers, at no cost for the covered services themselves.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, walking) — but aide help only counts if skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.

To qualify, a person must be considered homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended) and have a doctor certify the need for skilled care after a face-to-face visit; the care must be ordered and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about scheduling a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and skilled care needs.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85, needing help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, with income between $1,000–$2,999/month and assets under $2,000, you fall well within TSOA's income and resource limits, which are set well above regular Medicaid amounts. Note, though, that this program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care and have income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 (single) — well above regular Medicaid limits — without requiring Medicaid enrollment, and involves no cost-sharing.

As of the most recent information, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional review happens once someone is called from that waitlist. People already enrolled before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: If interested, you can submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008 or via washingtonconnection.org) to be placed on the current waitlist, understanding that services won't start immediately.
Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funding while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 85 and need help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, which are the kinds of daily living needs this waiver's assessment looks at, and your assets under $2,000 match the program's resource limit for a single person. Whether this program is worth pursuing further also depends on your exact monthly income compared to a specific dollar limit, which hasn't been provided yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, environmental modifications, and transportation, provided in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, an assessment must find that a person needs nursing-facility-level care — for example, needing substantial help with 2 or more, or 3 or more, activities of daily living such as bathing, transferring, or eating, or needing skilled nursing oversight.
Financially, one path requires income at or below $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026) and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against the program's income limit.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit or review a financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org to get an official determination of income eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, which relates to the level of daily-living assistance this program is built around. Whether it fits also depends on whether you're already enrolled in — or could enroll in — regular community Medicaid, which hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, and toileting, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training, in home or community settings.

To keep receiving these services at reassessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and financial eligibility generally requires being on regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage), not institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in — or able to enroll in — Washington Apple Health community Medicaid coverage.
nextSteps: Check your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status, or ask HCS about applying.
Request a CARE assessment through your regional HCS intake line to check functional criteria.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85, needing help with dressing, bathing, and transferring, and with assets under $2,000, some pieces here line up with what this program's assessment looks at. As with the related waiver programs, comparing your exact monthly income to a specific dollar threshold is the missing piece.

whatItCovers.text: This is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway into HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care.

One income path requires available income at or below $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026); there is also a Medically Needy path for people above that amount, and resource limits are $2,000 for a single person.
Applying involves a financial application (online, paper, phone, or in person) plus a separate functional CARE assessment; eligibility is generally decided within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income, to compare against the program's income limit.
nextSteps: Submit a financial application via washingtonconnection.org or by calling DSHS customer service.
Call your regional HCS intake line to schedule the required CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a specific kind of recent hospital stay, and it isn't yet known whether you've had one.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status) and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, medications used in the facility, and related supplies, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full patient cost beyond day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward.
nextSteps: If you have a hospital stay coming up or recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies you for Medicare SNF coverage.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health or Medicaid options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with dressing, bathing, and transferring points to an ongoing care need, which relates to what respite programs are meant to support. However, these programs are primarily built around supporting an unpaid family caregiver, and it isn't yet known whether such a caregiver is involved in your care.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers mainly through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP); Lifespan Respite requires the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care and generally excludes people already receiving respite from another public program.
FCSP is available to unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with services tailored through an assessment by a caregiver specialist at the local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family member or friend who provides regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask which respite options might fit your household's situation.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact monthly income? Several programs use a specific dollar cutoff, and your bracket ($1,000–$2,999) straddles some of those lines.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at 7 programs based on what you've shared: you are a 79-year-old Korea-era veteran living alone in your own home in Adams County, WA, with Medicare coverage, monthly income under $1,000, assets between $2,000 and $85,000, and help needed with eating and bathing. Several programs look worth a closer look, especially VA Aid & Attendance and Medicare's home health benefit, based on the facts you've provided. However, some important details are still missing — like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), whether you need help with more than two daily activities, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, and whether you've had a recent hospital stay. Because several rule outcomes are still unknown, please treat this as a starting point for questions to ask, not a final answer — only the administering agency for each program can determine actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Korea-era veteran with a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, and because you need help with daily activities like bathing and eating, you match the core clinical and service criteria for Aid & Attendance. The rules engine also found your net worth appears within the VA's limit, based on the financial details you've shared.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's pension, which the household can use at its own discretion for care costs.
One of the qualifying clinical criteria is needing another person's help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing — matching the help you described needing with eating and bathing.

As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Aid & Attendance could have a Maximum Annual Pension Rate of $29,093/year, compared to $17,441/year without it; the VA pays the difference between countable income and this rate.
Documentation typically includes VA Form 21-2680 completed by a medical examiner, along with details on service history, income, and assets.

nextSteps: Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to begin VA Form 21P-527EZ (pension application) and VA Form 21-2680 (medical examination for Aid & Attendance).
Gather documentation of military service, income, and assets to support the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare and have indicated needing help with personal care tasks like eating and bathing, and you live at home rather than in a nursing facility — all of which line up with this benefit's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and — only alongside skilled care — home health aide help with tasks like bathing, grooming, and walking.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a person must need part-time or intermittent skilled care, be considered homebound, and have a face-to-face assessment and provider order for care from a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you meet Medicare's homebound definition and whether a referral for a face-to-face assessment makes sense.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County from your provider.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 79, you meet the age requirement, and your reported income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits. Your reported need for help with eating and bathing is also consistent with the kind of functional need TSOA looks for. However, TSOA is currently paused statewide, so new applicants are placed on a waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility level care but are not on classic Medicaid, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid; it also serves unpaid family caregivers of such individuals.

Financial limits (effective 1/1/2026) include income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single applicant; TSOA does not require Medicaid enrollment and has no client cost-sharing or Medicaid-style estate recovery.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional assessments happen only when called from the waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or washingtonconnection.org to ask about joining the TSOA waitlist.
Ask about non-Medicaid respite funding through your local Area Agency on Aging while waiting, since TSOA intake is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've mentioned needing help with eating and bathing, which are two of the ADLs this program counts, and CFC is designed for people who need ongoing personal care while living in the community — which matches your situation of living in your own home in Adams County. However, CFC generally requires needing help with at least 3 ADLs to continue receiving services, and it also requires being enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, both of which are currently unknown for your case.

whatItCovers.text: CFC can cover personal care help with ADLs (including bathing and eating) and IADLs, plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, in settings including your own home.

To qualify, a person generally must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage) and, to continue receiving CFC at reassessment, must have an unmet or partially met need in at least 3 ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health Medicaid
How many activities of daily living you need help with, beyond eating and bathing
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to ask about a CARE functional assessment and your Medicaid enrollment status.
Ask whether you might qualify for community Medicaid based on your reported income and assets.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service era, and discharge status match the underlying pension requirements for this benefit, similar to Aid & Attendance. However, it's unknown whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, which is the specific clinical test for Housebound (different from needing help with ADLs). Note that a person can receive either Aid & Attendance or Housebound, but not both at the same time.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's pension for those substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability; a claimant receives either Housebound or Aid & Attendance, not both.
As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Housebound benefits could have a Maximum Annual Pension Rate of $21,313/year, compared to $17,441/year standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability (rather than needing help with specific daily tasks)
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your situation, since only one can be received at a time.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a recent hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, and it's unknown whether you've had such a stay recently — nothing in your profile suggests a recent hospitalization.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy services, and related medical supplies in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up SNF stay before admission.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've indicated needing ongoing help with daily activities, which is relevant to respite programs generally. whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for respite care, typically over 3–4 month award periods, subject to available funding.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) can provide planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) along with support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

These programs generally require an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — Lifespan Respite specifically requires 40+ hours/week of unpaid care — rather than being based on the care receiver's needs alone.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, etc.) providing regular care, and if so, how many hours per week
nextSteps: If you do have an unpaid caregiver, contact your local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite eligibility.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or would you need to apply for it?

Besides eating and bathing, do you need help with other daily activities such as dressing, toileting, transferring, walking, or bathing-related tasks? (Some programs look at whether you need help with 3 or more activities.)

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Vietnam era, your discharge was not dishonorable, your net worth appears to fall within the VA's $163,699 limit, and you've described needing help with daily activities like dressing, transferring, and toileting — all of which line up with this program's eligibility layers.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and order home health services.
Ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are over 55, your reported monthly income ($1,000–$2,999) falls under the $3,976 limit, and your reported assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall under the $84,354 single-person resource limit; your ADL needs (dressing, transferring, toileting) suggest a nursing-facility level of care. However, this program's statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed immediately.

whatItCovers.text: Serves people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid, plus their unpaid caregivers.
Financial limits (effective April 1, 2026): income at or below $3,976/month, and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person.
Provides a 12-month certification with no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go onto a first-come, first-served waitlist, with no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Home and Community Services to ask about joining the current TSOA waitlist.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds in the meantime, since TSOA enrollment is currently paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with dressing, transferring, and toileting — three ADLs — which matches the level of need this program looks for at reassessment ("unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs") [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility]. However, this program also requires being eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), and it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in or eligible for that coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, eating, mobility, medication management) and, when ADL help is also needed, IADLs like meal prep and shopping.
Also covers relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.
Services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, among other settings.

Financial eligibility requires being eligible for Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP) Medicaid in the community; people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage only need the functional (CARE) assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or would be eligible for) community Washington Apple Health/Medicaid coverage (CN or ABP).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to ask about applying for Apple Health and requesting a CARE functional assessment.
Gather income, asset, and Medicare information for the financial application if you are not already on qualifying Apple Health coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your Vietnam-era service, discharge status, and net worth appear to meet the underlying pension requirements, similar to Aid & Attendance. However, this benefit specifically requires being substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, and that information hasn't been provided yet. Note that a person cannot receive both Aid & Attendance and Housebound at the same time — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: A monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313/year (versus $17,441 standard), effective December 1, 2025.
This benefit cannot be received at the same time as VA Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you spend most of your time at home because of a permanent disability (substantially confined to your home).
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA benefits counselor whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your situation, since only one can be received.
If pursuing this option, have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting housebound status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, but this benefit requires a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and it's not yet known whether you've had such a stay.

whatItCovers.text: Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Days 1–20 cost $0; days 21–100 have a $217/day coinsurance (2026); after day 100, the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, along with a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a SNF stay before admission.
If there has been no qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your care needs (help with dressing, transferring, and toileting) suggest ongoing supervision may be needed, and you live in a family member's home, which could involve an unpaid caregiver — but it hasn't been confirmed whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which these programs generally require.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of care, delivered through contracted agencies.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, training, and supplies for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+, with services tailored through a TCARE assessment; amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility for your caregiver.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive nights in the last 30 days?
Do you have a family member or friend who provides you with regular, unpaid care? If so, about how many hours a week?
Do you rarely leave your home because of a lasting health condition, or do you need help/equipment to leave home at all?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 70, live in your own home in Yakima County, have Medicare, heart disease, help needed with bathing, toileting, and eating, and low income and modest assets — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. However, some important pieces of information are still missing (like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether anyone helps care for you unpaid), so this screening is only a starting point. None of these programs can be confirmed as something you qualify for — only the agency that runs each program can make that determination. Please use the follow-up questions below to gather more details, and consider reaching out to the contacts listed for each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have care needs (including a heart condition and help with several ADLs) that suggest skilled or personal care support could be useful — these are the general areas this benefit is designed to address.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost to you for covered services.

Home health aide help with bathing, walking, and grooming can be included, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy — it does not cover ongoing custodial or personal care alone.
To use this benefit, a doctor or qualifying provider must certify homebound status and order the care, and it must be delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a face-to-face assessment could establish homebound status and a need for skilled home health services.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Yakima County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are over 55, your reported monthly income (under $1,000) is well within the program's $3,976 limit, and your assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall within the $84,354 single-person resource limit. Your care needs (help with bathing, toileting, and eating) suggest a nursing-facility level of care, which this program also looks for. Importantly, this program is currently paused statewide, so new applicants go on a waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, including those with no unpaid caregiver, generally with no cost-sharing and no estate recovery.

As of the most recent information, enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025: new applications only place you on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional review happens once you're called from the waitlist; people already enrolled before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: If interested, submit a TSOA application (online via Washington Connection or paper form) to establish your place on the statewide waitlist.
While waiting, ask your local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with three ADLs — bathing, toileting, and eating — which matches the substantial-need threshold this program looks for. Your reported monthly income (under $1,000) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) are in a range that can align with Medicaid financial rules, but it's not yet known whether you are currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which this program requires.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with ADLs like bathing, toileting, eating, dressing, and transferring, plus related IADL help such as meal prep and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also can include relief care, personal emergency response systems, nurse delegation, and caregiver management training, and services can be provided in your own home.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care and, to continue receiving services, an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs — plus eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or would be eligible for) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) or Community Living Connections to ask about applying for community Medicaid and requesting a CARE functional assessment.
Ask whether your income and assets would fit within current Medicaid CN/ABP limits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and it's not yet known whether you've had such a stay.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, or speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Costs generally start at $0/day for days 1–20, then a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026) for days 21–100, with the patient responsible for all costs after day 100.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has said you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward.
nextSteps: If you're hospitalized in the future, ask the hospital discharge team whether the stay would qualify you for Medicare SNF coverage.
If there was a recent hospital stay, ask your doctor whether it meets the 3-day inpatient requirement.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have ongoing care needs that could involve a family member or friend providing unpaid help, but it isn't yet known whether you currently have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — which these programs are generally designed to support.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP, with no statewide cap but varying by county) can pay for short-term relief care — in-home, adult day services, or residential respite — for unpaid caregivers.
FCSP also can include caregiver support groups, counseling, training, and one-on-one consultation, with services tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment.
To qualify for Lifespan Respite vouchers, the caregiver must be unpaid and typically providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP eligibility is broader and varies by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, etc.) who regularly helps with your care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide.
nextSteps: If you do have an unpaid caregiver, contact your local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options in Yakima County.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in (or have you applied for) Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more nights in the recent past, and did a doctor say you need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Does anyone — a family member, friend, or neighbor — provide you with regular unpaid care or supervision, and if so, about how many hours a week?
Are you homebound, meaning it's hard for you to leave home without help or it's not recommended because of your health?

report.overallSummary: Based on what your family has shared so far, several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look for this 90-year-old veteran living in Okanogan County who needs help with bathing, eating, and dressing after a stroke. Programs like Medicare home health, several WA Medicaid long-term-care pathways, VA Aid & Attendance, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) all have at least some matching facts in the profile. However, a number of important details are still missing — exact monthly income and countable assets, discharge character from military service, whether there was a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing care. These missing pieces affect financial and clinical eligibility checks for several programs, so this screening should be treated as a starting point for conversations with the actual agencies, not a determination of what your family will receive. None of these programs can be confirmed as available until the administering agency reviews a full application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual is covered by Medicare, needs help with personal care tasks like bathing, eating, and dressing after a stroke, and lives at home rather than in a nursing facility — matching the basic building blocks for this Medicare benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help with bathing, grooming, and feeding — at $0 cost for the covered services — as long as skilled care is also being received.
To qualify, the person generally needs to be considered homebound and have a face-to-face provider assessment and a written care order.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment and a written order for home health services
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Okanogan County
Confirm with the agency, in writing, exactly which services Medicare will and will not cover before care starts

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 90 with reported income between $1,000–$2,999/month and assets between $2,000–$85,000, the individual's income and resources fall under the TSOA financial limits, and the need for help with bathing, eating, and dressing points toward the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement, while not currently being on classic Medicaid also matches TSOA's rules.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with financial limits well above regular Medicaid, no cost-sharing, and no estate recovery of the kind tied to Medicaid LTSS — though the program's statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008) online at washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to be placed on the statewide waitlist, since new applications are not being immediately processed

While waiting, contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile shows the individual is 90 years old and needs help with bathing, eating, and dressing — needs that line up with COPES's requirement of being age 65 or older and needing nursing-facility-level care based on ADL needs.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of personal care, such as adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living setting.
To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, which can be shown through unmet needs in ADLs like eating, toileting, bathing, or transferring.

informationStillNeeded: Exact monthly income amount, to compare against the Medicaid Special Income Level
Exact countable asset amount, to compare against the Medicaid resource limit
Marital status details, since being married can change the resource and income rules through spousal protections
nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE functional assessment
Gather documentation on any asset transfers or gifts from the past 5 years in case they're requested

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual needs help with three ADLs — bathing, eating, and dressing — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring) and related IADLs, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings like a private home, adult family home, or assisted living.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the individual is currently enrolled in — or could enroll in — community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
nextSteps: If not already on Apple Health, submit the LTSS financial application alongside a request for a CARE assessment
If already enrolled in Apple Health CN/ABP, ask HCS about scheduling just the functional (CARE) assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 90 with help needed for bathing, eating, and dressing, the profile matches the age and functional (nursing-facility level of care) building blocks Apple Health LTC uses as the financial gateway into HCS waiver programs.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports — it acts as the financial gateway to nursing facility care and HCS waivers such as COPES, and functional eligibility is based on a CARE assessment for nursing-facility level of care.

informationStillNeeded: Exact monthly income amount, to check against the Special Income Level or Medically Needy Income Level
Exact countable asset amount, to check against the resource limit
Marital status details, for possible spousal impoverishment protections
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit paper form HCA 18-005
Complete the required phone or in-person interview with an HCS financial specialist
Request the in-person CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual is a veteran from the Gulf War era or later (a recognized wartime period), needs another person's help with daily activities like bathing, eating, and dressing, and reported assets fall within the VA's net worth limit — all matching several of Aid & Attendance's clinical and financial building blocks.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of the VA pension, which the household can use at its discretion for care costs, with the payment based on the difference between countable income and the Maximum Annual Pension Rate.
Being married can raise the payment amount, since the Maximum Annual Pension Rate is higher for a veteran with a dependent.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the discharge from military service was characterized as anything other than dishonorable
nextSteps: Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680 describing the need for help with daily activities
File the pension application, VA Form 21P-527EZ, including service, income, and asset information
If income is above the payment limit, gather receipts or a care contract showing unreimbursed care costs for VA Form 21P-8416

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual is a veteran from a recognized wartime era, but the profile doesn't yet show whether they are substantially confined to the home by a permanent disability, which is the specific clinical test for this benefit (as opposed to needing help with daily activities, which fits Aid & Attendance instead).

whatItCovers.text: Housebound is a monthly cash payment added to the VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, but it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the discharge from military service was characterized as anything other than dishonorable
Whether the individual is substantially confined to the home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA representative or accredited claims agent whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits the situation, since only one can be received
If pursuing this route, have a medical examiner document housebound status on VA Form 21-2680

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual has Medicare Part A, but the profile does not indicate a recent 3-or-more-day inpatient hospital stay, which is required before this specific skilled nursing facility benefit applies.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay, Medicare Part A covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, generally at no cost for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21–100.
This is short-term skilled/rehab coverage, not a long-term custodial care benefit, and requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage
If no recent qualifying hospital stay exists, consider asking about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or VA benefits instead

programs.whyThisMayApply: The individual lives in a family member's home and needs ongoing help with daily activities, which suggests a family caregiver may be involved, but the profile does not yet confirm whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — a key eligibility factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers, including Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year), Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) services like in-home or adult day respite, support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with FCSP amounts varying by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to connect with the local Area Agency on Aging for an FCSP screening
If a family caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, also ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers through wapave.org

followUpQuestions: What is the exact monthly household income (not just the $1,000–$2,999 range)?
What is the exact countable asset/resource total (not just the $2,000–$85,000 range)?
Was the veteran's discharge from military service characterized as anything other than dishonorable?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past?
Is someone — such as a family member — providing regular unpaid caregiving, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Is the 90-year-old currently enrolled in any Washington Apple Health / Medicaid program?
Does the veteran spend most of the day substantially confined to the home because of a permanent disability?
What is the marital status detail needed for Medicaid spousal rules (already noted as married, but confirming this helps a caseworker apply spousal protections correctly)?
