report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared, several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look for this 67-year-old veteran living with a family member in Grays Harbor County who needs help with mobility, toileting, and dressing after a stroke, and who has Medicare coverage. However, quite a few key details are still unknown — including Medicaid enrollment status, military discharge type, exact net worth, any recent hospital stays, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because so much information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point rather than a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below will make the next conversation with each agency much more productive. No program listed here has determined eligibility — only the administering agency can do that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live in a family member's home rather than a nursing facility, and you have care needs (mobility, toileting, dressing) that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care support — all of which line up with the basic requirements for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology delivered at home, at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.
Home health aide help — such as bathing, dressing, and grooming — can also be covered, but only while you're also receiving skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy.
To qualify, a doctor must certify that you are homebound and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, and the care must be ordered and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether a face-to-face assessment for home health services would be appropriate.
If a referral is made, ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've shared that you need help with mobility, toileting, and dressing — three ADLs — which matches the level of need this program looks for. You also have Medicare, but it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in or could qualify for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, which is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, mobility, and medication reminders, plus related help such as light housework, meal prep, and transportation to medical appointments when ADL help is also needed.
It can also cover relief (respite) care for family caregivers, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training on how to manage personal care providers.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and must be eligible for community Medicaid (not the institutional financial rules) — people already on qualifying Apple Health coverage may only need the functional assessment step.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're currently enrolled in (or might be able to enroll in) community Medicaid, sometimes called Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Apple Health coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask DSHS or an eldercare benefits counselor whether you might qualify for community Medicaid coverage given your income and assets.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You served during the Korea era, which is a recognized wartime period, and you need help with daily activities like mobility, toileting, and dressing. Two pieces still need to be confirmed: your discharge status, and whether your net worth falls within the VA's pension limit — your reported assets of $160,000 or more are close to the current limit of $163,699.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.
As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Aid & Attendance can have income brought up to $29,093 per year, compared with $17,441 under the standard pension.

To qualify for the underlying pension, discharge must not have been dishonorable, net worth must be within the VA's limit ($163,699 effective December 1, 2025), and there must be at least one day of service during a recognized wartime period — the Korean Conflict (6/27/1950–1/31/1955) is one of these periods.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service (must not be dishonorable).
Your exact net worth, to compare against the VA's $163,699 limit.
nextSteps: Gather military discharge paperwork (DD-214) to confirm discharge type.
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to review income, assets, and unreimbursed medical/care expenses.
Ask a doctor to complete VA Form 21-2680 if you decide to apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your Korea-era service meets the wartime service requirement, and the screening indicates you may be substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability. As with Aid & Attendance, your discharge type and exact net worth still need to be confirmed, and note that a person can only receive Housebound or Aid & Attendance, not both.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.
As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Housebound benefits can have income brought up to $21,313 per year, compared with $17,441 under the standard pension.

This benefit shares the same underlying pension rules as Aid & Attendance — discharge status, net worth limit of $163,699, and wartime service requirements — and cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.

informationStillNeeded: Your discharge type from military service.
Your exact net worth, compared against the VA's $163,699 limit.
nextSteps: Gather discharge paperwork (DD-214) to confirm discharge type.
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to discuss whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance may be a better fit for your situation.
Ask a doctor to complete VA Form 21-2680 documenting housebound status if you decide to apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is one of the requirements for this benefit. However, it isn't known whether you've had a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, which is required for this specific benefit to apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.
Covered items include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in the facility.

To qualify, there must be a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has said you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
Ask your doctor whether skilled nursing facility care might be appropriate for your stroke-related needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and have ongoing care needs related to your stroke, which fits the general idea behind respite programs — support for families providing regular care. It isn't yet known whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing that regular care, which several of these programs require.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies statewide, for caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility — plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling, with services and amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.
These programs generally require that the caregiver is unpaid and that the care receiver and caregiver both live in Washington.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing unpaid regular care for you, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging serving Grays Harbor County to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite eligibility.
If a family caregiver is involved, gather details on hours of care provided per week to discuss with the AAA.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has anyone looked into whether you might qualify for community Medicaid coverage?
What type of discharge did you receive from military service (for example, honorable, general, dishonorable)?
Do you know your exact countable net worth, and is it above or below $163,699?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and has a doctor said you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 71-year-old veteran living in Whatcom County who needs help eating, has had a stroke, is married, has income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000, and is covered by Medicare — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. The clearest match right now is the Medicare home health benefit, since all the basic conditions line up. Several Medicaid long-term-care programs (Apple Health LTC, COPES, Community First Choice, and TSOA) also look promising on income and asset grounds, but they all depend on a functional assessment (how much help is needed with daily activities) that hasn't been completed yet, so we can't tell yet whether the 'nursing facility level of care' bar is met. A couple of other programs (Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care) depend on details — a recent hospital stay and whether there's an unpaid caregiver — that we don't have yet. None of this is a determination of eligibility; only the administering agency can decide that. Answering a few more questions would make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is reported to be covered by Medicare, is living at home (in a family member's home) rather than in a facility, and has care needs stemming from a stroke, including help with eating — all of which line up with this benefit's basic requirements of Medicare enrollment, an indicated skilled or personal care need, and living at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help with eating, bathing, and walking) when also receiving one of the skilled services — all at $0 cost to the beneficiary for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a health care provider must certify a need for skilled care and homebound status, meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended because of the person's condition; a Medicare-certified home health agency then delivers the ordered care.

nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor to complete the required face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Whatcom County.
Have the agency and doctor coordinate a written plan of care (CMS-485) before services start.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is generally for people age 65 and older with limited income and assets who need help at a nursing-facility level of care. At 71, with reported monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000, the age, income, and asset conditions line up with what's described here. The stroke diagnosis and the reported need for help with eating are the kind of care needs this program is designed around, but whether the case meets the specific 'nursing facility level of care' threshold hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver that layers extra services on top of personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, environmental modifications, and transportation, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care — generally meaning skilled nursing needs, or an unmet need in multiple activities of daily living such as eating, bathing, transferring, or toileting, or supervision needs from cognitive impairment plus an ADL need.

Financially, this pathway generally looks at income up to about $2,982/month and countable assets of $2,000 for a single person (with higher combined limits and protections when a spouse remains in the community), which is above the income and assets reported here.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like eating, bathing, transferring, toileting, or moving around) need help, so a nursing-facility level of care can be checked.
Confirmation this is filed as institutional Medicaid, since married status and a working spouse's finances can affect the calculation.
nextSteps: Apply for the financial piece using form HCA 18-005 through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Call the regional Home and Community Services intake line to request the CARE functional assessment.
Ask a caseworker how spousal income and asset protections would apply in this household.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This Medicaid option pays for personal-care help for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community. The reported need for help with eating and the stroke history point toward the kind of personal-care needs this program addresses, and the low reported income and assets are consistent with Medicaid-related programs generally. However, it isn't yet known whether community Medicaid coverage is in place or how many activities of daily living need help — both are required pieces of this program's eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems, relief care, and caregiver management training, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and financially must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than the higher institutional income limits.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the applicant is currently enrolled in, or could enroll in, Washington's community Medicaid coverage.
How many daily activities (like eating, bathing, or moving around) need help.
nextSteps: Check with the Health Care Authority or a benefits counselor about current or potential community Medicaid enrollment.
Request a CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake to document care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and the financial gateway to programs like COPES. At age 71, with reported monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000, the age, income, and asset pieces described here fall within the ranges this program uses. The stroke diagnosis and need for help with eating are relevant to the kind of care need this program is built to support, though the specific functional (nursing-facility level of care) determination hasn't been made yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care and community LTSS, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally requires meeting SSI-related income and resource rules — income up to about $2,982/month and resources of $2,000 for a single person, with spousal protections when a spouse remains in the community — plus a functional finding of nursing-facility level of care through a CARE assessment.

The application process involves a financial application (HCA 18-005, available online via washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax), a financial interview, and an in-person CARE assessment; for Whatcom County the relevant intake line is the Region 2 HCS number.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help, to determine whether the nursing-facility level of care standard is met.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or an HCS office.
Call the Region 2 HCS intake line (800-780-7094, which covers Whatcom County) to request the CARE assessment.
Prepare documentation of income, assets, and any recent asset transfers for the financial review.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is reported, which is one of the two basic requirements for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a need for daily skilled care, and it isn't yet known whether such a hospital stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical/occupational/speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, with $0 cost for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance from day 21 on.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was recommended afterward.
nextSteps: Ask the hospital or doctor whether any recent stay was billed as inpatient (not observation) for 3 or more consecutive days.
If a qualifying stay exists, ask the discharge team about Medicare-certified skilled nursing facilities in the area.

programs.whyThisMayApply: TSOA serves people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on regular Medicaid, with income and asset limits well above Medicaid's — limits this household's reported income and assets would fall within. The stroke diagnosis and need for help with eating are relevant to the kind of care need this program addresses, but the functional (nursing-facility level of care) determination hasn't been made, and the program currently has a statewide enrollment pause.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is for people age 55+ who need nursing-facility level of care but are not enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, with income up to about $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 single or $156,883 married — both above the amounts reported for this household.

As of the most recent information available, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed for immediate services, though current participants keep their benefits.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities need help, to check the nursing-facility level of care requirement.
nextSteps: If interested, submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to be placed on the statewide waitlist, understanding a full assessment happens only when called from the waitlist.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds available while the TSOA waitlist is in effect.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with eating and the stroke diagnosis suggest ongoing care needs, which is one condition for respite programs. It isn't yet known whether an unpaid family member or friend (such as a spouse) is providing regular care, which is a key factor in these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through options like Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program, which offers planned short-term care and support tailored through a caregiver assessment, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse or other family member) is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility and services.
If a spouse or relative provides 40+ hours a week of unpaid care, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers.

followUpQuestions: How many daily activities — such as bathing, dressing, transferring, walking, toileting, or medication management — need some or full help, and how often?
Is the applicant currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (community Medicaid), and if so, under which category?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (not just observation) recently, and was skilled care recommended afterward?
Is there a spouse or family member who unpaid and regularly provides care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at 9 programs for a 92-year-old veteran in Mason County who lives at home, needs help with transferring, is married, has Medicare, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000. Two programs — VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit — had every rule-engine check come back as a pass based on what was shared, so they look especially worth a closer look with the administering agency. Several Medicaid and state programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC, TSOA) look potentially relevant because of age, income, and county, but key details are still missing — especially how many daily activities need help with (beyond transferring), the exact asset amount, and current Medicaid enrollment status. VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs also appear on the list, but each needs more information before their fit can be judged. Nothing here is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency (DSHS, the VA, or Medicare) can make that call. The next best step is usually a phone call or assessment request to gather the missing pieces below.

programs.whyThisMayApply: He served during the Vietnam era, which is a recognized wartime period, and the rules engine found his discharge status, net worth, and wartime service all pass the underlying VA pension requirements. He needs help with transferring, which matches the Aid & Attendance clinical criterion of needing another person's help with daily activities.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's pension for those who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing; the money can be used however the household chooses.
The payment amount depends on the difference between countable income and a published limit (the Maximum Annual Pension Rate), and is higher for a veteran with a dependent, such as a spouse.

nextSteps: Have a medical examiner complete VA Form 21-2680
Gather income, asset, and marital/dependent information to support the underlying pension application (Form 21P-527EZ)
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to start the claim

programs.whyThisMayApply: He is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, and needs help with transferring, which points to a skilled or personal care need — all three checks the rules engine looked at came back as a pass.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (like assistance with walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled care — generally at no cost for the covered services.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must complete a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and order care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask his doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health services
Ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Mason County

programs.whyThisMayApply: He is 92 and lives in Mason County, Washington, which meets the age requirement for COPES. His reported monthly income (under $1,000) is well below the program's income limit ($2,982/month). He needs help with transferring, which is one of the daily-activity needs COPES functional assessments look at, though it isn't yet clear if his overall needs would meet the program's nursing-facility-level-of-care threshold.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of basic personal care, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and skilled nursing, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find the person needs a nursing-facility level of care, based on things like help with transferring, bathing, eating, and other daily activities, or supervision needs from memory or thinking problems.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities he needs help with, and how much help (this determines the functional/nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement)
The exact bracket his countable assets fall into
Confirmation of his and his spouse's marital and asset situation for the Medicaid resource rules
nextSteps: Contact Washington's Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office
Ask HCS how spousal asset rules would apply given the marriage

programs.whyThisMayApply: He needs help with transferring, which is one of the personal-care activities Community First Choice covers, and he is an adult over 18 living in Mason County. Whether he's already enrolled in community Medicaid, and how many total daily activities he needs help with, are not yet known, which are the two main gaps for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like transferring, bathing, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems, relief care, and caregiver management training.
It's available to adults through Home and Community Services once someone meets a nursing-facility level of care and qualifies for regular community Medicaid (not institutional financial rules).

informationStillNeeded: Whether he is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid
How many daily activities he needs help with
nextSteps: Check whether he is already enrolled in Washington Apple Health Medicaid
Request a CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 92, his age meets the program's requirement, and his monthly income under $1,000 is well within the Special Income Level of $2,982. He needs help with transferring, which is part of what the functional assessment considers, but it isn't yet clear whether his full set of needs and his exact assets and marital resource situation would fit the program's rules.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is the Medicaid coverage and financial gateway that connects eligible people to nursing facility care and to home-and-community waivers like COPES, based on meeting income, resource, and functional rules.

Applying involves a financial application (such as HCA 18-005) plus a required interview and an in-person CARE assessment by an HCS social worker; both financial and functional pieces are reviewed together.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities he needs help with
The exact bracket his countable assets fall into
Details about his marital and spousal asset situation
nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or submit HCA 18-005 by mail or fax
Request the CARE assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: His wartime service, discharge status, and net worth all pass the underlying VA pension checks, the same as for Aid & Attendance. However, Housebound requires being substantially confined to the home by a permanent disability, which is a different and stricter standard than needing help with transferring, and this hasn't been confirmed. Also, a veteran can only receive Housebound or Aid & Attendance, not both — and he already appears to meet the Aid & Attendance daily-activity criterion.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's pension for those who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
The payment amount is based on a published Maximum Annual Pension Rate and is higher with a dependent, such as a spouse.

informationStillNeeded: Whether he is substantially confined to his home because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with the VA or a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits his situation, since only one can be received

programs.whyThisMayApply: He has Medicare Part A, which is required for this benefit, but it is not yet known whether he has had a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, which is required to trigger skilled nursing facility coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing facility care — including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy — after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies him for Medicare SNF coverage

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 92 he is well above the program's age-55+ requirement, his income under $1,000/month is within the TSOA limit, and his assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within the single or married resource limits. However, whether his overall care needs meet the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard hasn't been confirmed, and the program is currently paused statewide with new applicants going on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, using income and resource limits that are higher than regular Medicaid, with no cost-sharing.

As of December 1, 2025, new applications only place someone on a statewide, first-come, first-served waitlist rather than starting services immediately; people already enrolled before that date keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities he needs help with, to assess the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections to ask about the current waitlist
Ask about other non-Medicaid respite options while waiting, since TSOA intake is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: He needs ongoing help with transferring, which points to an ongoing care need, but it isn't yet known whether he has an unpaid caregiver (such as his spouse) providing regular care — this is the main factor these respite programs are built around.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers, including Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which offers flexible in-home, adult day, or facility-based respite plus caregiver training and support groups.

informationStillNeeded: Whether he has an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) providing regular care, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and screening for Mason County

followUpQuestions: Beyond transferring, does he need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, bed mobility, walking, or taking medications — and if so, how much help (a little, a lot, or full assistance)?
What is the approximate countable asset total — closer to $2,000, closer to $85,000, or somewhere in between? This matters a lot for which Medicaid program fits.
Is he currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) already?
Is his spouse currently living with him in the home, and do they have separate or combined countable assets?
Does he have an unpaid family caregiver (such as his spouse) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week?
Has he had a hospital inpatient stay (3 or more days) recently, or is one expected?
Does he spend most of his time confined to the home because of a permanent disability, separate from needing help with transferring?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 88-year-old Gulf War-era veteran in Mason County, WA, living alone in their own home, needing help with toileting, diagnosed with Parkinson's, on Medicare, with income between $1,000–$2,999/month and countable assets under $2,000 — several programs may be worth a closer look. VA Aid & Attendance and Medicare's home health benefit look like strong fits given the veteran status, Medicare enrollment, and daily-activity help needed. Several Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Community First Choice, Apple Health LTC) could also apply, but a full functional (ADL) assessment and an exact income figure are still needed to know whether the level-of-care and income rules are met. A couple of programs — VA Housebound, Medicare SNF coverage, TSOA, and general respite programs — depend on information we don't have yet (housebound status, a recent hospital stay, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver), so they're included for awareness but are less clearly matched right now. This is an educational screening only — none of this determines eligibility. The agencies that run these programs make that call.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile indicates the person is a veteran who served during the Gulf War era or later, is age 88, needs help with a daily activity (toileting), and reports countable assets under $2,000 — all consistent with the criteria this program looks at (wartime service, discharge status, help needed with daily activities, and net worth within the VA's limit).

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities, such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

Qualifying service periods include the Gulf War era beginning 8/2/1990, with no end date given, and the underlying VA pension also requires a net worth within limits (currently $163,699) and a discharge that was not dishonorable.
The payment amount is based on the difference between countable income and an annual limit (MAPR) set by the VA; for a veteran with no dependents, the 2025 MAPR with Aid & Attendance is $29,093/year.
Applying involves VA Form 21-2680 completed by a medical examiner and documentation of service, income, assets, and any unreimbursed medical/care expenses.

nextSteps: Gather military service records, VA file number, and discharge paperwork.
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680 describing the toileting assistance and Parkinson's-related needs.
File the pension application (VA Form 21P-527EZ) along with income, asset, and medical documentation, or work with a Veterans Service Officer for help.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is on Medicare, lives at home, and has a care need (help with toileting, plus a Parkinson's diagnosis), matching the program's basic requirements around Medicare enrollment, a skilled or personal care need, and living outside a nursing facility.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B covers part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and — only when also receiving one of those skilled services — part-time home health aide help with bathing, walking, grooming, and feeding.
To qualify, a provider must certify a homebound status and order the care, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency; covered services cost $0 to the beneficiary.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only care needed — aide help is only covered alongside skilled care.

nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a face-to-face assessment for home health services can be scheduled.
If ordered, request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Mason County.
Discuss with the agency what will and won't be covered before care starts.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile shows age 88 (meeting the 65+ requirement) and countable assets under $2,000, both of which line up with two of COPES' basic tests. However, whether the toileting help and any other daily-activity needs add up to Medicaid's 'nursing facility level of care' standard, and whether the exact monthly income is at or below the $2,982 Special Income Level, are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver that layers extra services on top of personal care for people who need nursing-facility-level care but choose to stay in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

Covered services can include adult day care/day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, nursing services, and help transitioning to a new living situation.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find the person needs nursing-facility level care — for example, unmet needs in 3 or more daily activities (like toileting, bathing, transferring) at certain levels, or fewer activities at a higher level of need, or a nursing/cognitive-supervision need.

Financially, income is generally compared to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and countable resources to the $2,000 single limit, which this family's reported assets appear to meet.

informationStillNeeded: The number and level of daily activities (like toileting, bathing, transferring, eating) the person needs help with, so a nursing-facility level-of-care determination can be made.
The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the $2,982/month limit.
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Ask HCS how the toileting need and Parkinson's diagnosis might factor into the level-of-care assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: CFC is Washington's main Medicaid personal-care option for people meeting an institutional level of care while living at home. The profile mentions a toileting need, which is one of the ADLs CFC covers, but current Medicaid enrollment status and the full count of ADL needs are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as toileting, bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must find nursing-facility level of care, and the person must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) — clients already on qualifying Apple Health coverage generally only need the functional assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid coverage.
How many daily activities the person needs help with, since continuing CFC requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status through Washington Connection.
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake.
Ask HCS whether the toileting need alone is enough, or whether other ADL needs should be documented.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the financial gateway program behind COPES and CFC. Age 88 and reported assets under $2,000 line up with the general eligibility path, but the level-of-care finding and exact income compared to the $2,982 Special Income Level are still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like COPES and community LTSS, administered by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally requires meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based criteria, an income test compared to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) or a Medically Needy pathway, and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person, plus a functional nursing-facility level-of-care finding via the CARE assessment.
Applications can be submitted online via washingtonconnection.org, by paper form, or by phone, followed by a financial interview and an in-person CARE assessment.

informationStillNeeded: The number and severity of daily-activity needs, to assess against the nursing-facility level-of-care standard.
The exact monthly income amount.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax.
Call HCS central intake for the applicable Washington region to schedule the CARE assessment.
Gather documentation of income, assets, and any recent asset transfers for the financial review.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, and net worth appear consistent with the underlying VA pension rules, but whether the person is substantially confined to home by a permanent disability — the specific clinical test for this benefit — is not yet known. Note that this benefit cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance, which already looks like a stronger match based on the toileting-help detail.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound is a monthly pension increase for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability, and it cannot be paid together with Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.

The underlying pension eligibility (discharge status, net worth limit, wartime service) is the same as Aid & Attendance, with an added regulatory definition requiring either a 100% disability rating plus additional 60%+ ratings, or being substantially confined to the home and its immediate premises.
Applying uses the same forms as Aid & Attendance, including VA Form 21-2680 for the medical examination and VA Form 21P-527EZ for the pension itself.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is substantially confined to home most of the time because of a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound is the better fit, since only one can be received.
If pursuing this path, have a medical provider document housebound status on VA Form 21-2680.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit, but nothing in the profile indicates a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, which is the other core requirement, so this program's fit is currently unclear.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, including semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, and related medical supplies.
There is no coinsurance for days 1–20, coinsurance of $217/day for days 21–100 (2026), and the patient pays all costs after day 100 in that benefit period.

Ask the hospital or doctor whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay based on the specific situation.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid, including for unpaid family caregivers or for people without one.

Financial limits (as of 1/1/2026) are income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person, which the reported figures appear to fall within, alongside a functional nursing-facility level-of-care requirement.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs (Lifespan Respite vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program, and DDA respite for those with a qualifying developmental disability) provide short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers, generally requiring that an unpaid caregiver is providing regular care.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 86-year-old veteran living in a family member's home in Stevens County, needing help with mobility, managing heart disease, covered by Medicare, with monthly income between $1,000–$2,999 and countable assets under $2,000 — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it cannot determine whether you or your family member actually qualify for any program. Some key details (like exactly how many daily activities need help, your precise monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment, any recent hospital stays, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved) are still unknown, and they matter a lot for several of these programs. Filling in those gaps with an intake worker or the agencies listed below will make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being covered by Medicare, living at home (in a family member's residence rather than a nursing facility), and needing help with mobility together match the three requirements this program's screening checks for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (like walking, bathing, and grooming) — but only while skilled nursing or therapy is also being received.

Covered services generally cost $0, though durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible. To qualify, a doctor must certify a homebound status and order the care, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified agency.

Homebound status means leaving home is difficult or not recommended and normally requires help or special equipment (like a cane or walker) — attending adult day care does not disqualify someone from this status.

nextSteps: Ask the treating doctor about a face-to-face homebound assessment and referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency
Request the agency's list of covered vs. non-covered services in writing before care starts

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 86 years old, the age requirement for COPES is met, and countable assets under $2,000 fit within the program's resource limit. Needing help with mobility could relate to the functional (ADL) requirement, but it isn't yet clear whether the level of need and the exact monthly income (compared to the $2,982 limit) would fit.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care (CFC), including adult day care/adult day health, chronic-disease self-management and wellness training, help transitioning to community living, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation. It can be used in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.

To apply, there is a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a request for a CARE functional assessment through the regional Home and Community Services intake line; ongoing case management for in-home clients is handled by the local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, or moving around) require help, and how much help is needed
The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment
Gather income and asset documentation for a possible financial application (HCA 18-005)
Ask a caseworker how the mobility-related needs might be scored under the nursing-facility-level-of-care criteria

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with mobility suggests there may be personal-care needs that CFC is designed to address, but it isn't yet known whether your family member is enrolled in community Medicaid or how many activities of daily living need help — both are required pieces of this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, as well as related household tasks (meal prep, shopping) when ADL help is also needed. It also includes relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training.
Services can be delivered in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting, and CFC can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in (or has applied for) Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
How many daily activities require help with, since ongoing CFC generally requires help with 3 or more
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake
Ask about how CFC and COPES might work together if both apply

programs.whyThisMayApply: The age requirement (65+) and the asset limit (under $2,000) both line up with what's known. This program is also the financial gateway to several other Medicaid long-term-care services, but whether the mobility-related needs reach the required functional level, and how the exact monthly income compares to the $2,982 threshold, are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (like COPES), PACE, hospice in an institution, and community long-term services and supports.

To apply, there's a financial application (available online, by paper, phone, or in person) plus a required interview and an in-person CARE functional assessment; the standard processing time is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities require help with, to assess the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement
The exact monthly income amount, to compare against the $2,982 Special Income Level
nextSteps: Submit or update a financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office
Call regional HCS intake to schedule a CARE assessment
Ask how the Medically Needy pathway might apply if income turns out to be above the Special Income Level

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is in place, which is one requirement. However, nothing in the profile indicates a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay, which is the other key requirement for this short-term skilled nursing facility benefit.

whatItCovers.text: When the requirements are met, this benefit can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transportation — for up to 100 days per benefit period following a qualifying hospital stay.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20 in a benefit period, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost for day 101 onward (2026 figures).

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify for SNF coverage before any SNF admission
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider whether Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits may be better first options

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported income and assets both fall within TSOA's higher financial limits, and the age requirement (55+) is met. However, whether the mobility-related needs meet the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard is unknown, and — importantly — TSOA has paused new enrollment statewide, which affects how soon services could actually start.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on regular Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above Medicaid's; there is no client cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

As of the most recent information, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced date for the pause to end. People already enrolled or approved before that date keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities require help with, to assess the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist status and whether non-Medicaid respite funds are available in the meantime
Ask an Area Agency on Aging caregiver specialist about alternative supports while TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living in a family member's home and needing help with mobility suggests ongoing care needs, which is one requirement for respite programs. It's not yet known whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly providing that care, which is central to eligibility for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options), for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies — with no statewide dollar cap, though amounts vary by county Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend provides regular unpaid care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP eligibility and available funding in Stevens County
If applying for Lifespan Respite vouchers, apply online at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or call 253-442-3214

followUpQuestions: Exactly how many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or moving around) does your family member need help with, and at what level (some help vs. a lot of help)?
What is the exact monthly income, rather than just the $1,000–$2,999 range? Some programs have cutoffs right around $2,982 or $3,976 per month.
Is your family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have they applied?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past?

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — a 75-year-old veteran in Adams County with Parkinson's, help needed with dressing, Medicare coverage, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. VA Aid & Attendance and Medicare's home health benefit appear to line up well with the details given. A few other programs could also be relevant, but the screening needs more information (like Medicaid enrollment status, how many daily activities need help, whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, and any recent hospital stay) before a fuller picture is possible. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the agency that runs each program can confirm that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes a WWII-era veteran with a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, needing another person's help with daily activities like dressing, and countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) that fall under the VA's net worth limit — all of which match the criteria this program looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing, or who meet other clinical criteria like being bedbound or in a nursing home.

For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093 (effective December 1, 2025), paid as the difference between the veteran's countable income and that rate, split into monthly payments; the money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.

Applying requires VA Form 21-2680 completed by a medical examiner, plus the underlying pension paperwork (service history, income, assets, and other details); unreimbursed care costs only need to be documented separately if income is above the payment limit.

nextSteps: Gather military discharge (DD-214) and service dates to confirm wartime service documentation
Have a physician complete VA Form 21-2680 describing the need for help with dressing and other daily activities
File VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension along with the Aid & Attendance request, or contact a Veterans Service Officer for help applying

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile confirms Medicare coverage, a need for personal/skilled care help (Parkinson's, dressing assistance), and living at home rather than in a facility — all factors this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (like bathing, grooming, and dressing) — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided, at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a provider must certify a homebound status and order the care, which is delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency after a face-to-face assessment.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider whether a face-to-face assessment and home health order could be arranged
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Adams County

programs.whyThisMayApply: Community First Choice serves adults who need an institutional level of care but live at home; the profile mentions a 75-year-old with Parkinson's needing help with dressing and a very low monthly income (under $1,000), which could fit the financial track for this program, but it isn't yet known whether Medicaid enrollment or a 3+ ADL need — both required for CFC — are in place.

whatItCovers.text: CFC pays for personal care help with activities like dressing, bathing, eating, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, for people who qualify financially through community Medicaid (CN/ABP) or SSI-related rules and meet a nursing-facility level of care with an unmet need in at least three ADLs to continue services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many activities of daily living need help, since CFC generally requires an unmet need in at least three ADLs to continue
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Ask HCS or a Community Living Connections specialist whether current income/assets would support a Medicaid CN/ABP financial application

programs.whyThisMayApply: The veteran's wartime service, discharge status, and net worth appear to fit the underlying pension rules for this benefit, but it isn't yet known whether the person is substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability — the specific clinical test this program uses. Note that Housebound and Aid & Attendance can't both be received at once, so this would typically be considered only if Aid & Attendance doesn't apply.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound is an increased monthly pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability, and it cannot be paid together with Aid & Attendance — only one or the other.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313 (effective December 1, 2025), compared with $17,441 standard.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the veteran is substantially confined to home most of the time because of a permanent disability
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits the situation, since only one can be received

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A enrollment is confirmed, but this benefit also requires a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and that information hasn't been provided yet.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's order for daily skilled care — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Covered costs include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, and medications; for 2026, days 1–20 cost $0, days 21–100 cost $217/day coinsurance, and the patient pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is being planned or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would meet Medicare's 3-day qualifying stay rule

programs.whyThisMayApply: The stated age (75), monthly income (under $1,000), and countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within this program's income and resource limits, but whether the functional (nursing-facility level of care) requirement is met is unknown, and the program is currently on a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist rather than open enrollment.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers, using income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person as of 2026).

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional assessment happens only when called from the waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a nursing-facility level of care determination (based on ADL needs) has been made or could be requested
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist process and timing
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds as a possible option while TSOA enrollment is paused

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes an ongoing care need related to Parkinson's and help with dressing, which fits the general idea behind respite programs, but it isn't known whether an unpaid family caregiver is currently providing regular care — a requirement for most of these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (amounts vary by county/Area Agency on Aging), and DDA waiver respite for people with a developmental disability that began before age 18.

Lifespan Respite generally requires the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care, live in Washington along with the care receiver, and not already be receiving respite through another public program.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing regular care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program (TCARE) screening

Besides dressing, are there other daily activities (like bathing, eating, walking, or transferring) where help is needed, and about how many in total?
Is there a family member or friend who provides regular unpaid care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 75, living in your own home in Jefferson County, Washington, a Vietnam-era veteran, divorced, covered by Medicare, with monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range and assets of $160,000 or more, and needing help with transferring — the screening tool flagged four programs to look into with a professional. One (Medicare home health) lines up well with what you've told us so far. The other three have some missing pieces — like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether a family member or friend provides you regular unpaid care — that would need to be answered before a fuller picture is possible. None of this is a decision about eligibility; only the agency that runs each program can determine that. Please review the follow-up questions below, since answering them would sharpen this screening.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 75, already covered by Medicare, living in your own home in Jefferson County, and dealing with a need for help transferring — this is the type of situation this Medicare benefit is built to help with, when a doctor confirms you're homebound and need skilled or related care at home.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy and speech-language services, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, walking assistance) when it's paired with one of those skilled services.
It also can include durable medical equipment and supplies for home use, and covered services cost $0, though DME may involve a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To use this benefit, a doctor or qualified provider must do a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status and the need for skilled care, and order services from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a face-to-face assessment and homebound certification would apply to your situation
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned needing help with transferring, which is one of the activities of daily living this program is designed to help with, and you live in Washington (Jefferson County), which is where this state Medicaid option operates. However, it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in (or could qualify for) community Medicaid, or how many total ADLs you need help with — both of which matter for this program's rules — so it's unclear at this point whether the details would line up for you.

whatItCovers.text: This program can cover personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, medication management, transferring, and mobility, plus related support such as meal prep, housework, and travel to medical appointments when ADL help is also needed.
It also can include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and help transitioning back into the community.

Eligibility generally requires meeting a nursing-facility level of care assessment and being eligible for community Medicaid (not institutional Medicaid rules); to keep receiving it, a person generally needs an unmet or partly met need in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage
How many activities of daily living you need help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about requesting a CARE functional assessment
Ask HCS whether your income and assets would need a Medicaid financial application, or whether you might already qualify for a related Medicaid pathway

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, which is one requirement for this coverage, but it's not yet known whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more consecutive days, which this program requires. Right now your profile describes living at home with a need for help transferring, rather than a recent hospitalization, so this may be less immediately relevant unless that changes.

whatItCovers.text: This coverage can pay for a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in a skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

It requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not counting observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

Keep this program in mind as a resource if short-term rehab needs come up after a hospitalization

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with transferring suggests some ongoing care needs, but respite programs are designed to give unpaid family caregivers a break — and it isn't yet known whether you have a family member or friend providing you regular unpaid care, which is a key requirement for these programs.

Lifespan Respite vouchers require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver to live in Washington, and that the family cannot afford respite on their own; the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ and is tailored through a local assessment.

nextSteps: If a family member or friend does provide you regular care, they could contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options in Jefferson County
