Please review the follow-up questions below, since answering them would sharpen this picture considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home in Franklin County, and has care needs (help with bathing and eating, plus a dementia diagnosis) that suggest a possible skilled or personal care need — all factors this program's screening checked as met.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment for use at home.

Home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and walking is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy — it isn't available as stand-alone personal care.
Covered home health services cost $0; DME still involves the usual 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a doctor must certify homebound status and order the care through a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor whether they meet Medicare's homebound criteria and would benefit from a face-to-face assessment for home health orders.
If appropriate, ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is over 55, and their reported monthly income ($1,000-$2,999) and assets ($2,000-$85,000) fall within TSOA's financial limits ($3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person). Their dementia diagnosis and need for help with bathing and eating also point toward the kind of nursing-facility-level care need this program is designed for, and the screening found they are not currently on classic Medicaid.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits that are higher than regular Medicaid.
There is no cost-sharing for TSOA participants, and it does not carry the estate-recovery rules that apply to some other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

As of the most recent update, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025: new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional review happens only once someone is called from that waitlist.

nextSteps: Submit a TSOA application (online at washingtonconnection.org, or the paper HCA 18-008 form) to establish a place on the statewide waitlist.

While waiting, contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 88 and needs help with bathing and eating, which are the kinds of personal-care needs this program is built around. However, this program also requires eligibility for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) and an assessed need for help in at least three daily activities to continue receiving services, and we don't yet know your family member's Medicaid status or the full count of daily activities they need help with.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and help managing caregivers.

To keep receiving this program's services at a later review, a person must have an unmet or partly met need in at least three daily activities, and must qualify for regular community Medicaid (not institutional Medicaid rules).
Services can be delivered in someone's own home, an adult family home, or several other community settings, and cannot replace Medicare or other insurance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting) your family member needs help with
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask about current Apple Health (Medicaid) status, since that affects what additional paperwork is needed.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days with a doctor's order for daily skilled care afterward, and we don't know whether such a hospital stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical/occupational/speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Costs in 2026 include no daily charge for days 1-20, then $217/day coinsurance for days 21-100, and full cost after day 100.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it is meant for short-term skilled recovery care following hospitalization.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (as an admitted inpatient, not observation status)
Whether a doctor determined daily skilled nursing or therapy care was needed after that hospital stay
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge planners whether Medicare would cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay.
If there was a recent hospital stay, confirm with the doctor whether daily skilled care was ordered.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs ongoing help and supervision due to dementia and difficulty with bathing and eating, which points to an ongoing care need. Respite programs are designed to support the unpaid caregivers who provide that kind of care, but we don't yet know whether there is an unpaid caregiver in the picture or how many hours a week they might be providing care.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies (in-home respite, adult day services, and other options).

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, along with support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment — amounts vary by county and there is no statewide dollar cap.

To qualify for Lifespan Respite vouchers, the unpaid caregiver generally needs to be providing 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver must live in Washington, and the caregiver must not be able to afford respite on their own.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver currently providing regular care
How many hours per week that caregiver provides care, if applicable
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers and FCSP respite options available in Franklin County.
If there is an unpaid caregiver, ask the local Area Agency on Aging about a TCARE caregiver assessment to determine what level of respite support may be available.

followUpQuestions: Is your family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application been submitted?
Besides bathing and eating, does your family member need help with other daily activities — such as dressing, using the bathroom, walking, or moving from bed to chair?
Is there a family member or friend who provides regular unpaid care right now, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has your family member had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights in a row recently, and if so, did a doctor recommend ongoing daily skilled care afterward?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 72, live in your own home in Snohomish County, are a Gulf War-era veteran, need help with bathing, dressing, and toileting, have Medicare, and have modest income and savings — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility. Each agency listed below makes its own eligibility decision. A few key facts are still missing (whether you're enrolled in or eligible for Apple Health/Medicaid, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether you're mostly confined to home), so some answers below are incomplete until those are known. Please review the follow-up questions and consider discussing this list with a Home and Community Services (HCS) intake worker, your local Area Agency on Aging, or a VA representative.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a veteran of the Gulf War era or later with a discharge that was not dishonorable, needing help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, and toileting, and with net worth reported within the VA's limit, your profile lines up with the core VA Aid & Attendance criteria described in the program materials.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment on top of a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance for a veteran with no dependents is $29,093/year, compared to $17,441/year at the standard rate; the actual payment is the difference between countable income and this maximum.
Applying involves VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension and VA Form 21-2680 completed by a medical examiner for the Aid & Attendance clinical finding.

nextSteps: Gather military service records, income and asset documentation, and unreimbursed medical expense records.
Ask a physician to complete VA Form 21-2680.
File VA Form 21P-527EZ with the VA Pension Intake Center or a VA regional office, or work with a Veterans Service Officer for help.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have indicated a need for personal care help — the general pattern this Medicare benefit is designed to support once a skilled care need and homebound status are confirmed by a provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, OT, or speech therapy — it does not cover 24-hour care or custodial care alone.
Qualifying requires homebound status (trouble leaving home without help, and leaving takes considerable effort) and a face-to-face provider assessment and order.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or health care provider whether you meet Medicare's homebound definition and whether a skilled care need can be certified.
If so, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Snohomish County.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 72 (meeting the age requirement), live in Snohomish County — one of the four counties with PACE coverage — and need help with multiple ADLs, which is consistent with the nursing-facility-level-of-care criterion PACE uses.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Washington PACE coverage includes 21 ZIP codes in Snohomish County, served through Providence PACE (ElderPlace), which has a center in Everett South; whether your specific ZIP code is covered needs to be checked against DSHS's PACE ZIP code list.

Eligibility involves a state functional assessment for nursing-facility level of care plus a financial eligibility determination by HCS, and enrollees receive services exclusively through the PACE network.

nextSteps: Confirm whether your specific Snohomish County ZIP code is in the PACE service area by contacting Providence PACE intake at (206) 320-5325 or checking the DSHS PACE ZIP code list.
Ask about starting the state functional assessment and HCS financial eligibility review.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 72 you meet the age requirement, your reported income and assets appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits, and needing help with multiple ADLs is consistent with the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard TSOA uses. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed immediately.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid.
Financial limits (effective April 1, 2026 figures) allow gross income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 for a single applicant, and Medicaid enrollment is not required.

Enrollment is currently paused statewide; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist, with no announced date for the pause to end, though people already enrolled keep their services.

nextSteps: If interested, submit a TSOA application through Washington Connection or HCA form 18-008 to establish a place on the waitlist, even though services won't start immediately.
While waiting, ask your Area Agency on Aging about non-TSOA respite funds (like FCSP) that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with bathing, dressing, and toileting — three ADLs — which meets the functional pattern this program looks for. However, this program also requires being eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), and it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in or would qualify for that coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice pays for personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring, and more) and, when ADL help is also needed, IADLs like meal prep, housework, and shopping.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training, and can be delivered in your own home.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and financially the person must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in — or would be found eligible for — community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or DSHS about applying for community Medicaid if you are not already enrolled, since that is required alongside the functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, and net worth appear to line up with the underlying VA pension rules described for this benefit. However, Housebound requires being substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, and that specific clinical detail isn't yet known — and this benefit cannot be paid together with Aid & Attendance, so it would typically only be relevant if Aid & Attendance doesn't apply.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability.
As of December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status for a veteran with no dependents is $21,313/year, versus $17,441/year standard.
It uses the same application forms and process as Aid & Attendance, and a claimant can only receive one of the two benefits, not both.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home because of a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Discuss with the VA or a Veterans Service Officer which of Aid & Attendance or Housebound better fits your situation, since only one can be paid at a time.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is the base requirement for this benefit, but it only applies after a recent qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — and it isn't yet known whether you've had such a stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.

Covered items include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and medications and supplies used in the facility, with no charge for days 1–20 and a $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 rates).

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days along with an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You appear to need ongoing care or supervision, which is part of what these respite programs are designed around, but it isn't yet known whether you have an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — a requirement for the main voucher program.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, while the Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term care with no fixed statewide cap, tailored through a local assessment.

In Snohomish County specifically, FCSP-type respite funding through the county and Homage Senior Services has ranged roughly $1,000–$4,362 per year on a sliding fee scale, though this can change and should be confirmed locally.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver (family member, friend, etc.) providing you with regular care or supervision.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the Snohomish County Area Agency on Aging (Homage Senior Services) to ask about caregiver support and respite options, whether or not there's currently an unpaid caregiver in place.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or do you think you might qualify for it based on your income and assets?
Does anyone — a family member, friend, or neighbor — provide you with regular unpaid care or supervision, and if so, about how many hours a week?
Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more days recently, or do you expect one, along with an ongoing need for skilled nursing or therapy care?
Would you say you rarely leave your home without help, or that leaving takes a lot of effort because of a lasting health condition?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at 9 programs based on the details shared: an 85-year-old Washington resident (Island County) living in a family member's home, needing help with bathing and toileting, diagnosed with dementia, a Korea-era veteran, single, with income between $1,000-$2,999/month, assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage. Based on this, VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit look like strong matches to explore first, since all the known facts line up with their basic criteria. Several Washington Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Apple Health LTC, Community First Choice) and TSOA also look potentially relevant, but confirming them depends on a few missing details — especially the exact monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment status, and how many daily activities need help. A few other programs (VA Housebound, Medicare SNF coverage, respite care) depend on information not yet provided. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency (DSHS, HCA, VA, or Medicare) can determine actual eligibility. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Korea-era veteran needing help with daily activities like bathing and toileting, and with reported assets and income that appear to fall within the program's net worth limits, this profile closely matches the core Aid & Attendance criteria.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing and dressing.
Eligibility involves wartime service (Korean Conflict service counts), a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, and net worth within the VA limit of $163,699.
For the period effective December 1, 2025, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance for a veteran with no dependents is $29,093/year.

nextSteps: Contact a Veterans Service Officer (VSO) or the VA to begin VA Form 21P-527EZ.
Have a medical provider complete VA Form 21-2680.
Gather military service records, income, and asset documentation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Being covered by Medicare and needing help with bathing and toileting due to dementia while living in a home setting (not a nursing facility) fits the basic profile for Medicare's home health benefit, pending a doctor's certification of homebound status and skilled-care need.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care and, alongside it, home health aide help with bathing, grooming, and other personal care, at no cost for covered services.
To qualify, a doctor must certify the person is homebound and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment and referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Island County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85, with income and assets that appear to fall within TSOA's generous limits, and care needs — bathing, toileting, dementia — suggesting nursing-facility-level care, this profile fits TSOA's general criteria. However, statewide enrollment is currently paused, so new applicants join a waitlist rather than receive immediate services.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above Medicaid's — up to $3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person.

Statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date, though current participants keep their services.

nextSteps: Because statewide enrollment is currently paused, ask about joining the waitlist via washingtonconnection.org or a local Area Agency on Aging.
Ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 85 with dementia and needing help with bathing and toileting, this profile suggests a level of care similar to what COPES is designed for. Assets under $2,000 fit the program's resource limit for a single person, though the exact monthly income (reported only as somewhere between $1,000 and $2,999) still needs to be compared against the program's income threshold.

whatItCovers.text: COPES eligibility generally looks at being age 65+, an assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, and asset limits of $2,000 for a single person — this profile's reported assets fit within that limit.
If eligible, COPES can add services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, and skilled nursing on top of personal care.
Income eligibility depends on staying at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), or qualifying through a Medically Needy pathway if income is higher.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to see how it compares with the $2,982/month income limit used for this program.
nextSteps: Contact the Home and Community Services (HCS) intake line for Island County (Region 2: 800-780-7094) to request a CARE assessment.
Submit financial application HCA 18-005 via washingtonconnection.org or by mail to HCS-LTSS.
Have the exact monthly income figures ready to discuss with a caseworker.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for help with bathing and toileting, along with a dementia diagnosis, points toward the kind of personal-care assistance CFC covers. Whether this program fits further depends on current Medicaid enrollment and the total number of daily activities needing help, both of which aren't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, toileting, dressing, and eating, plus related support such as meal prep and housework.

To qualify financially, a person generally needs to be enrolled in community Medicaid (CN or ABP); functional eligibility requires a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, and continuing services requires unmet needs in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the applicant is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health Medicaid.
How many activities of daily living the applicant needs help with — this program generally requires help with three or more.
nextSteps: Confirm current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with HCA or DSHS.
Discuss the full list of ADL needs with a caseworker during a CARE assessment.
Contact HCS central intake to request an assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As an 85-year-old Washington resident with assets under $2,000 and care needs — bathing, toileting, and dementia — that may meet a nursing-facility level of care, this profile aligns with the financial and functional gateway that Apple Health LTC provides. The exact income figure would still need to be checked against the program's threshold.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to Medicaid long-term care programs like COPES; it requires meeting SSI-related criteria, a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment, and asset limits of $2,000 for a single person.
Income eligibility is based on the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026); people with higher income may still qualify through a Medically Needy pathway.
Application involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a functional CARE assessment arranged through the regional HCS intake line; Island County falls under Region 2.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, to see how it compares with the $2,982/month income limit used for this program.
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit HCA 18-005 by mail.
Call the Region 2 HCS intake line (800-780-7094) to arrange a CARE assessment, since Island County is in Region 2.
Confirm the exact monthly income to compare with program limits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Korea-era veteran, some underlying pension requirements may be met, but it's unclear whether the applicant is substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability, which is the specific test for this benefit — and it cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability; it can't be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance.
Eligibility depends on the same underlying pension rules (wartime service, discharge status, net worth) as Aid & Attendance, plus a clinical finding of being substantially confined to the home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the applicant is substantially confined to the home because of a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Discuss with a Veterans Service Officer whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound is the better fit, since only one can be received.
Have a medical provider document any home-confinement due to permanent disability, if applicable.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A enrollment is in place, but this benefit only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and it isn't known whether that has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility — including nursing care, therapy, and meals — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay of 3 or more days occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare SNF coverage applies.
Keep records of any hospital admission dates and status (inpatient vs. observation).

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living in a family member's home suggests there may be an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care for someone with dementia and ADL needs, which is the situation respite programs are designed to support — though this hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs like Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers providing regular care.
Lifespan Respite generally requires the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours a week of care; Family Caregiver Support Program eligibility and amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there's an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and about how many hours a week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to discuss caregiver hours and screen for FCSP or Lifespan Respite eligibility.
If the caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, consider applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher.

followUpQuestions: What is the exact monthly income amount, rather than just the $1,000-$2,999 range?
Is the applicant currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)?
Besides bathing and toileting, does the applicant need help with other daily activities, such as dressing, eating, transferring, walking, or managing medications?
Is there an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient recently?
Does the applicant have trouble leaving home, or are they largely confined to the home because of a disability?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 73-year-old living alone in Walla Walla County with dementia, needing help with transferring and dressing, on Medicare, with income under $1,000/month and assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — five programs may be worth a closer look. Two (Medicare home health and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) line up well with the details you've given so far. The others could also be relevant, but the screening is missing a few key facts (like current Medicaid enrollment, total number of ADLs needing help, whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and any recent hospital stay) that would sharpen the picture. None of this is a decision about eligibility — only the administering agency can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below, and reaching out to the offices listed in the next steps, are the best ways to move forward.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with transferring and dressing, plus a dementia diagnosis) that suggest a possible skilled or personal-care need — all factors this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language services, and medical social services delivered at home, at no cost for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help with things like bathing, dressing, and walking is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language services.
To qualify, a doctor must certify the person as homebound and order the care, and the care must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency after a face-to-face assessment.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only type of help needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers the person 'homebound' under Medicare's definition.
Whether there is a specific skilled nursing or therapy need (not just personal care help) that a doctor would order.
nextSteps: Talk with the person's doctor about ordering a home health assessment.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Walla Walla County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 73 (above the program's age-55 minimum), has monthly income under $1,000 (within the program's $3,976 limit), and countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 (within the $84,354 single-person resource limit). Needing help with transferring and dressing, along with a dementia diagnosis, points toward the kind of care needs this program looks for, and the profile does not indicate current Medicaid enrollment, which fits this program's target group.

whatItCovers.text: This program is designed for people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid — it can also serve people with no unpaid caregiver.
Financial limits as of a recent update are gross income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person.
Approved participants generally have no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs, with 12-month continuous eligibility once approved.

Important: statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025. New applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional review happens only once someone is called from that waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit paper form HCA 18-008 to get placed on the statewide waitlist, since a request date matters under the current pause.
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about interim options, such as non-Medicaid respite funds, while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 73, lives in Walla Walla County, has a dementia diagnosis, and needs help with transferring and dressing — the kind of personal-care needs this program is designed to help with. Their income (under $1,000/month) and assets ($2,000–$85,000) may fall within the ranges this program considers, but the screening doesn't yet know whether they're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, or how many total activities of daily living they need help with, both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care.
To keep receiving these services at a later review, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living.
Financially, this program requires eligibility for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) — not the same financial rules used for nursing-home Medicaid.
Services can be provided in someone's own home, and clients get to choose their own care providers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in, or would qualify for, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid coverage.
The total number of daily activities (like bathing, eating, toileting, walking) that need help, not just transferring and dressing.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or the local Area Agency on Aging whether the household's income and assets would support a community Medicaid application, if not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this program is specifically for short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay, and the screening doesn't know whether there has been a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay, which is required to qualify for this coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a skilled nursing facility for a limited time after a qualifying hospital stay — up to 100 days per benefit period — but it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

A qualifying stay means at least 3 consecutive days as a hospital inpatient (time spent under observation status does not count), followed by admission to a skilled nursing facility within about 30 days.
After day 20, there is a daily coinsurance cost, and after day 100 the person pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay is being planned or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay.
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs ongoing help with transferring and dressing and has dementia, which suggests a need for regular supervision or care — a factor these programs consider. However, the screening doesn't know whether an unpaid family member or friend is currently providing that regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for most of these respite options.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies statewide.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential) plus support groups, counseling, training, and equipment, with amounts varying by county and assessed through a caregiver specialist screening.

These programs generally do not stack with each other — for example, Lifespan vouchers exclude people already receiving respite through another publicly funded program, with a limited waitlist exception.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (family member or friend) providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they help.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about Family Caregiver Support Program options in Walla Walla County.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 54, live in Pacific County, WA, married, in your own home, have Medicare, need help with bathing, have a dementia diagnosis, monthly household income in the $3,000–$3,999 range, and countable assets under $2,000 — this screening found six programs worth learning more about. None of these results are eligibility decisions; only the administering agency (DSHS, the Health Care Authority, Medicare, or an Area Agency on Aging) can confirm whether you actually qualify. Several important pieces of information are still unknown (such as whether a formal disability determination exists, the exact number of daily activities you need help with, and whether you're already enrolled in Apple Health/Medicaid), so for the Medicaid-related programs this report leans more on questions to explore than firm recommendations. The clearest starting point right now is the Medicare home health benefit, since your Medicare enrollment and home-based care need line up well with how that benefit works. For the others, a conversation with Home and Community Services or your local Area Agency on Aging can fill in the gaps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home rather than in a facility, and you have an indicated care need (help with bathing, along with a dementia diagnosis) — these are the three factors this screening checked, and all of them were confirmed as met based on what you've shared.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled care delivered at home by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost to you for the covered services.

Covered services can include skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help with bathing, walking, and grooming — but aide help is only covered while you're also receiving one of the skilled services.
To qualify, a provider must certify a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care and confirm homebound status through a face-to-face assessment before care starts.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal care alone when no skilled service is also needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether a home health referral and face-to-face assessment for homebound status and skilled care need would be appropriate.
If a referral is made, ask for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Pacific County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 54 with a dementia diagnosis and need help with bathing, and your countable assets are under $2,000, which meets this program's resource limit for a single person. COPES also considers cognitive impairment along with an unmet daily-living need (like bathing) as one path to meeting its functional requirement, which may be relevant given your dementia diagnosis. However, whether you meet the age/disability rule and the income rule for this program is not yet known from what you've shared.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care for people who need more support than basic personal care provides.

It can help pay for things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and help with a transition to a new home setting.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a need for nursing-facility-level care — one path to meeting this is having a cognitive impairment (such as disorientation or memory impairment) combined with an unmet or partially met need in at least one daily activity like bathing.

Financially, available income generally must be at or below $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), though a medically-needy pathway exists for people above that amount, and countable resources generally must be at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is a formal disability determination under SSI criteria (since you're under 65)
How many daily living activities you need help with, beyond bathing
Your exact monthly income amount, to compare against the program's income limit
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services regional intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org or an HCS office to start the process.
Ask HCS how the medically-needy income pathway works if your income turns out to be above the Special Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your need for help with bathing is the kind of personal-care support this program is built around, and it's designed for people who live in the community rather than a facility, which matches your situation of living in your own home in Pacific County. Whether you're already enrolled in the required Medicaid coverage category, and whether your overall daily-living needs meet the 3-or-more-activity threshold, isn't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and medication management, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

It's available to people who are eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community and who have an assessed nursing-facility level of care need; continuing on the program at reassessment requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities.
It can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services, and services may not replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal support you already have.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're currently enrolled in (or would be eligible for) community Medicaid coverage
How many daily living activities you need help with in total
nextSteps: Ask HCS or your local Community Services Office whether you're eligible for community Medicaid coverage.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS regional intake to evaluate your daily-living support needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the financial application that opens the door to Medicaid-funded long-term services in Washington, including COPES and CFC, and your countable assets under $2,000 meet the resource limit used for this pathway. Because your age-based disability status and exact income relative to the program's limits aren't yet confirmed, it's not yet clear how you'd fit into this program's specific eligibility categories.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care, PACE, and community LTSS, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility involves meeting SSI-related income rules (income at or below about $2,982/month, or a medically-needy pathway above that) and resource limits of $2,000 for a single person, along with a functional assessment of nursing-facility level of care.
The application involves both a financial application (HCA 18-005, available online, by mail, fax, or in person) and a separate functional CARE assessment arranged through HCS regional intake.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation of age and whether a qualifying disability determination applies
How many daily living activities you need help with
Your exact monthly income, to compare against program limits
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or an HCS office.
Call HCS regional intake (Region 3 covers Pierce, Kitsap, Thurston, and southwest Washington — confirm which region serves Pacific County) to request a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and nothing in your profile indicates a recent hospitalization, so it's unclear whether this benefit is currently relevant to your situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Covered costs include $0/day for days 1–20 and $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 rates); after day 100, the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before choosing a facility.
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health or Medicaid options instead for ongoing needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need related to dementia and help with bathing, which fits the kind of situation these respite programs are meant to support unpaid family caregivers through. Since you're married, a spouse could potentially be providing unpaid care, but this hasn't been confirmed, so it's not yet clear whether the caregiving-hours requirement for some of these programs would be met.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care such as in-home respite or adult day services, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, while the Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term care and other supports (like counseling and training) through local Area Agencies on Aging, with amounts varying by county.

These programs generally serve unpaid caregivers of adults who need care, and eligibility and service levels for the Family Caregiver Support Program are determined through a screening/assessment with a caregiver specialist.

followUpQuestions: Has a doctor, Social Security, or DSHS ever determined that you meet SSI's disability criteria (separate from being 65 or older)?
About how many daily activities do you need help with — for example bathing, dressing, eating, moving around, toileting, or managing medications?
What is your exact gross monthly household income, rather than the $3,000–$3,999 range?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 85-year-old in Grays Harbor County with dementia who needs help with eating and toileting, is married, has Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid), and has monthly income and assets in the ranges you gave — several Washington programs look worth a closer look. Because exact income and asset figures, the full list of daily-living needs, and caregiver details aren't yet known, we can't tell which specific programs someone would actually qualify for. Only the agencies that run these programs (DSHS/HCS, the Area Agency on Aging, or Medicare) can make that determination. This screening is meant to help you know which offices to call and what to ask.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This family is already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), which is the main financial gateway for Community First Choice. The person needs help with eating and toileting, which are two of the ADLs CFC covers, but continuing CFC eligibility generally requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and the profile only mentions two so far.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (including eating, toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring) and, when ADL help is also needed, IADLs like meal preparation and housework, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

Financial eligibility is based on being enrolled in community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than the stricter institutional rules, and people already on Apple Health typically don't need a new financial application — just a functional assessment.

informationStillNeeded: The full list of daily activities the person needs help with, to see if it reaches the three-ADL threshold used for CFC
nextSteps: Request a CARE assessment through HCS central intake to have all ADL and IADL needs formally reviewed

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with eating and toileting, dementia) that suggest a possible need for skilled or supportive home-based care — all conditions this benefit is built around.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and — only alongside skilled care — home health aide help with things like bathing, grooming, and feeding, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended), have a face-to-face assessment and provider certification, and have the care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only kind of care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers the person homebound and in need of part-time skilled nursing or therapy
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment and possible referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency

However, whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (such as a spouse) is not yet known, and the program is currently paused for new enrollment statewide.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports unpaid family caregivers of people 55+ already on Apple Health who meet a nursing-facility level of care and choose not to use other Medicaid LTSS, offering benefits in steps: a one-time $250, then $500/year, then a higher monthly amount (about $928/month for 07/2025–06/2026) for things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and training.
Enrollment in MAC has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver, age 18 or older, providing regular care to the person
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the current waitlist and next steps

programs.whyThisMayApply: The COPES waiver is generally aimed at people 65 and older who need a nursing-facility level of care, and this family profile describes an 85-year-old with dementia who needs help with eating and toileting — needs that are often used as markers for that level of care. The household's income falls in the $1,000–$2,999/month range and assets in the $85,000–$160,000 range, both of which could fit within COPES's rules depending on the exact numbers and the spousal protections that apply to married couples.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, environmental modifications, and transportation, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability) and have a CARE assessment finding a nursing-facility level of care — for example, based on cognitive impairment requiring supervision plus an unmet ADL need, which can include eating or toileting.

On the financial side, income is compared to the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), and resources are compared to $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, but married couples have additional spousal-impoverishment protections that can raise the effective resource limit (state amount $72,529 as of 7/1/2025; federal maximum $162,660 as of 1/1/2026).

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount for the household
The exact countable asset amount and how it is divided between spouses
Whether the person is already enrolled in an SSI-related Apple Health program
nextSteps: Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Ask an HCS financial specialist how spousal impoverishment rules would apply given the household's specific income and assets

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health long-term care is the financial gateway that connects to COPES and other Medicaid waiver services, and this profile — an 85-year-old with dementia needing help with eating and toileting, already on Medicare and Apple Health — matches the kind of situation this program is built around. As with COPES, the exact income and asset figures will determine which specific pathway (regular or Medically Needy) applies.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and institutional or community LTSS, and is administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Income is compared against the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway available for income above that level (Medically Needy Income Level $994/month, 1/1/2026).

Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with spousal impoverishment protections for married couples raising the effective allowance (community spouse resource allowance up to $162,660 as of 1/1/2026).

Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online at washingtonconnection.org) plus a CARE assessment arranged through regional HCS intake; for Grays Harbor County, that would be the Region 3 (Olympic Peninsula/SW WA) intake line.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount
The exact countable asset amount and how resources are divided between spouses
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application
Call the Region 3 HCS intake line (800-786-3799) to request a CARE assessment
Ask about how the Medically Needy pathway or spousal impoverishment rules might apply

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing facility care — including nursing care, therapy, meals, and medical supplies — but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
This is short-term rehabilitative coverage, not a long-term custodial care benefit, and admission to the SNF generally must happen within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs in Washington include Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (planned short-term care plus counseling and training, with amounts varying by county), and DDA respite for people with a qualifying developmental disability.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 89-year-old widowed individual living in a family member's home in Adams County, needing help with bathing, mobility, and transferring, living with heart disease, on Medicare, with monthly income under $1,000 and assets under $2,000 — several Washington state and federal programs look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility. A few pieces of information are still missing (current Medicaid enrollment status, any recent hospital stay, and whether a family member is providing regular unpaid care), and getting those answers will sharpen which programs are the best fit. Please treat every item below as a lead to explore with the administering agency, not a decision about benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 89 years old with help needed for bathing, mobility, and transferring, plus monthly income under $1,000 (well below the $2,982 Special Income Level) and assets under $2,000 (at the Medicaid resource limit), this profile lines up closely with COPES's age, functional, and financial guidelines.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, community support training, environmental modifications, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.
Services can be received in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.
COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and most clients are enrolled in both CFC and COPES together.

nextSteps: Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Ask a caseworker to confirm current income and resource limits, since these figures are updated yearly.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The combination of being 89, needing help with bathing, mobility, and transferring, having income under $1,000/month, and assets under $2,000 matches the age, functional, and financial guidelines described for this program, which also serves as the financial gateway to waivers like COPES.

whatItCovers.text: This program covers nursing facility care for people meeting a nursing-facility level of care, and it is the gateway to home-and-community waivers (including COPES), PACE, and community LTSS such as CFC.
Full Apple Health Categorically Needy medical benefits come along with this long-term care coverage.
Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview, and a separate in-person CARE assessment of functional needs; both are determined together before services can start.

nextSteps: Submit application HCA 18-005 via washingtonconnection.org, or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Call the regional HCS central intake line to schedule the required CARE assessment and financial interview.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Because Medicare coverage is already in place and there is a described need for help with mobility, transferring, and bathing while living at home (not in a nursing facility), this benefit is worth checking into for at-home skilled or aide-level support.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with walking, bathing, and grooming — as long as skilled care is also being received.
Covered home health services cost $0, though durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
Eligibility requires a homebound status finding and a face-to-face provider assessment before care starts.

If appropriate, get a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 89 with income under $1,000/month (within the $3,976 limit) and assets under $2,000 (within the $84,354 limit), plus described ADL needs suggesting a nursing-facility level of care, this profile fits the general TSOA guidelines. However, statewide enrollment is currently paused, which affects how soon any support could start.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits well above regular Medicaid.

As of the most recent information, statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than processed for immediate services, and there is no announced reopening date.
People already fully enrolled or approved for presumptive eligibility before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about current TSOA waitlist status.
While waiting, ask the AAA about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, transferring, and mobility, plus IADLs like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.

Additional covered services include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, and services can be delivered at home, an adult family home, or assisted living.
CFC is often combined with COPES for extra wraparound services ("CFC+COPES").

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, this screening found five programs worth a closer look, but several important details are still missing that would make the picture clearer. Your Medicare coverage and your need for help with eating, dressing, and toileting point toward the Medicare home health benefit as a strong starting point. Other programs — Medicaid Community First Choice, Tailored Supports for Older Adults, respite care, and Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage — may also be worth exploring, but each has at least one key piece of information (such as Medicaid enrollment status, exact income, caregiver situation, or recent hospital stays) that we don't yet know. None of this means you do or don't qualify for anything — only the administering agency can make that call. Answering a few more questions below would help sharpen this screening.

To use this benefit, a provider must certify homebound status and order the care, and a Medicare-certified home health agency must deliver it.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 64, live in your own home in Clallam County, are married, have Medicare, and need help with transferring, eating, and dressing due to dementia — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. Two programs (Medicare's home health benefit and Tailored Supports for Older Adults) match well on the details we have. Several others (the Medicaid COPES waiver, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) could also be worth exploring, but key facts — your exact age relative to disability rules, your exact monthly income and asset amounts, whether you're enrolled in Washington Medicaid, whether you have a recent hospital stay, and whether your spouse is providing significant unpaid caregiving — are still missing. Because so many of these details are unanswered, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an eldercare specialist or DSHS office, not as a conclusion about what you'll receive. No agency can confirm eligibility except the one that runs the program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already enrolled in Medicare and living at home in Clallam County, and your reported needs — help with transferring, eating, and dressing — are the kind of needs this benefit is designed to support alongside skilled care, at no cost for covered services.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, dressing, feeding, walking) — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.

To qualify, a person needs a face-to-face provider assessment confirming homebound status and a need for skilled (not just personal) care, with care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency; there is no visit cap while the person continues to qualify.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or health care provider for a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 64, with your reported needs for help transferring, eating, and dressing, and income and assets that appear to fall within TSOA's relatively generous limits ($3,976/month income cap and $84,354–$156,883 asset caps depending on marital status), this program's screening criteria appear to line up well with your situation as described. TSOA is designed for people who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, which fits since Medicaid enrollment hasn't been confirmed for you.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers; it has no cost-sharing and no estate-recovery exposure of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Eligibility requires nursing-facility-level-of-care functional status, gross income at or below $3,976/month, and countable resources at or below $84,354 (single) or $156,883 (married with a community spouse), with 12-month continuous eligibility once approved.

Important: TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025. New applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and presumptive-eligibility screenings are suspended, with no announced reopening date; people already enrolled before the pause keep their services.

nextSteps: Contact washingtonconnection.org or the local Area Agency on Aging (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to be placed on the TSOA statewide waitlist and get a request date on record.
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds, which don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 64, married, living in your own home in Clallam County, and need help with transferring, eating, and dressing — needs that line up with COPES' nursing-facility-level-of-care standard, which can also be met through cognitive impairment like dementia when paired with an unmet ADL need. However, COPES eligibility also depends on your exact age/disability status, income, and assets, which we don't yet have confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver that adds services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, transportation, and community transition help on top of personal care, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care — this can include having an unmet need in 3 or more daily activities (like eating, transferring, or dressing) or having a cognitive impairment such as memory problems or disorientation combined with an unmet ADL need.

Financially, the program looks at age or disability status (65+ or 18–64 with an SSI-level disability), monthly income compared to a special income limit, and countable assets compared to Medicaid resource limits, with extra protections for a spouse still living at home.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are 65 or older, or under 65 with a confirmed disability determination under SSI criteria
Your exact monthly income compared to the program's income limit
Your exact countable assets compared to the program's resource limit
Details about your marital status and any spousal income/asset protections that might apply
nextSteps: Contact DSHS Home and Community Services to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit or review a financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office to get an official determination of income and asset eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with transferring, eating, and dressing meets the kind of substantial, multi-ADL need that Community First Choice is built around. What's missing is confirmation of whether you're already enrolled in — or could enroll in — Washington's community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage, which is the financial gateway to this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice pays for personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, for people living at home or in certain residential settings.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and financially must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in — or could become enrolled in — Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status, or apply for community Medicaid coverage if not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health long-term care is the financial doorway to waiver programs like COPES, and your need for help with transferring, eating, and dressing fits the functional side of this program. As with COPES, we don't yet know your exact age/disability status, income, or assets, which determine the financial side of eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to nursing facility care, HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS programs, administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility depends on meeting general Apple Health rules, an institutional-status test, a nursing-facility-level-of-care functional assessment, and either SSI-related or MAGI-based financial criteria, with income compared against a special income limit and, for the SSI-related pathway, a $2,000/$3,000 resource limit with spousal protections.

Applying involves a financial application plus a required interview with an HCS financial specialist and an in-person CARE functional assessment; processing is generally within 45 days (60 if a disability decision is needed).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are 65 or older, or under 65 with a confirmed SSI-criteria disability determination
Your exact monthly income compared to the special income limit
Your exact countable assets compared to the resource limit
Marital status details relevant to spousal resource protections
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office.
Request a CARE assessment through the regional HCS intake line serving Clallam County (Region 3).

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is required for this benefit, but Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage only applies after a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a doctor-ordered need for daily skilled care. whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care — including a semi-private room, meals, nursing care, therapy, and medical social services — in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying hospital stay.
Costs include no daily charge for days 1–20, a $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 figures), and full cost after day 100 within a benefit period.
