Three others (Medicare SNF coverage, Medicaid Alternative Care, and Respite Care programs) may also be relevant, but the screening is missing a couple of key facts — mainly whether there's been a recent hospital stay and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved — so those need to be confirmed before knowing if they're a good fit. None of this means eligibility has been decided; only the administering agency can make that determination. Please answer the follow-up questions below and bring this summary to a benefits counselor or the agencies listed for each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 84 (well above the 18+ minimum), needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting — three activities of daily living, which matches the level of need this program looks for — and is already covered by Apple Health (Medicaid), which is a key building block for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can pay for help with personal care tasks like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and moving around, plus related help such as meal preparation, housework, and medication management for people who also need ADL help.
It also covers relief (respite) care for caregivers, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training.

This program is generally available to adults 18 and older who are enrolled in community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) and whose assessment shows nursing-facility level of care, with an ongoing need for help in at least three ADLs to continue services.
Services can be provided in a person's own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, and people can choose their own care providers.

nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Confirm the specific type of Apple Health coverage (CN or ABP) to see if a separate financial application is needed.
Ask HCS how CFC could work alongside other services already in place, like Medicare home health.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare coverage and clear care needs (dementia, help with mobility and daily tasks) that could involve skilled or personal care support at home, which is the kind of situation this benefit is designed for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit pays for part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services, plus home health aide help — though aide help is only covered when it comes along with one of those skilled services.
There is no cost to the beneficiary for the covered home health services themselves; durable medical equipment has a separate 20% coinsurance.

To use this benefit, a doctor or other provider must certify the person is homebound and needs skilled care, based on a face-to-face assessment, and services must come from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face homebound assessment and whether skilled care is needed.
If a skilled need is identified, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Skagit County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare Part A, which is required for this benefit, but it isn't yet known whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which is a required trigger for this coverage — so it's unclear yet whether this program is currently relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility — including nursing care, therapy, meals, and medical supplies — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count).
Days 1–20 have no daily charge, days 21–100 carry a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026), and after day 100 the patient pays the full cost.
This is short-term skilled/rehab coverage, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of at least 3 days in a row (not just observation) connected to current care needs.
nextSteps: Check hospital or discharge records for a qualifying 3-day inpatient stay.
If such a stay occurred, ask the hospital discharge staff or doctor whether SNF care might be covered.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is 84 (well over the 55+ age requirement), is already on Apple Health, lives in a family member's home (not a licensed facility), and has significant ADL needs — but it's not yet known whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, which this program is specifically designed to support.

whatItCovers.text: MAC is designed to support the unpaid family caregiver of an eligible person, offering caregiver-focused benefits like respite care, housekeeping/errands, adult day care, home-delivered meals, training, and counseling — rather than direct personal care for the elder.
Benefits escalate in steps: a one-time $250 amount, then up to $500 per year, then (for 2025–2026) an average of $928 per month up to $5,568 over six months.
As of December 1, 2025, Washington has paused new enrollment in this program statewide, with a first-come, first-served waitlist; people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver (age 18 or older) providing regular care.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging / Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about current waitlist status.
Confirm whether an unpaid caregiver is involved and interested in caregiver-support services.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has dementia and needs ongoing supervision and help with daily activities, which often involves a family caregiver — but whether there is currently an unpaid caregiver providing regular care isn't yet known, and that's a key factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Several respite options exist for unpaid caregivers in Washington: Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (amount varies by county, no statewide cap), and DDA respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40+ hours per week of care, and cannot be used if the household is already receiving respite through another public program.

The Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with the amount and type of help set through a screening/assessment process by the local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask about Lifespan Respite vouchers through PAVE (253-442-3214).

followUpQuestions: Is there a family member or friend who provides regular unpaid care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient nights (not just observation) in the recent past connected to current care needs?
Is the current Apple Health coverage Categorically Needy (CN) or Alternative Benefit Plan (ABP), or a different Medicaid category?
Has a doctor described this person as 'homebound' (meaning it's hard to leave home without help or isn't medically advised)?
Is there a current need for skilled nursing or therapy (like wound care, IV therapy, or physical therapy), separate from help with daily activities?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far, several programs might be worth a closer look, but there are quite a few unknowns (like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, how many daily activities you need help with, whether anyone helps care for you unpaid, and whether you've had a recent hospital stay) that make it hard to say much more right now. This screening is a starting point, not a determination — please treat it as a list of programs to ask a professional (like your local Area Agency on Aging or a Home and Community Services intake worker) about, rather than a confirmed set of benefits. Answering the follow-up questions below would make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home in Clallam County rather than a nursing facility, and have care needs (help with toileting, stroke-related needs) that could point toward a skilled or personal care need — all three basic screening checks for this benefit came back as met.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and durable medical equipment delivered at home, generally at $0 for the covered services themselves.

Home health aide help (such as bathing, grooming, or walking assistance) is covered only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language therapy — it is not covered as stand-alone personal care.
To qualify, a doctor must certify that the person is homebound (has trouble leaving home without help, or leaving is not recommended) and needs skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether they believe you meet Medicare's homebound and skilled-care criteria
If so, ask your doctor for a referral and a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Clallam County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 66, live in your own home in Clallam County, need help with toileting, and have a stroke diagnosis — CFC is built around helping people with exactly these kinds of personal care needs. However, it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid, or how many total daily activities you need help with, both of which matter for this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like toilet use, bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care need, and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional Medicaid rules); continuing to receive CFC at reassessment requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities.
People already enrolled in qualifying Apple Health coverage don't need a separate financial application — just the functional (CARE) assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or would likely qualify for) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities (such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring) you need help with, since staying on CFC over time requires unmet need in at least three of these
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment
Ask HCS or your local Community Living Connections office whether you're already enrolled in, or might qualify for, community Medicaid

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but skilled nursing facility coverage only applies after a recent qualifying hospital stay, and it's not known whether you've had one.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy, medical social services, and related supplies in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a hospital stay.

To qualify, the person generally needs a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days (not observation status) and a doctor's order for daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for skilled nursing facility coverage
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55, and your reported monthly income ($1,000–$2,999) and countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits, and you don't appear to be currently on Medicaid — but whether your care needs meet the required nursing-facility level of care isn't yet known. Also important: TSOA has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist rather than being processed right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is aimed at people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, including those with income and resources above regular Medicaid limits, as well as their unpaid family caregivers (it also serves people without an unpaid caregiver).

Financial limits (as of 1/1/2026) are gross income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person; the program requires a functional finding of nursing-facility level of care, and there is no cost-sharing or estate recovery of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

Since December 1, 2025, enrollment has been paused statewide; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial and functional review happens once someone is called from that waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an assessment shows you need nursing-facility level of care (based on your daily activity needs)
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the TSOA waitlist and whether a functional assessment can be scheduled

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have ongoing care needs related to a stroke and help with toileting, which is the kind of situation these programs are meant to support — but respite programs are generally designed to give relief to an unpaid caregiver, and it's not known whether you have one, especially since your profile lists you as single.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, delivered through contracted agencies including in-home respite and adult day services.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) plus caregiver support groups, counseling, training, and equipment, with service levels set through a caregiver assessment; amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging.
DDA respite is only available when the care recipient has a developmental disability that generally began before age 18.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family member or friend who regularly provides you with care, since most respite programs are designed to give that caregiver a break
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options in Clallam County
If you do have an unpaid caregiver, ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment to see what level of respite support might be available

Besides help with toileting, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, walking, or transferring in and out of bed or a chair?

report.overallSummary: Based on what has been shared so far — a 69-year-old living in Clallam County with heart disease, mobility challenges, Medicare coverage, a monthly income between $3,000 and $3,999, countable assets under $2,000, and a spouse in the household — several Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. However, many of the rules that decide whether a program fits (like the exact number of daily activities needing help, the exact monthly income figure, whether there was a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved) are still unanswered. Because so many of these details are missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an official agency, not as a prediction of what will be approved. whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy), plus durable medical equipment, delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; for covered durable medical equipment, the person pays 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a health care provider must complete a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status, and order the care, which is then delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of basic personal care, such as adult day care and adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, an assessment (the CARE assessment) must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care, and available income generally needs to be at or under the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (2026 figure), with resources at or under $2,000 for a single person.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a request for a CARE assessment through the regional Home and Community Services intake line.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds waiver services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, transportation, and specialized medical equipment, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Eligibility involves both an age/disability requirement and a functional assessment showing a need at nursing-facility level of care, based on unmet needs in daily activities like transferring, bathing, or toileting.

Financial eligibility allows income up to $2,982/month, and resource limits are normally $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), but spousal impoverishment rules can protect significantly more for a community spouse, up to $162,660 (1/1/2026).

Marital status, since it affects how resources are counted for a couple

Ask an HCS or Area Agency on Aging caseworker how spousal resource protections might apply to your specific asset situation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with transferring is one of the personal-care tasks this program is designed to support. Whether it applies further depends on Medicaid enrollment status and the total number of daily activities needing help, both of which are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, medication management, and transferring, plus related supports such as personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training, in a home or community setting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 70 years old with reported monthly income under $1,000 (within the program's income threshold) and a need for help with transferring, this program's financial gateway function for long-term-care Medicaid services may be relevant. whatItCovers.text: This is Washington's Medicaid long-term care coverage, serving as the financial gateway to services like COPES, PACE, and nursing facility care, requiring both financial eligibility and a functional (nursing-facility-level-of-care) assessment.

Income eligibility allows up to $2,982/month (Special Income Level) or, above that, a Medically Needy pathway; resource limits are normally $2,000 single/$3,000 couple, with larger protections for a community spouse.

Applications can be submitted online, by mail/fax, by phone, or in person, followed by a financial interview and an in-person CARE assessment; for Eastern Washington (including Whitman County), Region 1 intake numbers apply.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 70, live in your own home in Lewis County, have Medicare, are managing dementia and mobility challenges, and have income and assets in the ranges you gave — a few Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. The Medicare home health benefit lines up well with what you've told us so far. Several other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, Tailored Supports for Older Adults, respite care, and Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit) could also be relevant, but we're missing some key details — like whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), how many daily activities you need help with, whether an unpaid caregiver helps you regularly, and whether there's been a recent hospital stay. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening much more useful, and none of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can confirm that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, need help with mobility, and live at home in Lewis County — all of which match the basic requirements this benefit is built around. It's designed for people who need part-time skilled care at home and meet Medicare's homebound and skilled-care criteria.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as help with walking, bathing, and grooming) when paired with skilled care.
There is no cost for the covered home health services themselves; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a doctor must certify — after a face-to-face assessment — that the person is homebound and needs skilled care.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a face-to-face assessment and homebound certification would apply to your situation
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Lewis County

whatItCovers.text: CFC can pay for personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, moving around, and toileting, plus related tasks such as meal preparation and housework when personal care help is also needed.
It can also cover relief (respite) care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for choosing and managing caregivers.

To use CFC, a person generally needs to already qualify for regular community Medicaid (not institutional rules) and have an assessment showing help is needed with at least three daily activities to continue receiving services.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 79, live in your own home in San Juan County, WA, need help with eating, toileting, and dressing after a stroke, are divorced with countable assets under $2,000, and are covered by both Medicare and Apple Health (Medicaid) — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. Community First Choice and the Medicare home health benefit appear to align closely with what you've described, since you're already enrolled in the right coverage and your care needs point in that direction. A few other programs (COPES, Apple Health LTC, Medicaid Alternative Care, respite care, and Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit) could also be relevant, but some key details — your monthly income, whether you've had a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether you have an unpaid family caregiver — are still unknown and would change how strongly these apply. This is an educational screening only; none of these programs can be confirmed until you apply and the administering agency reviews your full situation.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), and you need help with eating, toileting, and dressing — three ADLs — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to receive ongoing services.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.
These services can be provided in your own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, and can be combined with COPES for additional wraparound services if needed.
CFC is a Medicaid state plan benefit, meaning it isn't limited by waiver enrollment slots — anyone who meets the eligibility rules can access it.

nextSteps: Contact regional HCS central intake (800-780-7094 for San Juan County) to request a CARE assessment.
Since you're already on Apple Health, you likely won't need a new financial application — just the functional assessment paperwork.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, live at home, and need help with ADLs after a stroke, which often comes with a need for skilled nursing or therapy services delivered at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health covers part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, walking, feeding) when also receiving skilled care — at $0 cost for covered services.
To qualify, a doctor must certify a need for intermittent skilled care and that you are homebound — meaning leaving home takes considerable effort and generally requires help or isn't recommended.
It does not cover 24-hour care, home meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor to complete a face-to-face assessment and certify homebound status if home health services seem appropriate.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving San Juan County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 79, need help with eating, toileting, and dressing, and have countable assets under $2,000 — all of which line up with COPES' age and functional and resource requirements. What's missing is your monthly income, which determines whether you meet the program's income rules.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra waiver services on top of Medicaid personal care for people who need more help than personal care alone provides — including adult day care and adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing and skilled nursing services, and transportation.

These services can be received in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and are meant to add to — not replace — Medicare, Medicaid, or other insurance you already have.

COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, generally for people 65+ (or with a qualifying disability) whose needs meet a nursing-facility level of care.

informationStillNeeded: Your monthly income amount, since COPES has an income limit ($2,982/month as of 2026, with a possible pathway for higher income) that hasn't been confirmed yet.
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS office (Region 2 for San Juan County: 800-780-7094) to request a CARE assessment.
Submit the financial application (form HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or a local HCS office.
Ask about the Special Income Level and Medically Needy pathway if your income is above $2,982/month.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 79 and need help with eating, toileting, and dressing, which may point to a nursing-facility level of care, and your assets are under $2,000. whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC provides Medicaid coverage for nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and other community long-term services.

The resource limit is $2,000 for a single person, with a home equity limit of $1,130,000, and a 60-month look-back on asset transfers.

Days 1–20 have no daily cost; days 21–100 have a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100 the patient pays all costs.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared — a 90-year-old in Snohomish County living at home with a spouse, needing help with bathing and toileting, diagnosed with Parkinson's, covered by Medicare, with countable assets under $2,000 and monthly income somewhere between $1,000 and $2,999 — several Washington and federal programs look worth a closer look. Medicare home health, PACE, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) currently show all known criteria as a match, though TSOA has a statewide enrollment pause to be aware of. Medicaid COPES and Apple Health long-term care look potentially relevant but hinge on a precise monthly income figure compared to a $2,982/month threshold — the bracket given straddles that line, so this can't be sorted out yet. Community First Choice, respite care, and Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage need more details (Medicaid enrollment status, full count of daily activities needing help, caregiver situation, and any recent hospital stay) before it's clear whether they're worth pursuing. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the agencies that run these programs can determine that. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has clear care needs (help with bathing and toileting alongside a Parkinson's diagnosis) that could indicate a need for skilled or therapy-related home care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, and home health aide help with activities like bathing — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided.
To qualify, a health care provider must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care after a face-to-face assessment.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Snohomish County.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: At 90 years old and living in Snohomish County — one of the four Washington counties with PACE coverage — with care needs (bathing and toileting help) that align with a nursing-facility level of care, PACE looks worth exploring further.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, coordinated by an interdisciplinary team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Snohomish County is one of four Washington counties served by a PACE organization, though whether a specific ZIP code is covered must be checked separately, since coverage within a county isn't complete.
Enrollees receive services exclusively through the PACE provider's network and cannot separately receive hospice; no asset-transfer penalties apply to PACE.

informationStillNeeded: The specific ZIP code in Snohomish County, to confirm it falls within a PACE organization's service area.
nextSteps: Contact a Snohomish-area PACE provider (such as Providence PACE, intake (206) 320-5325) to check ZIP coverage and begin the assessment process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 90 years old, with reported income and assets well within TSOA's higher limits, and care needs suggesting a possible nursing-facility level of care, this program's criteria appear to align — but statewide enrollment is currently paused, which affects timing.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid — $3,976/month income, $84,354 in resources for a single person, and $156,883 for a couple (2026 figures).

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date, though current participants keep their services.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of personal care for people who can safely stay in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living, including adult day services, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing, environmental modifications, and transportation.

This program is meant for short-term rehab after hospitalization rather than ongoing home-based needs.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Covered services include skilled nursing, therapy, meals, and medical supplies; there's no cost for days 1–20, then a $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026 figures).

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before choosing a facility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant has an ongoing care need (help with bathing and toileting), and being married raises the possibility of a spouse providing unpaid care, but it isn't yet confirmed whether there is an unpaid caregiver or how many hours of care they provide, which these programs require.

whatItCovers.text: The Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term respite care — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility — plus caregiver support groups, counseling, training, and equipment, with amounts varying by Area Agency on Aging.

In Snohomish County, Family Caregiver Support Program respite funding has generally ranged roughly $1,000–$4,362 per year on a sliding fee scale, though this is unpublished information that should be confirmed with the local Area Agency on Aging.
Lifespan Respite vouchers of up to $1,000 per year are available to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, though funding is limited and awards aren't guaranteed.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a Family Caregiver Support Program screening.

Is the 90-year-old currently enrolled in any Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at four programs based on what was shared: a 90-year-old living alone in Jefferson County, WA, who has had a stroke and needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets in a moderate range. Because several important details are still unknown — such as whether this person is enrolled in or might qualify for Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), whether there's been a recent hospital stay of three or more nights, and whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular care — this screening is only a starting point. None of the programs below are confirmed matches; they are areas that seem worth a closer look with the administering agency. Please review the follow-up questions, as answering them will make a future screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with mobility, dressing, and toileting following a stroke) that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care — all factors this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can pay for part-time skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language therapy delivered at home, plus medical social services — at no cost to the beneficiary for these covered services.
A home health aide can help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming, but only while skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy is also being provided.

To qualify, a doctor must certify — after a face-to-face assessment — that the person is homebound and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care, and the care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

informationStillNeeded: The screening did not flag missing data for this program, but actually receiving services will require a doctor's confirmation that your family member meets Medicare's 'homebound' definition and needs skilled care.
nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor whether they would be considered homebound under Medicare's definition and whether skilled home health services would help with recovery from the stroke.
If the doctor agrees, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Jefferson County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting — three areas of daily living — which lines up with the kind of substantial, ongoing ADL need this program is built around. However, whether they are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid coverage is not yet known, and that is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for help with daily personal care tasks like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, along with related supports such as medication reminders and other health-related tasks.
It can also cover relief (respite) care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for choosing and managing personal care providers.
Services can be provided in a person's own home or other community settings, though this benefit is not meant to replace Medicare, other insurance, or informal help already in place.

To keep this kind of help going after the first review, a person generally needs an unmet or partly met need in at least three daily living activities — which matches the dressing, toileting, and mobility needs described.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is enrolled in, or might be able to enroll in, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about a CARE functional assessment.

Ask HCS how the reported income (around $4,000/month) and assets ($85,000–$160,000) would be reviewed against community Medicaid financial rules, since this determination can only be made by the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, it's not yet known whether there has been a recent hospital stay of three or more consecutive inpatient nights, which is a key requirement for this type of coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a stay in a skilled nursing facility for rehabilitation — including skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language therapy — for up to 100 days per benefit period, after a qualifying hospital stay.

To qualify, there generally needs to have been a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days (observation status does not count), with admission to a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility within about 30 days afterward.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member has had a recent inpatient hospital stay of three or more consecutive days (not observation status).
nextSteps: If there has been or will be a hospital stay, ask the hospital discharge planner whether Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage might apply.
Keep documentation of any hospital admission and discharge dates in case this becomes relevant.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs ongoing care and supervision related to the stroke and mobility limitations, which fits the kind of situation these respite programs are designed to support. It is not yet known whether an unpaid family member or friend is currently providing that regular care, which is what these programs are actually built to support (relief for the caregiver, not direct payment to the care receiver).

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year to pay for short-term relief care for an unpaid caregiver.

The Family Caregiver Support Program can arrange planned short-term care — from a few hours to a few days, at home, an adult day center, or a residential facility — along with caregiver counseling, training, and support groups; amounts vary by county's Area Agency on Aging.
These programs are generally designed to support an unpaid caregiver, such as a family member or friend, who is providing regular care — not to pay the care receiver directly.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) is providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Identify whether anyone is acting as an unpaid caregiver for your family member.

If so, contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or the local Area Agency on Aging serving Jefferson County to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program or Lifespan Respite vouchers.

followUpQuestions: Is your family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has anyone looked into whether their income and assets might fit within community Medicaid limits?
Has your family member had a hospital inpatient stay (not just observation) of three or more consecutive days recently, for example related to the stroke?
Is a family member, friend, or other unpaid person providing regular care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor said your family member is homebound and needs part-time skilled nursing or therapy at home?
Would your family member and any caregiver be open to a CARE functional assessment through Home and Community Services to look at care planning options?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 67-year-old with Parkinson's living in a family member's home in Whitman County, needing help with bathing, toileting, and transferring, covered by Medicare, and with income and assets in the ranges you gave — a few Washington programs look worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only: it does not determine eligibility. Some key facts are still unknown (whether you're enrolled in community Medicaid, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved), so the next best step is usually a conversation with the agencies below or answering the follow-up questions so a fuller picture can guide next steps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you live at home (in a family member's house) rather than in a nursing facility, and you've described care needs — help with bathing, toileting, and transferring related to Parkinson's — that suggest a skilled or personal care need worth discussing with a doctor for this Medicare benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care at home, such as wound care, injections, and monitoring of a serious illness.

It also covers physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help with bathing, walking, and grooming — though aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being received.
Covered home health services cost $0, though durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To qualify, a doctor must certify — after a face-to-face assessment — that the person is 'homebound' and needs part-time skilled care, and a Medicare-certified home health agency must deliver the care under a doctor-ordered plan.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation from a doctor that current health and mobility meet Medicare's 'homebound' definition, since that is required for this benefit.
nextSteps: Ask a doctor about a face-to-face assessment and a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Ask the agency for a list of Medicare-certified home health providers serving Whitman County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 67 and living in Whitman County, WA, and you've reported needing help with three ADLs — bathing, toileting, and transferring — which is the kind of substantial daily-care need this program is built around. Whether it's worth pursuing further depends heavily on whether you're already enrolled in (or would qualify for) regular community Medicaid coverage, which is currently unknown.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with ADLs like bathing, toileting, transferring, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus IADL help such as meal preparation and shopping when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers relief (respite) care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and training for family caregivers on managing paid providers.
Care can be provided in a home, adult family home, assisted living, or similar community setting, and can sometimes be combined with a Medicaid waiver for additional wraparound services.

To continue receiving these services at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs — a threshold your reported needs (bathing, toileting, transferring) appear to meet.

Financially, this program requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than the higher income/asset limits used for institutional Medicaid.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or would qualify for) Washington Apple Health community Medicaid coverage (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Check with DSHS/Apple Health about your current Medicaid enrollment status, or apply if you are not enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent qualifying hospital stay, and it's currently unknown whether such a stay has occurred, so it's unclear yet whether this benefit is relevant right now.

whatItCovers.text: If there has been a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period, including a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and physical, occupational, and speech therapy.
Costs for 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100.
This is short-term rehabilitation coverage, not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has since determined a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage.
Ask a doctor whether skilled nursing facility care is being recommended.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need ongoing help with several daily activities due to Parkinson's, which suggests a family member may be providing regular unpaid care — a key requirement for these programs — though this hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can pay up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief for an unpaid caregiver providing 40 or more hours a week of care, delivered through contracted agencies such as in-home respite or adult day services.

The Family Caregiver Support Program, run through local Area Agencies on Aging, offers planned short-term relief care — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility — plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling, with amounts varying by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care and supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to connect with the Area Agency on Aging serving Whitman County for a caregiver assessment.
If applying for Lifespan Respite vouchers, apply online at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or call 253-442-3214.

Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive overnight (inpatient, not observation) nights in the recent past, and if so, did a doctor recommend follow-up skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving help — for example, 40 or more hours a week?
Would a doctor currently consider you 'homebound' (meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended)?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 70, live in Garfield County, WA, in your own home, have a stroke diagnosis, need help with bathing, toileting, and mobility, are covered by Medicare and a long-term care insurance policy, and have a household income of $4,000+/month and assets of $160,000+ — the screening tool found four programs worth a closer look. One (Medicare's home health benefit) lines up closely with your current situation. The other three could be relevant but depend on a few pieces of information we don't have yet, like whether you're enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid, whether you've had a recent 3+ day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family member or friend regularly helps care for you. None of this is a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can decide that. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider bringing this report to a professional (such as an Area Agency on Aging counselor or Home and Community Services intake worker) who can help you sort out the next steps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home in Garfield County, and have reported needing help with bathing, toileting, and mobility along with a stroke diagnosis — this matches the kind of skilled and personal care need this benefit is designed to help with.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology, medical social services, and — only alongside skilled nursing or therapy — home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming.
Covered home health services generally cost $0, though durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must certify that you are homebound (have difficulty leaving home without help, or leaving isn't recommended) and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment; a Medicare-certified home health agency then delivers the care under a doctor-ordered plan.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only kind of care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or a Medicare-certified home health agency to complete the face-to-face assessment and homebound-status evaluation.
Request the list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Garfield County from your doctor.
Since you also have long-term care insurance, ask your LTC insurance carrier how it coordinates with any Medicare-covered home health services.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with bathing, toileting, and mobility — three ADLs, which matches the kind of substantial, ongoing care need this program looks for. However, this program requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid (not institutional Medicaid rules), and we don't yet know whether you're enrolled in or would qualify for that coverage, especially given your reported income and assets.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice provides personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related IADL help (such as meal prep or housework) when ADL help is also needed, health-related tasks, relief care, nurse delegation, a personal emergency response system, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

To get ongoing CFC services, an assessment must find an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and the person must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage — not the income/asset rules used for institutional Medicaid.

Applying involves the same Long-Term Services and Supports pathway as other Home and Community Services programs: a financial application (unless already on qualifying Medicaid) plus a CARE functional assessment requested through regional HCS intake.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or would be financially eligible for, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about the financial application and CARE assessment process.
Ask a Medicaid or benefits counselor to review your income and asset information against current community Medicaid financial rules, since eligibility can only be confirmed by DSHS.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a qualifying 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor-ordered need for daily skilled care, and we don't know whether you've had a recent hospital stay like that.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and medical supplies in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Costs in 2026 include no daily charge for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay (not observation status) of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and whether a doctor determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care afterward.
nextSteps: If you've had a recent hospitalization, ask the hospital discharge planner whether it meets the 3-day inpatient qualifying stay rule.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask your doctor about other options such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or long-term care insurance benefits for ongoing care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing ongoing help with bathing, toileting, and mobility, which fits the kind of ongoing care or supervision need these programs are meant to support. We don't yet know whether an unpaid family member or friend is currently providing that regular care, which is a key factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care delivered through contracted agencies, though funding is limited and awards are not guaranteed.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care (in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with the amount and availability varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, that both caregiver and care receiver live in Washington, that the family cannot afford respite on their own, and that they aren't already receiving respite through another program.
FCSP is available to unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with services tailored through a TCARE screening by a caregiver specialist, and eligibility details varying by community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular care or supervision for you, and if so, approximately how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org to connect with your local Area Agency on Aging about FCSP options.
If a family member or friend provides substantial unpaid care, ask about the Lifespan Respite Washington voucher application process (wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or 253-442-3214).

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past that led to a need for ongoing skilled nursing or therapy care?
Does a family member or friend provide unpaid, regular care or supervision for you? If so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor assessed you as 'homebound' (having difficulty leaving home without help, or leaving home not being recommended because of your health)?

It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — a 64-year-old in Cowlitz County living in a family member's home, married, with dementia and needing help with toileting, dressing, and mobility, monthly income under $1,000, countable assets under $2,000, and Medicare coverage — several Washington programs may be worth exploring further with an official agency. This is an educational screening only; it cannot determine eligibility, which only the administering agency can decide. A few key pieces of information are still missing (such as whether you have a formal SSI-criteria disability determination since you're under 65, whether you're currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether there's an unpaid family caregiver and how many hours they help, and whether there's been a recent 3+ day hospital stay). Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening much more useful and help narrow down which programs are most worth pursuing.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare and need help with mobility, dressing, and toileting, which may relate to a homebound situation. If a doctor confirms a need for part-time skilled nursing or therapy care alongside these needs, Medicare's home health benefit could apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, walking, grooming) when paired with skilled nursing or therapy — at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a doctor must certify a need for part-time or intermittent skilled care and confirm homebound status — meaning leaving home takes considerable effort or requires help.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, home meal delivery, or personal/custodial care alone if skilled care isn't also needed.

informationStillNeeded: Confirmation from a doctor that home health services are medically necessary and that homebound status criteria are met.
Whether skilled nursing or therapy care (not just help with daily activities) is currently needed.
nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment and possible certification for home health services.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Cowlitz County from your doctor.
Discuss with your doctor whether a homebound determination applies given your dementia and mobility needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are older than the program's 55+ minimum age, your monthly income under $1,000 and assets under $2,000 are well within TSOA's higher financial limits, and your dementia and help needs with toileting, dressing, and mobility may support a nursing-facility level of care finding. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist, which affects how soon services could start.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is designed to support people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers; it involves no cost-sharing and no estate recovery of the kind tied to other Medicaid long-term-care programs.

As of the most recent information, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than processed immediately, though people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Confirming your current Medicaid enrollment status will help clarify whether TSOA or a Medicaid program like COPES is the better fit, since TSOA is not available to people already on Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current TSOA waitlist status.
Ask about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds while the TSOA waitlist is in effect.
If you'd like to pursue it despite the pause, submit a TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish your place on the waitlist.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You need help with toileting, dressing, and mobility, and have a dementia diagnosis — needs that can factor into a nursing-facility level of care assessment. Your monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000 are within COPES' financial limits, and being married means spousal protections may also apply. However, COPES generally requires either being 65+ or having a documented disability under SSI criteria, and since you are 64, that part is not yet confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds extra home- and community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care, such as adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment and supplies, nursing services, and transportation.
It also covers help transitioning into community living, such as rental deposits, utility hookups, and household goods through the Community Choice Guide service.
Services can be delivered in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, but cannot duplicate what Medicare or other insurance already covers.

COPES is Washington's largest Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and typically pairs with Community First Choice personal care for people whose needs go beyond what CFC alone provides.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have a documented disability determination under Social Security (SSI) rules, since you are 64 rather than 65, which affects which eligibility pathway applies.
nextSteps: Contact your local Home and Community Services (HCS) office or call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment for nursing-facility level of care.
Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) online at washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Ask HCS about the SSI-criteria disability determination process, since you are under 65.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your needs with toileting, dressing, and mobility line up with the kind of substantial ADL support CFC is built around. CFC eligibility also depends on being enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid coverage, which hasn't been confirmed from what you've shared.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, and mobility, plus related health tasks, for people needing this level of support at home.
It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems (PERS), assistive technology, skills training, and help learning to manage personal care providers.
CFC can be combined with COPES ("CFC+COPES") for additional wraparound services when needs go beyond personal care alone.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are already enrolled in — or would qualify for — Washington's community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, since CFC eligibility depends on that.
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health / HCA about your current Medicaid enrollment status.
If not enrolled, consider submitting a financial application through washingtonconnection.org.
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS to evaluate personal care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported income under $1,000/month and assets under $2,000 fall within Apple Health LTC's financial standards, and your dementia and help needs with toileting, dressing, and mobility may support a nursing-facility level of care finding. As with COPES, this program generally requires being 65+ or meeting SSI-criteria disability rules, which hasn't been confirmed since you are 64.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial gateway into HCS waivers such as COPES.

Applying involves both a financial application (HCA 18-005, or online through Washington Connection) and a functional CARE assessment by HCS; both are worked on at the same time before services can start.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have a disability determination under SSI rules, since you are under 65, to establish which eligibility pathway applies.
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit the paper HCA 18-005 application.
Call the HCS regional intake line covering Southwest Washington (Region 3, which includes areas near Cowlitz County) to schedule a CARE assessment.
Ask about the SSI-criteria disability determination process given your age.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is a base requirement, but this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and nothing in what you've shared indicates a recent hospitalization.

whatItCovers.text: If there is a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.
Days 1–20 have no daily cost; days 21–100 involve a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026); after day 100, the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been, or will be, a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, since that is required to trigger this coverage.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for SNF coverage before admission to a nursing facility.
Keep records of hospital admission and discharge dates, since observation status does not count toward the 3-day requirement.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need ongoing help with toileting, dressing, and mobility due to dementia, which suggests there may be an unpaid caregiver involved — though this hasn't been confirmed, and it's a key factor for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care such as in-home respite or adult day services, for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours a week of care who can't afford respite on their own.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (from a few hours to a few days) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with amounts and details varying by county/Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week they provide, since this determines eligibility for these respite programs.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or your local Area Agency on Aging in Cowlitz County to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options.
Ask about completing a TCARE caregiver screening through the AAA to determine service level.
If the caregiver provides 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, consider applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher online at wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 88, live in your own home in Grays Harbor County, have Medicare, need help transferring, and have a stroke diagnosis — this screening found four programs worth a closer look. One (Medicare's home health benefit) lines up well with what you've told us so far. The other three could be worth investigating, but we're missing some key details (like whether you're enrolled in Washington's Medicaid program, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you unpaid care) that would help sharpen the picture. This is an educational screening only — it cannot tell you whether you qualify for any program. Only the agency that runs each program can make that determination. We recommend answering the follow-up questions below and then contacting the agencies listed in the next steps.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, you have a stroke diagnosis, and you need help transferring — this is the kind of situation where a doctor might order skilled nursing or therapy services delivered at home. All the screening questions we could check (Medicare enrollment, an indication of a skilled or personal care need, and living at home rather than in a facility) came back as a match.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language therapy delivered in your home when ordered by a health care provider.
It can also cover a home health aide for help with things like walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding — but only while you're also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.
There is no cost to you for the covered home health services themselves; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

To use this benefit, you need a face-to-face assessment, a provider's order and care plan, care from a Medicare-certified agency, and you generally need to meet Medicare's 'homebound' definition — trouble leaving home without help, and normally unable to leave without considerable effort.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether a face-to-face assessment and referral for home health services would make sense given your stroke history and transfer needs.
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 88, living in your own home in Grays Harbor County, and you need help transferring after a stroke — the kind of hands-on daily care this program is designed to help pay for. However, this program requires meeting community Medicaid financial rules and needing help with at least three daily activities, and we don't yet know whether you're enrolled in (or would meet the financial rules for) Washington Apple Health, or how many activities you need help with beyond transferring.

whatItCovers.text: This program can pay for personal care help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and moving around, provided in your own home or other community settings like an adult family home or assisted living.

To keep receiving this coverage over time, you generally need an assessed nursing-facility level of care and an ongoing unmet need in at least three daily activities, and you need to qualify for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than the stricter institutional financial rules.
It also can include relief care for caregivers, nurse delegation, a personal emergency response system, assistive technology, and training on managing personal care providers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or might financially qualify for) community Medicaid (Apple Health) coverage.
How many daily activities you need hands-on help with — this program generally requires help with at least three, such as bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or transferring.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask DSHS about whether your income and assets fit within community Medicaid financial limits, since this determination can only be made by the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, and a stroke can sometimes lead to a hospital stay followed by a need for rehabilitation care. This coverage is specifically tied to a recent qualifying hospital stay, and we don't yet know whether you've had one.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of care per benefit period in a skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count) and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and medical equipment and supplies used at the facility.
Costs for 2026: days 1–20 are $0 per day, days 21–100 are $217 per day, and after day 100 you pay the full cost.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital stay as a formally admitted inpatient (not just under observation) lasting 3 or more consecutive days recently — this is required for this coverage to apply.
nextSteps: If you've had a recent hospital stay, check with the hospital discharge team whether it counted as a 3-day qualifying inpatient stay.
Ask your doctor whether skilled nursing facility care is being considered as part of your recovery plan.

programs.whyThisMayApply: With a stroke diagnosis and a need for help transferring, you may need ongoing care or supervision — the kind these programs are meant to support. However, respite programs are aimed at giving relief to an unpaid family caregiver, and we don't know whether you have an unpaid caregiver helping you regularly.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers, mainly through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program, whose amount and services vary by county and caregiver situation.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours a week of care, both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington, and an inability to afford respite on their own; the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with services tailored through a local assessment.
Respite can include in-home care, adult day services, or short residential stays, along with caregiver support groups, training, and equipment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family member or friend who regularly provides you care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they help.
nextSteps: If you have a family caregiver, contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 to learn about the Family Caregiver Support Program available through your local Area Agency on Aging.
Ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers by calling 253-442-3214 or visiting wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or do you know if your income and assets might fit within Medicaid's community coverage limits?
Besides transferring, do you need hands-on help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around the house? About how many of these do you need help with?
Have you had a hospital stay as a registered inpatient (not just under observation) lasting 3 or more days in the recent past?
Do you currently leave home rarely, or only with a lot of difficulty or help (a cane, walker, wheelchair, or another person)?
Does a family member, friend, or other unpaid person regularly help take care of you or supervise you? If so, about how many hours a week?
