report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared, this 79-year-old Snohomish County resident who lives at home, needs help with mobility, eating, and bathing, and has Medicare coverage may want to look into several Washington and federal programs. Two programs (Medicare home health and PACE) look like a strong match based on the details provided. Three others (Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) could also be worth investigating, but a few key facts are still missing — whether the family member has or can get community Medicaid, whether there's been a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved. None of this is a determination of eligibility; only the administering agency can confirm that. Filling in the missing details below will make this screening more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with mobility, eating, and bathing) that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care support — all factors this benefit is built around.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as walking, bathing, and grooming) when paired with skilled care.
This benefit is for people who are homebound and need skilled, intermittent care that is ordered by a health care provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost the beneficiary $0; durable medical equipment provided through the benefit has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask the family member's doctor or health care provider about a face-to-face assessment to evaluate homebound status and the need for skilled care.
If a need is identified, ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Snohomish County.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is 79 (meeting PACE's age requirement), lives in Snohomish County (one of the four Washington counties with PACE coverage), and has care needs — mobility, eating, and bathing — consistent with needing a nursing-home level of care while still being able to live at home.

whatItCovers.text: PACE combines all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered services — including personal care, home care, adult day primary care, therapy, transportation, prescriptions, and more — coordinated by one care team.

Snohomish County is one of the four Washington counties currently served by a PACE organization, though coverage depends on the specific ZIP code, which should be checked against the DSHS PACE ZIP code list or with the organization directly.

General eligibility involves being age 55 or older (or 65+), needing a nursing-home level of care as certified by the state, living in a PACE service area, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's support.
People with Medicaid pay no monthly premium for PACE; people with Medicare only pay a monthly premium quoted by the PACE organization at intake.

nextSteps: Confirm whether the home ZIP code in Snohomish County is within a PACE organization's service area by checking the DSHS PACE ZIP code list or calling a local PACE intake line.
Contact a PACE organization (for example, Providence PACE at (206) 320-5325) to ask about the functional and financial assessment process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member needs help with three daily activities — mobility, eating, and bathing — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services. However, it's not yet known whether they are enrolled in, or would qualify for, regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, which is a required piece of this program.

whatItCovers.text: CFC can pay for help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and moving around, and with tasks like meal preparation and housework when help with daily activities is also needed.

It can also include relief care for caregivers, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and help transitioning from an institution back into the community.

To keep receiving CFC services after the first assessment, a person needs an ongoing unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities — a threshold this family member's needs appear to meet.
This program requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid coverage (not the institutional kind), separate from the functional (care needs) assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the family member is enrolled in, or would be eligible for, Washington Apple Health community Medicaid coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional intake to ask about a financial (Medicaid) application and a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or a benefits counselor whether current income and assets would allow eligibility for community Medicaid coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member has Medicare Part A, which is one requirement for this benefit. It's not yet known whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days followed by a need for daily skilled care, which is required for this coverage to apply.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, and speech therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period — but only after a qualifying 3-day-in-a-row inpatient hospital stay and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Costs in 2026 are $0/day for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100, and the full cost after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days, followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
nextSteps: If there is a hospital stay, ask hospital discharge planners whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay and whether SNF coverage may apply.
If there is no recent hospital stay, this benefit likely doesn't apply right now — but it's worth keeping in mind if hospitalization happens in the future.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member's needs with mobility, eating, and bathing suggest ongoing care or supervision is needed, which is one factor these programs consider. However, it's not yet known whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) is currently providing regular care, which is a key eligibility factor for these programs.

whatItCovers.text: The Family Caregiver Support Program can arrange short-term planned respite care — from a few hours to a few days — at home, an adult day center, or a residential facility, along with caregiver counseling, training, support groups, and one-on-one consultation.
Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year toward respite care, though funding is limited and awards are not guaranteed.
These programs are designed to support unpaid caregivers who are providing regular care to a family member, rather than the care receiver directly.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (family member or friend) is providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If there is an unpaid caregiver providing significant hours of care, ask about applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher through PAVE (253-442-3214 or getrespite@wapave.org).

followUpQuestions: Is the family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have they applied for it?
Has the family member had a hospital stay of 3 or more nights in a row recently, followed by a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Does anyone provide regular unpaid care or supervision to the family member (for example, a family member or friend), and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
What is the specific home ZIP code in Snohomish County, since PACE coverage varies by ZIP within the county?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 67, live in your own home in Kittitas County, need help with dressing, eating, and toileting, have heart disease, are a Vietnam-era veteran, are enrolled in Medicare, and have monthly income in the $3,000–$3,999 range with countable assets of $160,000 or more — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. This is an educational screening only; it does not determine eligibility for any program. Some key facts are still missing (like whether you're enrolled in Washington Medicaid, your exact net worth for VA purposes, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stays), so a few of these programs need more information before you'll know whether they're worth pursuing. Please use the follow-up questions below to gather more detail, and confirm everything directly with each program's administering agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have reported needing help with skilled or personal care tasks — all factors the rules engine confirmed as matching this benefit's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech-language therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (like bathing, dressing, and feeding) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy care.
To qualify, a person generally needs to be considered homebound and have a doctor certify the need for skilled care, with services delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 out of pocket; durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor about a face-to-face assessment to determine homebound status and need for skilled care.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with dressing, eating, and toileting — three activities of daily living — which matches CFC's requirement of an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue receiving services. However, CFC also requires being eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, and it isn't yet known whether you're enrolled in or would qualify for that coverage, especially given your reported assets of $160,000 or more.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, plus help with meals, housework, and other daily living needs, in your own home or other community settings.
To keep receiving CFC after the first assessment, a person must have an ongoing unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
CFC also covers relief care for caregivers, personal emergency response systems, nurse delegation, and training on how to select and manage caregivers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid (CN/ABP) coverage in Washington.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) regional intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment.
Ask DSHS or an Apple Health representative whether your income and assets would fit community Medicaid rules, since asset limits for that pathway are typically much lower than $160,000.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that was not dishonorable, and given that you need help with daily activities like dressing, eating, and toileting, you meet several of the clinical and service criteria the rules engine checked. However, the underlying VA pension has a net worth limit of $163,699, and your reported countable assets of $160,000 or more could be near or above that threshold, so this needs to be checked with exact figures.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance adds a monthly cash payment to a veteran's VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.
As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Aid & Attendance could receive up to $29,093 a year, compared with $17,441 a year without it.

To qualify for the underlying VA pension, net worth must be within $163,699 (effective December 1, 2025), and service must include at least one day during a recognized wartime period, such as the Vietnam era.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable net worth for VA purposes, to compare against the $163,699 limit.
nextSteps: Gather VA Form 21-2680 (to be completed by a medical examiner) and pension-related documents (SSN, VA file number, service history, income and asset details).
Contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to get an exact net worth calculation and discuss whether Aid & Attendance or another VA benefit fits better.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your Vietnam-era wartime service and discharge status match the underlying VA pension requirements checked by the rules engine. However, the Housebound benefit specifically requires being substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability — this hasn't been reported — and the same net worth question that applies to Aid & Attendance also applies here.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit adds a monthly payment to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
As of December 1, 2025, a veteran with no dependents receiving Housebound could get $21,313 a year, versus $17,441 a year standard.
A veteran cannot receive both Aid & Attendance and the Housebound benefit at the same time — only one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability.
Your exact countable net worth for VA purposes, to compare against the $163,699 limit.
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA representative whether your situation fits the Housebound criteria or Aid & Attendance criteria better, since only one can be received.
Provide net worth details as part of any VA pension application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and it isn't known whether you've had such a stay.
informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled care afterward.
nextSteps: If you are hospitalized in the future, ask the hospital discharge planner whether you have a qualifying stay for skilled nursing facility coverage.
Keep this benefit in mind as a resource if short-term rehabilitation care is needed after a hospital stay.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your reported needs for help with dressing, eating, and toileting suggest ongoing care needs, which matches one part of this program's criteria. However, it isn't yet known whether you have an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, which is a key requirement for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Respite care programs provide short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers, mainly through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to provide 40 or more hours per week of care, while FCSP eligibility and services vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are based on a caregiver assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: If a family member or friend provides regular unpaid care, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
Consider applying for Lifespan Respite vouchers if the caregiver provides 40+ hours per week and isn't already receiving respite through another program.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage?
What is your countable net worth for VA purposes (total assets minus certain exclusions), and is it above or below $163,699?
Do you spend most of your time confined to your home because of a permanent disability, or are you generally able to leave home (even with help)?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, or do you anticipate needing rehabilitation care after a hospital stay?
Do you have an unpaid family member, friend, or other caregiver who regularly helps you, and if so, about how many hours a week do they provide care?

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home, and needs help with transferring and dressing. Heart disease can also mean a need for skilled monitoring or nursing support at home, which this benefit is designed to help with.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as bathing, dressing, and walking) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven Washington programs based on the information shared: you are 87, live in Klickitat County in a family member's home, are married, have Medicare, were diagnosed with a stroke, and need help with transferring and dressing. Several important details — your exact income and asset amounts, whether you're already enrolled in Medicaid, whether you've had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone provides you with regular unpaid care — are still needed to know which of these programs are worth pursuing. Because so many of these details are missing, this report leans on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations, and none of it should be read as a decision about what you will qualify for. A benefits counselor or the administering agency for each program can confirm actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, you've shared a need for help with transferring and dressing related to a stroke, and you live in a family member's home rather than a nursing facility — these line up with the basic conditions this benefit looks at.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, and home health aide help at home when a person is considered homebound and a doctor has ordered the care.

Covered services can include skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help with things like bathing and grooming when also receiving skilled care, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only kind of care needed.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether a face-to-face assessment and homebound-status certification could be arranged.
If your doctor agrees, ask for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Klickitat County.
Ask the home health agency to explain in writing what will and won't be covered before care starts.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 87, which meets this program's age requirement, and you've shared that you need help with transferring and dressing after a stroke — the kind of care need this program's assessment looks at. However, this screening doesn't yet know your exact monthly income, exact countable assets, or how your marital status affects the financial rules, and those details determine whether the financial side of this program could apply to you.

whatItCovers.text: COPES serves people 65+ (or younger adults who meet SSI disability criteria) who are assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, and it layers extra home-and-community-based services on top of Medicaid personal care.

Covered services can include adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, provided in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Financially, the program generally looks at income against a Special Income Level (about $2,982/month as of 2026) and countable resources against Medicaid limits, though married couples have special spousal-protection rules that can allow the non-applying spouse to keep significantly more in resources (up to about $162,660 in 2026).

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income bracket
Your exact countable asset bracket
Your marital status details as they apply to Medicaid's spousal rules
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit financial application HCA 18-005 through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS to get an official read on income and asset eligibility.
Ask a benefits counselor to review your specific income, assets, and marital status against current Medicaid LTSS limits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've mentioned needing help with transferring and dressing due to a stroke, which are among the ADLs this program looks at, but this screening doesn't know whether you already have Medicaid coverage or how many total ADLs you need help with, both of which affect whether this program's rules would apply to you.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice provides Medicaid-funded personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, and requires either being enrolled in Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid or SSI-related Medicaid.
To continue receiving CFC services after the initial assessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.

Covered services include help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, transferring, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid
How many activities of daily living you need ongoing help with
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health or a benefits counselor about your current Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS intake to get a full picture of your ADL needs.
Ask HCS whether you'd be considered for CFC alongside COPES if you pursue that application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 87 with a stroke diagnosis and needs around transferring and dressing, you may fit the age and functional criteria this program's assessment considers. However, your exact income, exact assets, and how your marital status factors into the spousal rules are still unknown, and those pieces determine whether the financial side would apply.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and serves as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and institutional nursing facility care.

Financial eligibility is generally based on an income test against the Special Income Level (about $2,982/month in 2026) and a resource test (around $2,000 single/$3,000 couple), with spousal impoverishment protections letting a community spouse keep more in resources.
The application involves a financial application plus a required interview with an HCS financial specialist and an in-person CARE assessment, typically processed within 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income bracket
Your exact countable asset bracket
Details about your marital status relevant to spousal impoverishment rules
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application via washingtonconnection.org, by mail, or in person at an HCS office.
Call your regional HCS central intake line to schedule the CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS about how your spouse's income and assets would be treated under the spousal impoverishment rules.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which is one condition for this benefit, but this screening doesn't know whether you've had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay, which is a required trigger for this particular coverage.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, and admission to the SNF generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.

Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and the patient paying all costs after day 100 in a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and if so, when
nextSteps: If you're hospitalized in the future, ask the hospital discharge team whether the stay qualifies you for SNF coverage.
If there's been a recent qualifying hospital stay, ask your doctor about a referral to a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility.
If there's no recent hospital stay, ask about alternatives like the Medicare home health benefit or Medicaid long-term care options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 87 you meet the age-55-or-older requirement, and your reported needs around transferring and dressing suggest the kind of care level this program's assessment considers. Your reported assets ($85,000–$160,000, married) also appear to fall within this program's resource limits. However, your exact monthly income is still needed to check it against this program's income limit, and importantly, this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a waitlist in place.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, using income and resource limits that are higher than regular Medicaid, and it also serves unpaid family caregivers.

Financial standards (as of 2026) include income at or below about $3,976/month, and resource limits of about $84,354 for a single person or $156,883 for someone with a community spouse; the program requires a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment and generally excludes people already on Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.

As of the most recent update, new applications only place a person on a statewide first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed for immediate services, though people already enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact monthly income bracket
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist status and how to get placed on it.
If pursuing this route, submit the TSOA application (HCA 18-008) to establish a request date on the waitlist.
While waiting, ask the Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your stroke-related needs for help with transferring and dressing suggest ongoing care needs, which is one condition these respite programs look at. However, this screening doesn't yet know whether a family member or friend provides you with regular unpaid care, which is central to whether these caregiver-support programs would be relevant.

whatItCovers.text: Washington's main respite options for older adults are Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), both aimed at giving unpaid caregivers short-term relief; Lifespan vouchers require the caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care.

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP respite amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, covering things like in-home respite, adult day services, or short residential stays, plus caregiver training and support groups.
Lifespan vouchers are applied for through PAVE, while FCSP is accessed through the local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend provides you with regular unpaid care, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: If a family member is providing regular unpaid care, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to connect with the local Area Agency on Aging about FCSP.
Ask about a TCARE caregiver assessment to see what level of respite support might be available.
If interested in Lifespan Respite vouchers specifically, contact PAVE at 253-442-3214 or getrespite@wapave.org.

followUpQuestions: What is your exact monthly household income (not just the $3,000–$3,999 range) and exact countable assets (not just the $85,000–$160,000 range)? Some programs have cutoffs inside these ranges.
Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) in any category, or on a Medicare Savings Program?
Besides transferring and dressing, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, eating, toileting, walking, or medication management?
Have you had a hospital stay (admitted as an inpatient, not just observation) in the last 30 days?
Does a family member or friend regularly provide you with unpaid care, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor or nurse assessed you as 'homebound' (meaning leaving home is difficult or not recommended)?

Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 78, live in a family member's home in Skagit County, have dementia, need help with dressing, transferring, and mobility, and are covered by Medicare — a few Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. A few important details are still missing (like whether you're enrolled in Medicaid, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether a family member is providing unpaid care), so this screening is a starting point, not a final answer. None of this means you do or don't qualify for anything — only the administering agency can determine that. Please review the follow-up questions below so a fuller picture can be built.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare and need regular help with dressing, transferring, and mobility due to dementia — needs that can overlap with the skilled or personal-care services Medicare's home health benefit provides, especially if a doctor determines you need skilled care and are largely unable to leave home without assistance.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, and home health aide help (such as bathing, walking, and grooming) — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.
To qualify, a doctor must complete a face-to-face assessment and certify that the person is homebound and needs skilled care.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment used at home has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask a doctor or nurse practitioner for a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and skilled-care need.
Request a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency to discuss what services would be ordered.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 78, living in a family member's home in Skagit County, and dealing with dementia along with needs for help dressing, transferring, and moving around — this kind of substantial, ongoing personal-care need is the type Community First Choice (CFC) is built to support. However, we don't yet know whether you're enrolled in, or would qualify for, community Medicaid coverage, which this program requires.

whatItCovers.text: CFC helps pay for assistance with daily activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, and toileting, along with related tasks such as meal preparation and light housekeeping when personal care needs are also present.
It's a Washington Medicaid state plan benefit for people assessed at a nursing-facility level of care, and services can be delivered at home, in an adult family home, or other community settings.
It also covers extras like personal emergency response systems, nurse delegation, relief care, and training for managing personal care providers.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in — or would meet the financial and enrollment requirements for — community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS or a benefits counselor how your income and assets would be evaluated for community Medicaid eligibility, since CFC requires that coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, which this benefit requires, but the profile doesn't mention a recent hospital stay. Skilled nursing facility coverage only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three days, so this program may become relevant only if that kind of hospitalization has happened or happens in the future.

whatItCovers.text: After a qualifying 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay, Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of care in a skilled nursing facility, including nursing care, therapy, meals, and a semi-private room.
Costs are $0 per day for days 1–20, then a daily coinsurance ($217/day in 2026) for days 21–100; after 100 days, the person pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been (or will be) a hospital inpatient stay of three or more consecutive days, and whether a doctor determined daily skilled care was needed afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies for Medicare-covered skilled nursing facility care.
If there's no qualifying hospital stay, consider looking at Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits as alternatives for ongoing care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Living with dementia and needing help with dressing, transferring, and mobility while residing in a family member's home often means a family caregiver is providing regular support — a situation these respite programs are designed to help with. We don't yet know if there is an unpaid caregiver in this situation or how many hours of care they provide.

whatItCovers.text: The Family Caregiver Support Program offers short-term relief care — from a few hours to a few days — provided in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility, along with caregiver training, support groups, and counseling for unpaid caregivers of adults 18 and older.
Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40 or more hours of care per week who can't afford respite on their own.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid caregiving, and roughly how many hours per week they spend on it.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 to ask the local Area Agency on Aging about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If interested in Lifespan Respite vouchers, apply through wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or call 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you looked into whether your income and assets would meet community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) limits?
Have you had a hospital inpatient stay of three or more days recently, or is one expected, and did a doctor say you'd need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Does a family member or friend regularly provide unpaid caregiving help, and if so, about how many hours per week do they spend on it?
Has a doctor said that leaving your home is difficult or not recommended because of your health condition (this is called 'homebound status')?

It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Thank you for sharing details about your 80-year-old family member in Cowlitz County, who lives with family, has Parkinson's disease, needs help with toileting, eating, and mobility, and is covered by Medicare. Based on this profile, four programs may be worth a closer look: Washington's Medicaid Community First Choice program, the Medicare home health benefit, Medicare's skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs for family caregivers. None of these programs have been confirmed as a fit — only the agency that runs each program can make that determination. A few key pieces of information (such as Medicaid enrollment status, any recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is providing regular care) are still missing and would help sharpen this screening. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider reaching out to the programs' official contacts to learn more.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with eating, toileting, and mobility) that could relate to the kind of skilled or personal care this benefit supports.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment, at no cost for the covered services themselves (20% coinsurance applies to DME after the Part B deductible).

Home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — it does not cover personal care alone or 24-hour care.

To use this benefit, a person generally needs to be considered homebound and have a health care provider certify the need for skilled care, ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor whether a face-to-face assessment and referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency would be appropriate.
Ask the doctor to evaluate and document homebound status if home health services are being considered.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs help with toileting, eating, and mobility — three ADLs, which lines up with the level of need this program is designed for. However, whether they are enrolled in (or would qualify for) community Medicaid, which is required for this program, is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with ADLs such as toilet use, eating, transfers, bed mobility, and walking, as well as IADLs like meal prep and shopping, plus health-related tasks.

It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and help managing personal care providers, and can be provided in a home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financial eligibility requires qualifying for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is already enrolled in — or might qualify for — Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage, since this program requires that coverage.
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about the CARE assessment process.
Ask about whether a Medicaid financial application is needed, or whether current Apple Health enrollment already covers this step.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, which is a starting requirement for this benefit. Whether they have had a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay — required for this coverage — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay — but it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Covered services include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy and speech-language pathology, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transportation.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member has had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify as the required 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage.
Ask the doctor about whether daily skilled nursing or therapy care might be needed after any hospital stay.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member lives in a family member's home and needs ongoing help with toileting, eating, and mobility due to Parkinson's disease, which suggests there may be a family caregiver providing regular support — though this has not been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care delivered through contracted agencies, including in-home respite and adult day services, though vouchers are limited and not guaranteed.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days — in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential care facility — along with support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40 or more hours per week of care, with both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington; FCSP is available to unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with services tailored through a caregiver specialist's assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week that care is provided.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections at 1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program through the local Area Agency on Aging.
Contact PAVE (253-442-3214 or getrespite@wapave.org) to ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers.

followUpQuestions: Is your family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) been submitted?
Has your family member had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days in the recent past?
Is there an unpaid family caregiver (for example, the relative whose home they live in) providing regular care or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has a doctor determined that your family member is homebound and needs part-time or intermittent skilled nursing or therapy care at home?

Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven Washington-state programs based on the profile shared: a 69-year-old Vietnam-era veteran in Grant County who needs help with bathing and eating, lives in a family member's home, has Medicare and long-term care insurance, and has income and assets in the ranges given. Two programs — VA Aid & Attendance and the Medicare home health benefit — look like strong candidates worth a closer look because the available facts line up closely with what those programs consider. A few other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, VA Housebound, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) could also be worth investigating, but there isn't yet enough information to know how well they fit — mainly around Medicaid enrollment, the exact number of daily activities needing help, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. None of this is a determination of eligibility for any program — only the administering agency (VA, DSHS/Home and Community Services, Medicare, or the local Area Agency on Aging) can make that call. Answering the follow-up questions below would sharpen this picture before contacting an agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is a Vietnam-era veteran, needs another person's help with daily activities (bathing and eating were mentioned), and countable assets in the $85,000–$160,000 range appear to fall under the VA's net worth limit of $163,699. All the rule checks the screening ran — veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, need for help with daily activities, and net worth — came back as met, which is why this program stands out as a strong one to look into.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans (or some survivors) who need help with daily activities, and it can be used at the household's discretion for care costs.

To qualify for the underlying pension, service must include at least one day during a recognized wartime period — the Vietnam era (8/5/1964–5/7/1975, or earlier for in-country service) is one of the recognized periods — along with a discharge that was not dishonorable and net worth within the VA's limit ($163,699 effective December 1, 2025).
On top of the underlying pension, at least one Aid & Attendance clinical criterion must apply, including needing another person's help with activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

nextSteps: Gather VA file number, military service history, income and asset documentation, and unreimbursed medical/care expense records.
Ask a physician or qualified examiner to complete VA Form 21-2680.
Submit VA Form 21P-527EZ (and 21-2680) to start the pension and Aid & Attendance claim, or work with an accredited Veterans Service Officer for help with the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare and needs help with activities like bathing and eating, and the screening's checks for Medicare enrollment, an indication of skilled or personal care need, and living at home (not a nursing facility) all came back as met — making this a program worth exploring with a home health agency or physician.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide help with activities like bathing and grooming — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech-language therapy is also being received.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home is difficult or not recommended), have a face-to-face assessment by a provider, and have care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; only durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Ask a primary care provider for a face-to-face assessment and, if appropriate, a homebound-status certification.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grant County from the provider.
Share the existing long-term care insurance and Medicare information with the agency so they can coordinate coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile notes needs with bathing and eating, which are the kind of activities Community First Choice covers, and the family lives in Washington (Grant County). However, it's not yet known whether this person is enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid, and only two ADL needs were mentioned so far — CFC generally requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs to continue at reassessment.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs such as bathing, eating, dressing, and other daily activities, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and qualify for community Medicaid.

Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (not institutional financial rules), and functionally it requires a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, with at least three ADLs needing help to continue at reassessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is enrolled in, or could qualify for, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).
The total number of daily activities (ADLs) this person needs help with.
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a CARE assessment.
If not already on Apple Health, discuss a Medicaid financial application with HCS or a DSHS caseworker.
Ask HCS how CFC would interact with the existing long-term care insurance coverage.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, and net worth all came back as meeting the VA's general pension criteria in this screening. However, whether this person is substantially confined to the home by a permanent disability is unknown, and the Housebound benefit cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — since Aid & Attendance appears to be a stronger match based on the reported need for help with bathing and eating, Housebound is likely a lower priority to pursue.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, and it cannot be paid together with Aid & Attendance — a claimant receives one or the other.

The clinical standard is spending most of one's time at home because of a permanent disability, with a more detailed regulatory definition involving either an additional 60%-or-more disability rating or being substantially confined to one's dwelling on a lasting basis.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is substantially confined to home because of a permanent disability.
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound is the better fit, since only one can be received.
If pursuing Housebound, ask a medical examiner to document housebound status on VA Form 21-2680.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is enrolled in Medicare, which is one requirement for this benefit, but it's unknown whether there has been a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — which is required for this benefit to apply. Based on what's known so far (help needed with bathing and eating, living in a family member's home), this looks less likely to be relevant unless a hospitalization has occurred or is anticipated.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying inpatient hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026), and full patient responsibility after day 100 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay.
Keep long-term care insurance and Medicare information handy in case day 21+ coinsurance needs a secondary payer.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile indicates this person needs ongoing help with daily activities and lives in a family member's home, which suggests a family caregiver may be involved — but it isn't yet confirmed whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is a key requirement for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), generally for caregivers of adults who need care; a third channel (DDA respite) applies only when the care receiver has a developmental disability that generally began before age 18.

Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, subject to available funding, while FCSP respite amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging and are tailored through a caregiver assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) is providing regular care, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program through the local Area Agency on Aging serving Grant County.
If a family caregiver is providing 40+ hours per week, ask about Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers via wapave.org or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Is this person currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or in a Categorically Needy/Alternative Benefit Plan version of it?
How many daily activities does this person need help with in total — for example bathing, eating, dressing, toileting, transferring, or walking?
Is someone (a family member or friend) providing unpaid care on a regular basis, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more days in the recent past?
Is this person substantially confined to the home because of a permanent disability, or able to leave home fairly easily?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at six programs for a 76-year-old in Douglas County, WA, who lives at home, is married, has Medicare, and needs help with eating, dressing, and toileting after a stroke. Because Medicare eligibility and daily care needs are clear, the Medicare home health benefit stands out as a strong first program to look into. Several Medicaid-based programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care) may also be worth exploring because of the age and care needs described, but the stated income ($4,000+/month) and assets ($160,000+) are close to or above the usual limits for those programs, so it isn't possible to tell from this information alone whether the financial side would work out — especially since special rules can protect income and assets for a spouse. Two more programs (Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage and respite care) depend on details not yet provided, like a recent hospital stay or an unpaid caregiver's hours. None of this means the family does or doesn't qualify for anything — only the administering agency can make that call. Getting a few more details would sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, and needs help with eating, dressing, and toileting following a stroke — needs that often involve skilled nursing or therapy services this benefit is designed to support.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (when also receiving skilled care), at no cost to the beneficiary for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a person generally needs a doctor-certified need for part-time skilled care, homebound status, a face-to-face assessment, and care delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary care provider about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and skilled care needs
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County
Discuss with the doctor which specific skilled services (nursing, PT, OT) might be ordered

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 76 with a stroke diagnosis and needing help with eating, dressing, and toileting, this person may meet the age and functional (nursing-facility level of care) criteria that COPES looks at. However, the stated monthly income of $4,000+ and countable assets of $160,000+ are close to or above the program's usual financial thresholds, and since the family is married, spousal protection rules could change how those numbers are counted — so it isn't clear from this information whether the financial side would line up.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care and adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing and skilled nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and specialized medical equipment, provided in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be age 65+ (or meet a disability standard) and have a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, which can include needing substantial help with activities like eating, toileting, or dressing.

Financial eligibility for the standard pathway generally requires monthly income at or below about $2,982 (2026) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, though special spousal impoverishment rules can protect additional income and resources for a spouse still living at home.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, rather than an open-ended bracket
The exact countable asset amount, rather than an open-ended bracket
Confirmation of marital status details relevant to spousal resource protections
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS office to request a CARE functional assessment
Gather detailed income and asset documentation for both spouses
Ask HCS about spousal impoverishment protections given the marital status

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with eating, dressing, and toileting is three activities of daily living, which matches the substantial ADL need CFC looks for at reassessment. However, CFC also requires being eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage, and this family's Medicaid enrollment status wasn't provided — only Medicare coverage was mentioned.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and medication management, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in the home or other community settings.

To keep receiving CFC services at reassessment, a person needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living, and generally must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional financial rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether either spouse is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid community coverage
nextSteps: Check current Medicaid/Apple Health enrollment status
If not enrolled, ask HCA or HCS about applying for community Medicaid coverage
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document ADL needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway for Medicaid long-term care services, and the age (76) and functional needs (help with eating, dressing, toileting after a stroke) may align with the program's functional criteria. As with COPES, the stated income ($4,000+/month) and assets ($160,000+) are near or above the usual limits, and being married brings spousal protection rules into play that could change the picture, so financial fit isn't clear from what's known.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is the Medicaid financial gateway that supports programs like COPES, and functional eligibility is based on a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care.

Financial eligibility generally requires income at or below about $2,982/month (2026) for the standard pathway, or meeting a Medically Needy spend-down test if income is higher; resource limits are $2,000 for a single person or $3,000 for a couple, with special protections available for a spouse living in the community.
Applying involves both a financial application (such as HCA 18-005 or online at washingtonconnection.org) and a functional CARE assessment arranged through HCS.

informationStillNeeded: The exact monthly income amount, rather than an open-ended bracket
The exact countable asset amount, rather than an open-ended bracket
Confirmation of marital status details relevant to spousal resource protections
nextSteps: Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) if pursuing this pathway
Request a CARE assessment through HCS
Ask about Medically Needy or spousal impoverishment options given the higher income and assets

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A enrollment is confirmed, which is one requirement for this benefit. However, whether there has been a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care — a required condition for this coverage — wasn't part of the information provided.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

This benefit requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care is still needed
nextSteps: If there was a recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay
Keep documentation of any hospital admission and discharge dates
If no recent hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described stroke-related needs (help with eating, dressing, and toileting) suggest ongoing care or supervision is needed, which is one factor these respite programs consider. Since the family is married, a spouse could potentially be providing unpaid care, but whether that's the case — and how many hours per week — wasn't provided.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs such as Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year), the Family Caregiver Support Program, and DDA respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability) offer short-term relief for unpaid caregivers through in-home care, adult day services, or short residential stays.

Eligibility generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — for example, Lifespan Respite vouchers require 40+ hours per week of unpaid care — for a person needing ongoing supervision or care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing unpaid caregiving on a regular basis, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss caregiver support options
If a spouse or family member is providing regular unpaid care, ask about a TCARE screening through the Family Caregiver Support Program

followUpQuestions: What is the household's exact monthly income (a specific number rather than '$4,000 or more') and how does it break down between spouses?
What is the exact total of countable assets (a specific number rather than '$160,000 or more'), and how much of that is titled to each spouse?
Is either spouse currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage, such as a Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and if so, is there an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care?
Is a family member, friend, or other unpaid person providing regular caregiving, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 66, live in Douglas County, WA, in a family member's home, have heart disease, need help with bathing and transferring, are covered by Medicare, and are single with income between $3,000–$3,999/month and assets of $160,000 or more — one program (Medicare's home health benefit) looks like a strong match to look into right away, since you meet the basic Medicare and at-home care criteria described in the program materials. The other three programs may or may not apply, but we're missing some key details: whether you're enrolled in Washington's community Medicaid program, how many activities of daily living you need help with, whether you've had a recent qualifying hospital stay, and whether a family member or friend is providing unpaid caregiving for you. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening much more useful. Remember: none of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare, live at home (in a family member's house) rather than in a nursing facility, and need help with bathing and transferring — needs this Medicare benefit is designed to help with when paired with skilled care. Your heart disease diagnosis may also mean you need skilled monitoring, which this benefit can cover when ordered by a health care provider.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, including monitoring of unstable health conditions, along with physical, occupational, and speech therapy when needed.
Home health aide help with bathing, grooming, and similar personal care is covered, but only while you are also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.
Covered home health services cost $0 to the beneficiary; durable medical equipment involves a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a person must be considered homebound and have a provider certify the need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers you 'homebound' under Medicare's definition (difficulty leaving home without help, and leaving takes considerable effort)
Whether a provider has done or can do a face-to-face assessment and order skilled home health services for you
nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a home health referral and face-to-face assessment make sense given your heart disease and mobility needs
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County
Review the written notice from any agency about what Medicare will and won't cover before services start

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned needing help with bathing and transferring, which are among the personal care needs this Washington Medicaid program is designed to support, and you live in Douglas County, WA. However, we don't yet know whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, or how many total activities of daily living you need help with — both are needed to see whether this program is worth investigating further.

whatItCovers.text: This program can cover personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and toileting, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

To keep receiving this benefit at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and must qualify for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.
Services can be provided in a home, adult family home, or similar community settings, and are meant to work alongside — not replace — Medicare or other insurance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could qualify for) Washington's community Medicaid program (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many activities of daily living (ADLs) you need help with, since continuing eligibility generally requires needing help with at least three
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment
Ask a Medicaid specialist whether your income and asset situation could fit within community Medicaid rules
Confirm your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status before applying

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are enrolled in Medicare (which includes Part A coverage referenced in this benefit), and your heart disease diagnosis means a hospital stay could become relevant at some point. This benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and we don't currently have information about whether you've had such a stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and therapy services in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.
There is no cost for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance of $217 for days 21–100 (2026), and full cost to the patient after day 100 in a benefit period.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
Whether a doctor has determined you need daily skilled nursing or therapy care following such a stay
nextSteps: If you are hospitalized, ask hospital discharge staff whether your stay would qualify for SNF coverage
Keep track of admission and discharge dates and whether you were formally 'admitted' versus under observation status

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need help with bathing and transferring, which suggests you may have an unpaid caregiver helping you regularly. These respite programs are designed to give relief to unpaid family caregivers, but we don't yet know whether someone is providing that unpaid care and how many hours per week they spend doing so.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, delivered through contracted agencies statewide, for caregivers providing 40+ hours of unpaid care per week.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care plus support groups, counseling, training, and supplies for unpaid caregivers of adults 18+, with services and amounts tailored through a local caregiver specialist assessment and varying by Area Agency on Aging.
Applications generally rely on self-reported information, such as certifying that the caregiver provides 40+ hours of care per week for Lifespan vouchers, or completing a TCARE screening for FCSP.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver, such as a family member, is providing regular care or supervision for you
About how many hours per week that caregiver spends providing care
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options available in Douglas County
Ask about a TCARE screening/assessment through the local Area Agency on Aging for the Family Caregiver Support Program
If a caregiver provides 40+ hours per week, look into the Lifespan Respite Washington voucher application

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied for it?
Besides bathing and transferring, do you need help with other daily activities such as dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around the home?
Have you had an inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more days in a row recently, and if so, did a doctor say you need daily skilled nursing or therapy afterward?
Is a family member or friend currently providing regular unpaid care or supervision for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has a doctor indicated that leaving your home is difficult or not recommended because of your health condition?
