report.overallSummary: Based on what you shared, we found four programs worth a closer look for this 78-year-old individual in Franklin County who is living with Parkinson's disease and needs help with dressing and mobility. Because you have Medicare and long-term care insurance, and because some key details (like current Medicaid enrollment, the full list of daily activities needing help, any recent hospital stays, and whether a family member is providing regular unpaid care) are still unknown, this screening is a starting point, not a determination of eligibility. Only the agency that runs each program can decide who qualifies. A few follow-up questions below could help sharpen which of these programs are most worth pursuing.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, live in a family member's home (not a nursing facility), and have a Parkinson's diagnosis along with needs for help with dressing and mobility — all of which line up with the basic building blocks of this benefit (Medicare enrollment, a care need, and living at home).

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language services delivered at home by a Medicare-certified agency, generally at no cost to you for the covered services themselves.
It can also include home health aide help with things like walking, bathing, and grooming, but only while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided — not as personal care alone.
To qualify, a person generally needs to be considered homebound and have a face-to-face assessment and a doctor's order certifying the need for skilled care.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, homemaker services unrelated to the care plan, or custodial/personal care when that is the only type of care needed.

nextSteps: Talk with a doctor about whether homebound status and a skilled care need (such as therapy related to Parkinson's or mobility issues) could be certified.
If a need is identified, ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You mentioned needing help with dressing and mobility due to Parkinson's disease, which are two of the daily activities this program is designed to help with. However, Community First Choice generally requires enrollment in community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) and an unmet need in at least three activities of daily living to continue receiving services — and both your current Medicaid enrollment status and your full ADL needs are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: This program can help pay for personal care assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and mobility, as well as related tasks like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It also covers things like relief (respite) care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family members who help manage care.

To keep receiving this program's help over time, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and must be eligible for regular community Medicaid coverage rather than institutional Medicaid rules.
Services can be delivered in a home, adult family home, or assisted living setting, and are meant to add to — not replace — Medicare, other insurance, or informal help from family.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
Whether you need help with three or more daily activities (beyond dressing and mobility)
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional intake to ask about a CARE assessment.
Check with DSHS or an Apple Health caseworker about current Medicaid enrollment status, since countable assets and income can affect financial eligibility for this pathway.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare coverage, which is one requirement for this benefit, but it generally only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row — and whether that has happened recently is not yet known. This program is meant for short-term rehabilitation after a hospitalization rather than ongoing help with dressing or mobility at home.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, or speech therapy in a skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.

To qualify, a person needs a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not observation status), admission to the skilled nursing facility within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Coverage costs nothing for days 1–20, then a daily coinsurance applies for days 21–100, after which the person pays all costs; this is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: If a hospitalization occurs or has recently occurred, ask hospital discharge staff whether the stay qualifies for Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage.
Keep hospital discharge paperwork on hand in case it's needed to show a qualifying stay.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You live in a family member's home and need help with dressing and mobility due to Parkinson's disease, which suggests a family member may be providing regular care — but whether there is an unpaid caregiver, and how many hours per week they provide care, is not yet known. Respite programs are designed to give such caregivers short-term relief.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers a few respite options for unpaid family caregivers, including Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which can pay for short-term in-home or facility-based relief care.
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, generally for caregivers providing 40 or more hours of unpaid care per week who could not otherwise afford respite.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in a residential facility, along with caregiver support groups, counseling, training, and supplies; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing unpaid caregiving or supervision on a regular basis, and roughly how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about a caregiver assessment (TCARE) and available respite funding in Franklin County.
If applicable, have the caregiver complete the Lifespan Respite Washington application to see what may be available.

followUpQuestions: Are you currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or have you applied recently?
Besides dressing and mobility, do you need regular help with other daily activities such as bathing, eating, toileting, or moving around the house?
Has there been a hospital stay of 3 or more days in a row (as an admitted inpatient, not just observation) in the recent past?
Is a family member or friend providing regular unpaid caregiving or supervision, and if so, roughly how many hours per week?
report.disclaimer: This screening is educational only. It identifies programs that appear worth investigating based on the information provided — it does not determine eligibility, provide legal or financial advice, or estimate benefit amounts. Program rules and dollar limits change; verify current details with the program's official contacts before making decisions, and consider consulting an elder-law attorney, benefits counselor, or the Community Living Connections line (1-855-567-0252).

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at six programs that might be worth exploring for a 73-year-old veteran in Clark County who has dementia, needs help with eating and dressing, lives in a family member's home, and is covered by Medicare. Because several key details are still missing — such as Medicaid enrollment status, exact countable assets compared to the VA's limit, whether an unpaid caregiver is involved, and recent hospital stays — this report should be treated as a starting point rather than a final answer. Nothing here means you qualify for any program; only the agency that runs each program can make that determination. The follow-up questions below will help narrow things down before contacting an agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are covered by Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have care needs (help with eating and dressing, plus a dementia diagnosis) that suggest a skilled or personal care need — all of which line up with what's required for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as bathing and dressing) when it's provided alongside skilled nursing or therapy services.
It also covers durable medical equipment for home use, and covered services generally cost $0 out of pocket.

To qualify, a person must be homebound (leaving home is difficult or not recommended), need part-time or intermittent skilled care, have a face-to-face provider assessment, and receive care ordered by a provider through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether a face-to-face assessment for home health services would be appropriate.
Ask your doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency serving Clark County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've shared that you need help with eating and dressing and have a dementia diagnosis, which are the kinds of needs this program is designed around. However, whether this program could apply also depends on being enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid and having at least three ADLs with unmet or partially met need — both of which are currently unknown for your situation, and your reported countable assets of $160,000 or more may be relevant to Medicaid's financial rules.

whatItCovers.text: This program can cover personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and medication management, along with related help such as meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
It can also include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family caregivers.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and — to continue receiving services — an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, along with financial eligibility for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage).

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in or eligible for community Washington Apple Health (Medicaid)
How many daily activities (ADLs) you need help with
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE assessment.
Ask about applying for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) if you are not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a Vietnam-era veteran with a discharge that was not dishonorable, and someone who needs help with daily activities like eating and dressing, you meet several of the listed criteria for this benefit. The remaining open question is whether your net worth falls within the VA's pension limit of $163,699 — you reported countable assets of $160,000 or more, which is close to this threshold, so it's worth checking your exact figure.

whatItCovers.text: This is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing; the money can be used for care costs at the household's discretion.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Aid & Attendance is $29,093 (effective December 1, 2025), compared to $17,441 without it.

Eligibility depends on the underlying VA pension, which requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, wartime service (Vietnam era service between August 5, 1964 and May 7, 1975 counts), and net worth within $163,699 as of December 1, 2025.

informationStillNeeded: Your exact countable net worth compared to the VA's $163,699 limit
nextSteps: Contact the VA or an accredited Veterans Service Officer to discuss your net worth and pension eligibility.
Gather military service records, income documentation, and a medical examiner's completed VA Form 21-2680 if you decide to apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service, and discharge status align with what's listed for this benefit, but it's unclear whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability, and your net worth relative to the $163,699 limit is also unconfirmed. Note that this benefit and VA Aid & Attendance cannot both be received at once — only one applies.

whatItCovers.text: This is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.
For a veteran with no dependents, the maximum annual pension rate with Housebound status is $21,313 (effective December 1, 2025), compared to $17,441 without it.
Eligibility also depends on the same underlying VA pension rules described for Aid & Attendance, including the net worth limit of $163,699.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability
Your exact countable net worth compared to the VA's $163,699 limit
nextSteps: Discuss with a VA-accredited representative whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance is a better fit, since only one can be received.
If pursuing this benefit, arrange a medical examination documenting housebound status using VA Form 21-2680.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, but it's unknown whether you've had a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, which is a requirement for this specific Medicare benefit. This program is generally for short-term rehabilitation after a hospitalization, not ongoing custodial care.

whatItCovers.text: This benefit can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, or speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay.
Coverage is limited to 100 days per benefit period, with no charge for days 1–20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21–100.
It is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it applies to short-term rehabilitation needs following hospitalization.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have had a recent 3-day (or longer) inpatient hospital stay
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay.
Keep this program in mind mainly for short-term rehabilitation needs, not everyday help at home.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your dementia diagnosis and need for ongoing supervision and help with daily activities suggest a caregiver may be involved, but it's not yet confirmed whether someone is providing unpaid care for you regularly, which is a key requirement for these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: These programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through options like Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and (for developmental disabilities only) DDA waiver respite.
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP amounts vary by county and Area Agency on Aging, with no statewide dollar cap published.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — an 87-year-old living alone in Skagit County with Parkinson's disease, needing help with toileting, and covered by Medicare — a few Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. Medicare's home health benefit looks like a strong match to explore because Medicare coverage, a personal/skilled care need, and living at home are all already confirmed. Three other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage, and respite care programs) could also be relevant, but each depends on details we don't have yet — such as Medicaid enrollment status, the full list of daily activities needing help, whether there's a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved. None of this is a determination of eligibility for any program; only the administering agency can make that call. Answering the follow-up questions below will help sharpen which of these are worth pursuing first.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, live at home in Skagit County, and have a diagnosed condition (Parkinson's) along with a personal care need (help with toileting) — all factors this benefit is built around. This program looks like one of the more directly applicable options to explore given what's already known about your situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology services.
It can also cover part-time home health aide help with walking, bathing, grooming, and feeding, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.
To use this benefit, a doctor must do a face-to-face assessment, certify homebound status, and order the care, which is then delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment used at home costs 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.

nextSteps: Talk with your doctor about whether a face-to-face assessment and homebound-status certification would apply to your situation
Ask your doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Skagit County if this seems worth pursuing

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're 87 and live in Skagit County with Parkinson's disease and a need for help with toileting, which is one of the activities this program helps with. However, Community First Choice generally requires being enrolled in — or eligible for — community Medicaid (not just Medicare), and it's not yet known whether you're enrolled in or eligible for Washington Apple Health, or exactly how many daily activities you need help with.

whatItCovers.text: CFC provides personal care help with daily activities including bathing, dressing, eating, and toilet use, plus support with tasks like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.
To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities (ADLs).
Financial eligibility for CFC is based on community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rules rather than the higher institutional Medicaid limits.

Services can be provided in a person's own home, and CFC is arranged through Home and Community Services after a financial application (if not already on qualifying Medicaid) and a CARE functional assessment.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or might qualify for, community Medicaid (CN/ABP) in Washington
How many daily activities (ADLs) you need help with, since ongoing CFC generally requires help with at least three
nextSteps: Contact Washington Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to ask about a financial and functional (CARE) assessment
Ask HCS or a benefits counselor whether your income and assets would fit community Medicaid rules, since these differ from Medicare

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this coverage only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a need for daily skilled care, and it isn't yet known whether you've had a qualifying hospital stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, and physical, occupational, or speech therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period.

This coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (not counting observation status) before SNF admission, generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital.
In 2026, days 1–20 cost $0, days 21–100 cost $217/day in coinsurance, and the person pays all costs after day 100 in a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days followed by a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask the hospital discharge staff or doctor whether it would qualify you for Medicare SNF coverage before choosing a facility

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need related to Parkinson's disease and help with toileting, which fits the general idea of needing regular care or supervision. whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for short-term relief care, for unpaid caregivers providing 40 or more hours per week of care.

The Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential care facility) plus caregiver training, support groups, and counseling, with funding amounts varying by county and no statewide cap.
These programs are generally intended to support the unpaid caregiver rather than to pay for care for the person receiving care directly.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week they provide
nextSteps: If an unpaid caregiver is or becomes involved, contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the Family Caregiver Support Program or Lifespan Respite vouchers for the Skagit County area

Besides toileting, do you need help with other daily activities such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, or walking?
Is there a family member or friend who provides unpaid, regular care or supervision for you, and if so, about how many hours per week?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at six programs based on what you shared: you are 67, live in your own home in Ferry County, have Parkinson's and need help dressing, are married, have Medicare, and have a monthly income of $4,000+ and countable assets between $85,000 and $160,000. Several programs look worth a closer look, especially the Medicare home health benefit, since you already have Medicare and live at home. For the Medicaid-related programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health long-term care), your age fits the basic starting point, but key details are still missing — especially how many daily activities you need help with beyond dressing, your exact income and asset figures compared to program limits, and whether a spouse or other unpaid person is providing regular care. Because several important pieces are unknown, please treat this as a starting point for further questions rather than a conclusion about what you'd receive — only the administering agencies can determine actual eligibility. Answering the follow-up questions below, or requesting a formal assessment from Home and Community Services, would sharpen this picture considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare, live at home rather than a nursing facility, and have a documented care need (help with dressing) along with a diagnosis of Parkinson's, which often involves therapy needs. These are the general starting points this benefit looks for, though it also requires a doctor to certify homebound status and a need for skilled (not just personal) care.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (such as bathing and dressing assistance) when also receiving skilled care — all typically at no cost for covered services.

Eligibility requires a face-to-face assessment and certification that the person is homebound (has real difficulty leaving home without help, or leaving isn't recommended) and needs skilled, provider-ordered care from a Medicare-certified agency.
Full custodial or personal care alone, without an accompanying skilled care need, is not covered.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor would certify homebound status
Whether there is a specific skilled care need (such as nursing or therapy), not just help with daily activities like dressing
nextSteps: Talk with the primary care provider about a possible home health referral and face-to-face assessment
Ask the doctor's office for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Ferry County

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 67, which meets the age 65+ starting point for COPES. You mentioned needing help with dressing and living with a diagnosis of Parkinson's, which could relate to the kind of daily-activity help COPES is meant to support, and you are married, which matters because COPES has special financial protections for couples. However, whether you meet the required 'nursing facility level of care' standard, and how your $4,000+/month income and $85,000–$160,000 in assets compare to program limits, are not yet confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's main Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver, layering services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing, transportation, and home modifications on top of personal care.

To qualify functionally, an assessment must find a need at the 'nursing facility level of care,' which can be based on needing help with several daily activities (like eating, toileting, bathing, dressing, or moving around) or needing nursing-level supervision.

Financially, the program generally looks for income at or below about $2,982/month and assets at or below $2,000 for a single person, but couples get extra protections — a spouse living at home can typically keep up to roughly $162,660 in assets and a monthly income allowance.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a CARE assessment requested through the regional Home and Community Services intake line.

informationStillNeeded: How many daily activities (beyond dressing) require help, and how much help is needed with each
The exact monthly income figure, to compare with the program's income limit
The exact countable asset figure, to compare with the program's asset limit
Confirmation of marital/living situation with a spouse, for spousal protection calculations
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE (functional) assessment
Submit or review a financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or by mail
Gather documentation on income, assets, and any past property transfers or gifts within the last 5 years

However, the program generally requires an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities to continue receiving services, and it's not yet known whether you have additional care needs beyond dressing, or whether you are currently enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with daily activities (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, medication management) and related tasks like meal prep and housework, plus services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily activities, and financially must be eligible for regular community Medicaid (not the institutional rules used by COPES).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the household is currently enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid (Apple Health)

nextSteps: Check current Apple Health enrollment status
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS intake to see what level of daily-activity help is documented

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 67, you meet the basic age threshold this program looks for, and this is the financial gateway many people use before accessing waivers like COPES. Your $4,000+/month income and $85,000–$160,000 in assets are the kind of figures this program's income and asset rules are built around, but exact comparisons haven't been made yet, and whether you meet the required level of care is unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it is the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES, along with nursing facility care.

Income limits are generally around $2,982/month (with a Medically Needy pathway for people above that), and asset limits are normally $2,000 single/$3,000 couple, though a spouse living at home can often keep substantially more — up to roughly $162,660 — under spousal protection rules.

Applying involves a financial application plus a phone or in-person interview, along with a CARE assessment; Eastern Washington counties including Ferry County are served by the Region 1 HCS intake line.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at four programs based on the information shared about your 86-year-old family member in Pacific County, who has dementia, needs help with bathing and toileting, lives at home, and has Medicare and long-term care insurance coverage. One program (Medicare's home health benefit) lines up closely with the details you've given so far. The other three programs could be worth a closer look, but several important pieces of information are still missing — such as Medicaid enrollment status, the full number of daily activities needing help, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved. Because so much is still unknown, please treat this as a starting point rather than a final answer, and use the follow-up questions below to gather more details before deciding where to focus next. Nothing here is an eligibility determination — only the administering agency for each program can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home, and has care needs (help with bathing and toileting, plus a dementia diagnosis) that could align with skilled or personal care needs this benefit is meant to address. This is the strongest match among the candidates given what's known so far, though a doctor still needs to confirm homebound status and a skilled care need to move forward.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy, and home health aide help — such as help with bathing, walking, and grooming — when ordered by a doctor and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency, generally at no cost for the covered services.

To qualify, the person must be considered homebound (leaving home is not recommended or requires considerable effort and help) and need only part-time or intermittent skilled care; aide help with personal care tasks is included only when skilled nursing or therapy is also part of the plan.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care at home, meal delivery, or personal care alone if no skilled care is also needed.
The process starts with a face-to-face assessment and a doctor's order, after which a Medicare-certified agency coordinates the care plan.

nextSteps: Ask your family member's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health services.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Pacific County.
Discuss with the doctor whether homebound status and skilled care needs (beyond personal care alone) apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs help with bathing and toileting and has a dementia diagnosis, which are the kinds of daily living needs this Medicaid program is designed to help with. However, whether this program is a fit also depends on Medicaid enrollment/eligibility and the full count of daily activities needing help, both of which are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice provides Medicaid-funded personal care for people who need help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, and mobility, delivered in the person's own home or other community settings.

To continue receiving services at reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three daily living activities, along with a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care need.

Financial eligibility generally requires qualifying for regular community Medicaid coverage (not the more limited institutional rules); people already on certain Apple Health coverage may only need the functional assessment.
Beyond personal care, the program can include relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, and caregiver management training.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is enrolled in, or would qualify financially for, Washington Apple Health (Medicaid) community coverage
The total number of daily living activities (like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring) that need help
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional intake to ask about a CARE assessment.
Ask whether a Medicaid financial application is needed or whether existing coverage already qualifies.
Discuss the full list of daily care needs with a caseworker so the ADL count can be assessed accurately.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days, and it isn't yet known whether such a stay has occurred, so relevance right now is uncertain.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days of skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days (observation status does not count).

Covered care includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medications, and medical supplies used at the facility, with $0 cost for days 1–20, $217/day coinsurance for days 21–100 (2026), and no coverage after day 100 in the benefit period.
This is intended for short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay, not for ongoing custodial or long-term care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was recommended afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as an inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers several respite options for unpaid family caregivers, including Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), which provides planned short-term relief care with amounts that vary by county and local funding.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require the unpaid caregiver to be providing 40 or more hours per week of care, while FCSP eligibility and service levels are determined through a local caregiver-specialist screening (TCARE assessment) that varies by Area Agency on Aging.
Respite can be delivered in different settings, including in-home care, adult day services, or short residential stays, and may include support groups, caregiver training, and equipment.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and durable medical equipment. A home health aide (help with walking, bathing, grooming, dressing) is covered too, but only while you're also receiving skilled nursing, PT, SLP, or OT — it's not available as personal care alone.
There is no cost to you for the covered home health services themselves; for Medicare-covered durable medical equipment you'd pay 20% of the Medicare-approved amount after the Part B deductible.

Are you interested in receiving care at home versus in a facility setting, since this affects which of these programs fits best?

report.overallSummary: Based on what your family has shared — an 86-year-old in Yakima County living at home with a spouse, needing help with transferring, covered by Medicare, with monthly income of $4,000 or more and countable assets between $85,000 and $160,000 — one program (Medicare's home health benefit) looks like a strong, immediate fit to explore. Several Washington Medicaid long-term-care programs (COPES, Community First Choice, and Apple Health LTC) could also be worth a conversation with a caseworker, but the screening tool still needs more specifics about daily-care needs and the exact income/asset figures before it can say more. Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit and respite care programs may or may not apply depending on details we don't have yet (a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved). None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency (DSHS/HCS, the Health Care Authority, or Medicare) can determine actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a nursing facility, and has a reported need for help with transferring — all of which line up with what this benefit is designed for. All the basic screening checks for this program came back as a match.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide help (like assistance with walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services, plus durable medical equipment for home use — generally at $0 for the covered services themselves.

To use this benefit, a health care provider must certify homebound status and the need for skilled care after a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Talk with your family member's doctor or nurse practitioner about a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and the need for skilled care.
Ask the provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Yakima County.
Before care starts, ask the agency for a written explanation of what Medicare will and will not cover.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 86, which meets the age 65+ pathway for COPES, and lives in Yakima County within Washington, where this program operates. The reported need for help with transferring is one of the activities of daily living COPES looks at when deciding if someone needs a nursing-facility level of care. Because your family member is married, the program's spousal protection rules (which shield some income and assets for the spouse) may be relevant, and the reported income ($4,000+/month) and assets ($85,000–$160,000) are the kind of figures the program's financial rules are built around — though we don't yet know how they compare to the exact limits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, wellness and behavior support training, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, delivered in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To be considered, a CARE assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care — this can be based on needing daily nurse-level care, or having unmet needs in several activities of daily living such as transferring, bathing, or toileting, sometimes combined with memory or judgment issues.

Financially, the program looks at whether income is at or below Washington's Special Income Level (about $2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) or a lower Medically Needy level, and generally expects countable resources at or below $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple) — but for married couples, spousal impoverishment rules can protect a larger share of income and resources for the spouse still living in the home (a federal maximum around $162,660 in protected resources as of 1/1/2026).

informationStillNeeded: A fuller picture of which daily activities your family member needs help with, beyond transferring, since the program's care-level test looks at multiple activities.
The exact monthly income figure, to compare against the program's income limits.
The exact countable asset figure, to compare against the program's resource limits and spousal protection amounts.
Confirmation of marital status for the purpose of applying spousal protection rules.
nextSteps: Contact HCS regional intake for Yakima County (Region 1: 509-568-3767 or 866-323-9409) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org, mail, fax, or in person to start the financial review alongside the assessment.
Ask HCS how spousal impoverishment protections would apply given the couple's reported income and assets.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with transferring is one of the activities of daily living that Community First Choice (CFC) personal care assistance is built around. Your family member is over 18 (age 86) and lives in Washington, where CFC is administered through Home and Community Services. However, we don't yet know whether your family member is enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), which is required for CFC.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities of daily living — including transferring, bathing, dressing, toileting, and medication management — plus related supports like relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, in the person's own home or in a residential care setting.

To qualify, a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care need, and to continue receiving CFC at reassessment the person must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three activities of daily living; financially, the person must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community (not institutional financial rules).

informationStillNeeded: Whether your family member is currently enrolled in or would qualify for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
How many daily activities besides transferring your family member needs help with, since ongoing CFC eligibility depends on needing help with three or more.
nextSteps: Ask HCS about current Medicaid enrollment status when applying for the CARE assessment.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS regional intake for Yakima County to get a functional needs picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health long-term care is the financial gateway to Medicaid-funded home and community services like COPES, and your family member's age (86) meets the 65+ pathway. Living in Yakima County and needing help with transferring both connect to the kind of functional need this program's assessment considers. As a married couple with reported income of $4,000+/month and assets of $85,000–$160,000, the program's income and resource rules — including special protections for a spouse — are relevant to check, though we don't know the exact comparison yet.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to nursing facility care, HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and community-based long-term care.

The program generally expects countable resources at or below $2,000 (single) or $3,000 (couple), with a home equity limit of $1,130,000 (1/1/2026); for married applicants, spousal impoverishment rules can protect additional resources and income for the spouse who isn't applying, up to a federal maximum around $162,660 in protected resources (1/1/2026).
Applying involves a financial application (such as HCA 18-005) plus a functional CARE assessment arranged through HCS regional intake, with eligibility determined within about 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: A fuller picture of daily living needs beyond transferring, to see whether a nursing-facility level of care would likely be found.
The exact monthly income figure, to compare with program income limits.
The exact countable asset figure, to compare with program resource limits and spousal protections.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call HCS Region 1 intake (509-568-3767 or 866-323-9409) to request the CARE functional assessment for Yakima County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this benefit only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and nothing in the information shared so far indicates a recent hospital stay, so it's unclear whether this program is currently relevant.

whatItCovers.text: When it applies, Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, and certain ambulance transportation, for up to 100 days per benefit period, with cost-sharing starting on day 21.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days as an admitted inpatient (not observation status).
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs or has occurred recently, ask the hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
If there's no recent qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives such as Medicare home health, Medicaid, or other long-term care options.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member needs ongoing help (such as with transferring) and is married, which raises the possibility that a spouse or other family member may be providing unpaid care — respite programs are designed to give such caregivers a break. We don't yet know whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is central to these programs.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers a few respite options for unpaid family caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (planned short-term in-home, adult day, or residential respite care, plus caregiver training and support groups, with amounts varying by county), and DDA respite for caregivers of someone with a developmental disability.

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours a week of care, that both caregiver and care receiver live in Washington, and that the family can't afford respite on their own; the Family Caregiver Support Program is available to unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ needing care, with details varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid caregiving, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to connect with the Yakima County Area Agency on Aging about the Family Caregiver Support Program.
If applicable, look into Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers via wapave.org/lifespan-respite-washington or 253-442-3214.

followUpQuestions: Besides help with transferring, does your family member need help with other daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or moving around the home?
Is your family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), or has an application ever been submitted?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at two programs based on the family profile for an 87-year-old in Clallam County, Washington, who has dementia, is currently living in a nursing facility, has Medicare, and has income and assets in the low range described. programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is enrolled in Medicare and is currently living in a nursing facility, which is why this Medicare skilled nursing facility (SNF) benefit came up as possibly relevant. However, this Medicare benefit is specifically for short-term skilled nursing and rehab care after a hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit — and we don't yet know whether there was a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay before the nursing facility admission.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications and supplies used in the facility, certain ambulance transport, and dietary counseling — but only in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

To be eligible for this coverage, someone generally needs active Medicare Part A benefits, a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row (observation status doesn't count), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
For 2026, there is no daily charge for days 1–20 of a benefit period, a coinsurance of $217/day for days 21–100, and the patient pays all costs after day 100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there was a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more days right before the nursing facility admission (this is required for this specific Medicare benefit to apply).
Whether a doctor has determined that daily skilled nursing or therapy care is needed.
nextSteps: Check hospital discharge paperwork to see if it shows an inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days (not observation status).
Ask the nursing facility's admissions office whether the current stay is being billed as Medicare skilled care or as long-term/custodial care.
If a qualifying hospital stay is unclear, ask the hospital discharge planner or call Medicare directly to clarify.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member has dementia and lives in Clallam County, Washington, which is why respite care programs were flagged as possibly relevant — these programs are designed to give unpaid family caregivers a break. However, we don't yet know whether there is currently an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, or how much hands-on help with daily activities is needed, and your family member is already living in a nursing facility, which may affect how this program applies.

whatItCovers.text: Washington has several respite care channels for unpaid caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for care receivers with a developmental disability that generally began before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington, and can offer up to $1,000 per household per year, though funding is limited and not guaranteed.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and supplies, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member or friend) is currently providing regular care or supervision, and roughly how many hours per week.

Clarify with the facility whether any family caregiving is happening outside the facility that might qualify for respite support.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is covered by Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has an indicated care need — all of which line up with this program's basic requirements.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and home health aide help with bathing and grooming — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.

report.overallSummary: Right now we only know that you are an adult living in Washington state. That is not enough information for this screening tool to tell you which programs are likely to be worth investigating — almost every rule these programs use (your age, income, assets, care needs, veteran status, Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, and more) is still unknown. Below is background on each program a screener flagged as possibly relevant in Washington, based only on general state rules, but none of them can be prioritized over another until we know more about your situation. Please treat this as general education, not a personalized recommendation, and answer the follow-up questions so a more useful screening can be produced. Only the administering agency for each program can determine actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've told us you're an adult in Washington state, which is the basic starting point for this Washington-specific Medicaid waiver, but we don't yet know your age, care needs, income, or assets — all of which determine whether COPES is worth looking into.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid home-and-community-based waiver run by DSHS Home and Community Services, serving over 51,000 people in their own homes, adult family homes, and assisted living facilities.
It can cover services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, layered on top of personal care.

Eligibility generally involves being 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), needing a nursing-facility level of care, and meeting income and asset limits such as $2,982/month income and $2,000 in countable assets for a single person (figures as of 1/1/2026).

informationStillNeeded: Your age

Your marital status

programs.whyThisMayApply: As an adult in Washington state, you could potentially interact with the state Medicaid system that includes this benefit, but we don't yet know if you're on Medicaid or how much help you need with daily activities, so we can't say more.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice (CFC) is a Washington Medicaid state-plan benefit that pays for personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community.
It covers help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care, and it's the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and Community First Choice.
It covers nursing facility care with 24-hour supervised nursing, personal care, therapy, and full Apple Health medical benefits alongside long-term care coverage.
Eligibility generally involves meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based criteria, nursing-facility-level care, and income limits such as the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (1/1/2026).

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: We know you're in Washington state, but PACE is only available in specific counties (King, Pierce, Spokane, Snohomish), and we don't yet know your county, age, or care needs.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care for people who need a nursing-home level of care but can live safely in the community, through an interdisciplinary team.
Washington has three PACE organizations serving only King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, and coverage depends on your specific ZIP code within those counties.
Covered services include adult day primary care, dentistry, home care, hospital care, therapy, prescription drugs, and transportation to the PACE center and appointments.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your county of residence
How many daily activities you need help with
nextSteps: Check whether your ZIP code falls within a PACE service area by contacting a PACE organization intake line.
If interested, ask about the state's nursing-facility-level-of-care certification process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: We don't know your age, income, assets, or Medicaid status, all of which determine relevance for this program, and enrollment is also currently paused statewide with a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: Tailored Supports for Older Adults (TSOA) supports people age 55+ who need a nursing-facility level of care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and asset limits higher than regular Medicaid (for example, income up to $3,976/month and resources up to $84,354 single, as of 1/1/2026).

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; current participants keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your monthly income range
Your countable assets range
Your marital status
How many daily activities you need help with
Whether you're enrolled in Medicaid
nextSteps: If interested, contact an Area Agency on Aging or HCS to ask about the current waitlist process.
Ask about non-Medicaid respite options in the meantime, since TSOA intake is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program supports unpaid caregivers of people 55+ already on Medicaid, but we don't know your age, Medicaid status, living situation, or whether you have an unpaid caregiver, so relevance is unclear; enrollment is also currently paused.

whatItCovers.text: Medicaid Alternative Care (MAC) supports unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already enrolled in Apple Health (Medicaid), meet nursing-facility level of care, and live at home rather than a licensed residential facility.

It covers caregiver assistance services such as respite care, housekeeping and errands, adult day care, home-delivered meals, training, and specialized medical equipment, but not direct personal assistance services.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Whether you're enrolled in Medicaid
Your living situation
How many daily activities you need help with
Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver
nextSteps: Contact a local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist process for MAC.
Confirm current Apple Health enrollment status if you plan to explore this further.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Respite programs support unpaid caregivers, but we don't know whether you have a caregiver providing regular care or how much help you need with daily activities, so relevance is unclear.

whatItCovers.text: Respite care programs in Washington provide short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through channels like Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers and the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP).

Lifespan Respite vouchers provide up to $1,000 per household per year, while FCSP offers planned short-term care plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options.
If a family caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, ask specifically about Lifespan Respite vouchers.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at seven Washington programs based on what you've shared: an 86-year-old widowed relative in Grays Harbor County, living in a family member's home, with dementia and help needed with transferring and eating, Medicare coverage, and countable assets under $2,000. Several programs — including Medicaid COPES, Apple Health long-term care, Community First Choice, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults — look like they could be worth a closer look based on age, functional needs, and assets, but key pieces of information (especially monthly income, current Medicaid enrollment, caregiver hours, and any recent hospital stay) are still unknown. Because those pieces are missing for most programs, this screening can only point toward things to ask about, not confirm what applies. Medicare home health stands out as a program where all the information needed for this screening was available. Please treat every item below as a starting point for a conversation with the administering agency, not a determination of eligibility or benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home (in a family member's residence, not a nursing facility), and has care needs — including help with transferring and eating and a dementia diagnosis — that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care services covered under this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A/B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (bathing, grooming, feeding, walking) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a health care provider must certify a homebound status and the need for skilled care through a face-to-face assessment, and the care must be ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Getting started involves a provider's face-to-face assessment and order, then choosing from a list of Medicare-certified agencies, which will coordinate the care plan with the doctor.

nextSteps: Talk with your relative's doctor about whether a homebound assessment and home health order would be appropriate.
Ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Grays Harbor County.
Review what documentation (like a POLST or care plan) the agency will need at intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is 86 (meeting the 65+ age rule), needs help with transferring and eating and has a dementia diagnosis (which can count toward the functional/nursing-facility-level-of-care standard), and has countable assets under $2,000 (within the resource limit). Whether income falls within the program's Special Income Level is not yet known, so that part can't be assessed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services like adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, transportation, and environmental modifications on top of basic Medicaid personal care, for people in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), have a CARE assessment showing a need for nursing-facility level of care (which can include cognitive impairment like dementia plus an ADL need), and have countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Your relative's monthly income amount, to check it against the program's income limit.
nextSteps: Contact your regional Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request a CARE functional assessment.
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, by mail, or in person.
Ask HCS how income above or below the Special Income Level would be handled in your relative's case.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative needs help with transferring and eating, which are among the ADLs this program covers, and lives in Washington state. Two key pieces are unknown: whether your relative is already enrolled in community Medicaid, and the full count of ADL needs (this program generally requires help with 3 or more ADLs to continue receiving services).

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, toileting, medication management, and more) and related IADLs, plus things like personal emergency response systems, caregiver management training, and relief care, in a home or residential care setting.

Eligibility generally requires a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, and being financially eligible for community Medicaid (categorically needy or alternative benefit plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your relative is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP).
A fuller picture of how many daily activities your relative needs help with.
nextSteps: Check with Washington Apple Health/HCA about current Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment through HCS to document ADL needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is 86 (meeting the 65+ rule), needs help with transferring and eating, and has assets under $2,000, which fits within this program's resource limit. Monthly income has not been shared, and that determines which financial pathway (regular or Medically Needy) might apply.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, as well as nursing facility care, and is administered jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally requires meeting resource limits ($2,000 for a single person) and either an income at or below the Special Income Level or qualifying through the Medically Needy pathway if income is higher.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a functional CARE assessment arranged through regional HCS intake; the process typically takes up to 45 days.

informationStillNeeded: Your relative's monthly income amount.
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 application online, by mail, or in person.
Call the Region 3 HCS intake line (covering Grays Harbor County) to request a CARE assessment.
Ask about the Medically Needy pathway if income turns out to be above the Special Income Level.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a qualifying 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay followed by a need for daily skilled care — and it's not known whether such a hospital stay has recently occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
This is a short-term skilled care benefit, not a long-term custodial care benefit, and costs increase after day 20 within a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was recommended afterward.
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it counts as a qualifying stay for SNF coverage.
If there is no recent qualifying hospital stay, consider asking about Medicare home health, Medicaid, or other options instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative is over 55 and appears to need nursing-facility-level care (help with transferring and eating, plus dementia), and countable resources are within this program's higher resource limits. However, statewide enrollment in this program has been paused since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist, and income information is still needed to see whether it fits within the program's limit.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits set well above regular Medicaid limits, and can also serve unpaid family caregivers.

As of the most recent update, the program's enrollment is paused statewide; new applications go on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed for services right away, and there is no announced date for reopening.

informationStillNeeded: Your relative's monthly income amount, to compare against the TSOA income limit.
Whether your relative is currently enrolled in Medicaid (categorically needy/ABP), since that would make TSOA not applicable.
nextSteps: Check current waitlist status by contacting Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or washingtonconnection.org.
While waiting, ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) respite funds mentioned as an alternative during the pause.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your relative needs ongoing help and supervision due to dementia and needs with transferring and eating, and currently lives in a family member's home — which suggests there may be an unpaid caregiver, though that hasn't been confirmed. Respite programs are designed to give such caregivers short-term relief.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers respite through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP, varying by Area Agency on Aging with no statewide cap), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities originating before age 18).

Lifespan Respite vouchers generally require an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours a week of care, while FCSP eligibility and service levels are set through a caregiver specialist assessment and vary by community.
Applying can be done online or by phone for Lifespan Respite, or by contacting the local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections for FCSP.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care for your relative, and roughly how many hours per week.
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about FCSP respite options.
If an unpaid caregiver is providing 40+ hours a week of care, look into Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers as well.

followUpQuestions: What is the monthly income for your relative (before taxes)? This affects several Medicaid-related programs.
Is your relative currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid (categorically needy or alternative benefit plan)?
Besides transferring and eating, are there other daily activities (bathing, dressing, toileting, walking, medication management) your relative needs help with?
Is there an unpaid family member or friend providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has your relative had a hospital inpatient stay (3 or more consecutive days) recently, and if so, is ongoing skilled nursing or therapy needed afterward?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you're 70, live in your own home in Douglas County, have Medicare, get by on income under $1,000/month, and need help with dressing — a few Washington and Medicare programs look worth a closer look. Medicare's home health benefit appears to be the strongest match right now since your Medicare enrollment and home-based living situation line up with its basic rules. A few other programs (Medicaid Community First Choice, Tailored Supports for Older Adults, respite care, and Medicare's skilled nursing facility benefit) could also be worth investigating, but key facts — like how many daily activities you need help with, whether you're enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, whether you have an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stays — are still unknown. This screening is educational only; it can't determine eligibility for any program. Please treat the follow-up questions below as your next step toward a more useful picture, and confirm any real decisions with the agency that runs each program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're already covered by Medicare, live at home rather than in a facility, and have a stated care need (help with dressing), which lines up with the basic requirements for this benefit — Medicare enrollment, an indicated care need, and living at home.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (including bathing and dressing) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — generally at $0 for the covered services themselves.

To use this benefit, a person must be homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended), get a face-to-face assessment from a provider, and have care ordered by that provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.

nextSteps: Ask your doctor or nurse practitioner about scheduling a face-to-face assessment to certify homebound status and care needs.
Ask your provider for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Douglas County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're an adult living in your own home in Washington with a stated need for help dressing, and CFC is built around helping people with personal care tasks like dressing while they stay in the community. However, CFC generally requires enrollment in community Medicaid (CN/ABP) and an ongoing need for help with at least three ADLs, and both of those are currently unknown for your situation.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care — including help with dressing — along with other ADLs, IADLs like meal prep and housework, relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and caregiver management training, in settings including a person's own home.

To qualify, a person generally must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid in the community, and to continue services at reassessment must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in (or able to enroll in) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities (ADLs) you need help with, beyond dressing
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) central intake to request a CARE assessment.
Check with HCA/DSHS about your current Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're covered by Medicare, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but it only applies after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days, and it's currently unknown whether you've had a recent hospital stay like that.

whatItCovers.text: Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy, medical social services, and related supplies for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying hospital stay, with cost-sharing starting on day 21 ($217/day coinsurance in 2026).

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to a Medicare-certified SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days that required follow-up skilled care
nextSteps: If you've had a recent qualifying hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover SNF care before admission.
If there's been no recent hospital stay, this program likely isn't relevant right now — Medicare home health may be a better fit to ask about.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're over 55, your income (under $1,000/month) is well within the TSOA income limit, and your countable assets ($2,000–$85,000) appear to fall within the single-person resource limit. TSOA is also designed for people not enrolled in classic CN/ABP Medicaid, which may fit your situation, but it's unknown whether your care needs meet the required nursing-facility level of care standard. Also, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA serves people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but aren't on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid ($3,976/month income limit and $84,354 resource limit for a single person as of 1/1/2026), no cost-sharing, and no estate recovery.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applications go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and full financial/functional review happens only once someone is called from the waitlist.

informationStillNeeded: Whether your care needs meet the nursing-facility level of care standard (generally based on how many daily activities you need help with)
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections/your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about the TSOA waitlist and functional screening.
Ask about non-Medicaid FCSP respite funds in the meantime, since TSOA enrollment is currently paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have an ongoing care need (help with dressing), which is part of what respite programs are designed to support, but these programs are built around relief for an unpaid family caregiver, and it's currently unknown whether you have one — your profile also describes you as single, which may mean there isn't a regular unpaid caregiver in the picture.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers up to $1,000 per household per year for caregiver relief, while the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day, or residential) plus caregiver training and consultation, with amounts varying by county/Area Agency on Aging.

Lifespan Respite generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours per week of care, while FCSP serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with eligibility and service levels set through a local screening.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver who helps you regularly, and about how many hours per week they provide care
nextSteps: If there is an unpaid caregiver in your life, contact your local Area Agency on Aging via Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP or Lifespan Respite options.
