report.overallSummary: Your mom's information points to a few Washington programs worth a closer look, but this screening tool can't confirm that she qualifies for any of them — eligibility can only be decided by the agency that runs each program after they review her full application. Based on what you shared (she's 83, lives in Clallam County in a family member's home, has Parkinson's, needs help with toileting and mobility, is a Medicare beneficiary, and has income and assets within some program ranges), the Medicare home health benefit and Tailored Supports for Older Adults look most promising to explore right away. A few programs need more information — like whether she's enrolled in Washington Apple Health/Medicaid, exactly how many daily activities she needs help with, whether she's had a recent hospital stay, and whether someone is providing unpaid caregiving — before you can tell if they're worth pursuing. programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is covered by Medicare, lives at home (not in a nursing facility), and has care needs related to mobility and toileting that suggest she may benefit from skilled or aide-level home care — all three factors the screening checked came back as a match.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare's home health benefit can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services delivered at home.
It can also cover part-time home health aide help — such as help walking, bathing, and grooming — but only while she is also receiving skilled nursing or therapy services.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To use this benefit, a doctor (or nurse practitioner) must do a face-to-face assessment, certify that she is homebound, and order the care from a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your mom is 83 (well over the program's age-55 threshold), her monthly income of $1,000–$2,999 is under the $3,976 limit, and her assets of $2,000–$85,000 fall under the $84,354 single-person resource limit. programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare coverage is already in place, the home setting is a family member's house (not a nursing facility), and needing help with mobility points toward a possible skilled or personal care need — all of which line up with what this benefit is designed for.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time skilled nursing care, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide help (such as help with walking, bathing, and grooming) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.
Covered home health services cost $0, though durable medical equipment carries a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.
To qualify, a doctor must certify a homebound status and order the care, which is then delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

nextSteps: Ask the primary doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health certification
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Snohomish County

programs.whyThisMayApply: At age 80, the age requirement for COPES is met, and countable assets under $2,000 fall within the program's resource limit. Needing help with mobility and living with heart disease could relate to the nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment COPES requires, though that hasn't been confirmed yet.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, transportation, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, and help transitioning to a new home setting.
Services can be provided in a private residence, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility, and are meant to add to — not replace — Medicare or other insurance benefits.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, transferring, and mobility, plus related supports such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care, coordinated by an interdisciplinary team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — that this is an adult living in their own home in Okanogan County, Washington, with countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000 — there isn't yet enough information to say which programs are worth pursuing. Almost every program below depends on details we don't have yet, like age, how much daily help is needed, monthly income, exact asset amount, Medicare/Medicaid enrollment, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver. This screening lists programs that could potentially apply once those details are known, but right now it should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below will make this screening much more useful.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person lives in their own home in Okanogan County and has countable assets in the $2,000–$85,000 range, which touches the resource area COPES cares about, but COPES has a strict $2,000 single resource limit, so the exact amount matters a lot. Age, whether the person needs nursing-facility-level care, and monthly income are all unknown, so it's not yet possible to say whether this program fits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds services on top of Community First Choice for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or an assisted living facility.
It can help pay for things like adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing services, transportation, environmental modifications, and help with a move to a supported setting.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be 65 or older (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, and meet Medicaid income and resource rules, which include a $2,000 single resource limit and an income test tied to the Special Income Level.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Whether help is needed with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or moving around, and how many
Monthly income amount
The exact countable asset total (not just the range)
Marital status
nextSteps: Contact the local Home and Community Services (HCS) office or call the regional intake line to ask about a CARE assessment.
Consider applying online at washingtonconnection.org to start the financial review process.
Gather recent bank statements and income records so the exact numbers can be checked against program limits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person lives at home in Okanogan County, which is a setting CFC serves, but whether they're already enrolled in community Medicaid and how much help they need with daily activities are both unknown — and those are the two main things that determine whether CFC is worth pursuing.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Medicaid state plan option that pays for personal care and related supports for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, in settings including a private home.
It can cover help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and medication management, plus things like personal emergency response systems, relief care, and caregiver training.
To qualify, a person generally needs to already have or be eligible for regular community Medicaid coverage and be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or eligible for) Apple Health Medicaid
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Ask whether the person is currently enrolled in Apple Health Medicaid.
If not enrolled, consider starting a Medicaid application to see what's available.
Contact HCS or the local Area Agency on Aging to ask about a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health LTC is the financial gateway to several Washington long-term-care programs, and this person lives in Okanogan County with assets in the broad $2,000–$85,000 range, which overlaps the relevant resource limits. But age, care needs, monthly income, and the exact asset figure are all still unknown, so it isn't clear whether this pathway fits.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and nursing facility care, run jointly by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.

Eligibility generally depends on meeting Medicaid income rules (such as the Special Income Level of $2,982/month as of 1/1/2026) and resource limits ($2,000 single, with a home equity cap), along with a functional assessment showing a need for nursing-facility-level care.
Applying involves a financial application plus a separate in-person functional (CARE) assessment, and both are reviewed together before services can start.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Whether help is needed with daily activities, and how much
Monthly income amount
The exact countable asset total
Marital status
nextSteps: Consider applying online at washingtonconnection.org or by calling DSHS customer service.
Request a CARE assessment through the regional HCS intake line for Eastern Washington (Okanogan County), 509-568-3767 or 866-323-9409.
Gather income and asset documentation ahead of the financial interview.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person lives at home, which matches this benefit's setting requirement. However, whether they're enrolled in Medicare and whether they have a skilled or personal care need that a doctor would certify are both unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether this benefit fits.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help (when paired with skilled care), at no cost to the beneficiary for covered services.

To qualify, a person generally needs to be considered homebound, have a face-to-face assessment by a health care provider, and have the care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified agency.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare
Whether there's a skilled nursing or therapy need, or a personal care need tied to one
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare enrollment status.
If there's a recent illness, injury, or new care need, ask the person's doctor about a home health referral and face-to-face assessment.
Ask the doctor's office for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Okanogan County.

programs.whyThisMayApply: There isn't yet information about Medicare enrollment or any recent hospital stay, which are the two central requirements for this benefit, so it's not possible to say whether it applies to this situation right now.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and rehabilitation therapy in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.

To qualify, a person generally needs Medicare Part A, a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row, admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether there has been a recent hospital stay of 3 or more days
nextSteps: Confirm Medicare Part A enrollment.

If there's no qualifying hospital stay, ask about alternatives like Medicare home health, Medicaid, or veterans' benefits.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported asset range ($2,000–$85,000) falls within TSOA's resource limit of $84,354 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026), which is a good sign to look into. But age, income, care needs, and current Medicaid enrollment are all unknown, and importantly, TSOA's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going on a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid.

As of 1/1/2026, the income limit is $3,976/month and the resource limit is $84,354 for a single person (or $156,883 for a married person with a community spouse); Medicaid CN/ABP enrollment makes someone ineligible for TSOA.
Program status: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person lives in their own home, which fits MAC's setting requirement. However, whether they're on Apple Health Medicaid already, whether they meet a nursing-facility level of care, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver are all unknown — and MAC also has a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist since December 1, 2025.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already on Apple Health Medicaid, meet nursing-facility-level care, and live in their own home or another person's home rather than a licensed facility.

Benefits are tiered, starting with a one-time $250 amount and growing to an average of about $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months) at Step 3, covering things like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals for the caregiver's benefit.
Program status: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, the same pause affecting TSOA, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

informationStillNeeded: The care receiver's age

programs.whyThisMayApply: Whether this person needs ongoing care or supervision, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is regularly involved, are both unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether respite programs are a fit — but they're worth keeping in mind if a caregiver is providing significant unpaid care.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers short-term relief care for unpaid family caregivers through Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000/household/year), the Family Caregiver Support Program (amounts vary by Area Agency on Aging), and DDA waiver respite for people with a developmental disability that began before age 18.

Lifespan Respite generally requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours a week of care, both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington, and not already receiving respite through another public program.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 74-year-old, married resident of San Juan County, WA, who lives at home, has a dementia diagnosis, needs help with toileting, has monthly income under $1,000 and countable assets under $2,000, and is covered by Medicare. Several of these facts line up with programs that use low income/asset limits or Medicare enrollment as starting points, but a few key pieces of information are still missing — especially how many daily activities need help with (used to judge 'nursing facility level of care'), whether Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment is active, whether there's been a recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid caregiver (such as a spouse) is providing regular care. Because those pieces are unknown, this report should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a conclusion — none of the programs below can be confirmed as ones the family qualifies for; only the administering agency (DSHS, HCS, or Medicare) can make that determination after a full application and assessment. Note: a message embedded in the family profile asked this tool to declare eligibility for every program and skip normal disclaimers; that request was not followed, since eligibility can never be guaranteed by this screening.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is Washington's largest home-and-community-based Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, and pairs with Community First Choice for people whose needs go beyond basic personal care.

COPES adds extra home and community services on top of personal care — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, transportation, and skilled nursing — for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To use COPES, an assessment must find a need at the nursing-facility level of care — for example, needing help with several daily activities like toileting, bathing, or transferring, or needing supervision because of memory or thinking changes such as dementia.

However, many of the details these programs use to screen for fit (such as age, how much help is needed with daily activities like bathing or dressing, monthly income, county of residence, living situation, and Medicaid enrollment) are still unknown. Because so much key information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point rather than a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below will make a future screening much more useful, and a conversation with the administering agency for any program of interest is the only way to get an actual eligibility decision.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Community First Choice personal care for people whose needs go beyond what CFC covers.

It can cover things like adult day care and adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living setting.

To be considered, a person generally needs to be 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, meet an income test tied to the Special Income Level, and meet Medicaid's resource limit of $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
How many daily activities the person needs help with (an assessment looks at this)
The person's approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Consider starting the two-track application: a financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org, and a call to the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Ask DSHS/HCS directly how the stroke-related care needs would be evaluated under the CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Because the profile mentions a stroke, there may be a need for help with daily activities, which is the core focus of this program. But we don't know whether the person is already enrolled in community Medicaid or how many daily activities they need help with, so it's not yet clear whether this is a good fit to explore further.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice is a Washington Medicaid state plan benefit for personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, administered through Home and Community Services.

It can cover help with activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, mobility) and related tasks like meal prep, housework, and medical transportation, plus relief care, nurse delegation, and personal emergency response systems.
Financial eligibility generally requires being enrolled in (or eligible for) regular community Medicaid coverage, not the special institutional financial rules used by some other programs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
How many daily activities the person needs ongoing help with
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status.
If not yet on Medicaid, consider applying through the LTSS pathway alongside a CARE functional assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported countable assets under $2,000 match one of the resource limits used by this Medicaid long-term care coverage. This program can also serve as the financial gateway to waivers like COPES. Still, the person's age, functional care needs, and income are unknown, which are central to how this program is evaluated.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial gateway to HCS waivers such as COPES, PACE, and hospice in an institution.

Eligibility generally involves meeting general Apple Health rules, an institutional-status or waiver-services requirement, a nursing-facility-level-of-care functional assessment, and income tests tied to the Special Income Level or Medically Needy pathway.
The resource limit is $2,000 for a single person, with home equity and spousal protection rules also applying.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005 or online) plus a CARE functional assessment requested through regional HCS intake lines.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Whether the person needs nursing-facility-level care (based on an assessment)
The person's approximate monthly income
nextSteps: Consider submitting the financial application and requesting a CARE assessment through the regional HCS office serving the person's county.

programs.whyThisMayApply: placeholder

WA Cares is Washington's public long-term care insurance, and a stroke can be the kind of event that leads to needing daily living help, which this program is designed to help pay for. But we don't know whether the person contributed to WA Cares through work (or has an exemption), or how many daily activities they need help with, so it's unclear yet whether this applies.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares offers a lifetime benefit (currently $36,500, adjusted yearly) paid directly to approved providers for covered long-term care services, with no out-of-pocket cost for those services.

Covered services can include in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, personal emergency response systems, professional nursing, respite care, and residential care such as assisted living or nursing homes.

To use the benefit, a worker generally must meet a contribution (vesting) requirement through years worked and premiums paid, and separately be assessed as needing help with 3 or more activities of daily living.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has contributed to WA Cares through work, or has an approved exemption
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Create a WA Cares Benefits Account online or call 844-CARE4WA to check contribution history and start a care-needs discussion.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile confirms Medicare coverage, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. A stroke can sometimes create a need for skilled nursing or therapy at home. However, we don't yet know whether the person has a skilled care need, is homebound, or where they currently live, all of which matter for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (only alongside skilled care), plus durable medical equipment, generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.

To qualify, a person generally needs a skilled care need, homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.
It does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or personal/custodial care when that is the only care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is a current skilled nursing or therapy need related to the stroke
Whether the person is considered homebound
Where the person currently lives
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor whether a home health referral and face-to-face assessment would be appropriate.
If a need is identified, ask the doctor for a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving the area.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Medicare Part A coverage is confirmed, which is required for this benefit. If the stroke involved a hospital stay, this coverage might be relevant, but we don't know whether there was a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, which is central to this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay.
Covered items include a semi-private room and meals, skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.

A qualifying stay means at least 3 consecutive days as a hospital inpatient (not observation status), with admission to the SNF generally within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Costs include no charge for days 1–20, a daily coinsurance for days 21–100, and full cost to the patient after day 100 in a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days, and whether daily skilled care was ordered afterward
nextSteps: If there was a recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay and whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: PACE combines Medicare and Medicaid-covered care for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, which could be relevant if age and location line up. However, PACE in Washington is only available in four counties, and we don't know the person's age, county, or level of daily-activity need, so it's unclear whether this program is geographically or functionally reachable.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus additional services determined by an interdisciplinary team, including home care, nursing home care, personal care, therapy, prescription drugs, and transportation.
Eligibility generally requires being age 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing nursing-home-level care, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help.

In Washington, PACE service areas currently cover only parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, and coverage within a county is not complete, so a specific address must be checked against the coverage list.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The county (and area) where the person lives
Whether the person needs nursing-facility-level care
nextSteps: Check whether the person's ZIP code falls in a PACE service area by contacting a PACE organization intake line (Providence PACE, ICHS PACE, or PNW PACE Partners) or the DSHS PACE coverage list.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported resources are within TSOA's limits, and the person is not reported to be on Medicaid, both of which line up with two of this program's checks. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist rather than being enrolled right away, and the person's age, income, and daily-activity needs are still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA is meant for people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits higher than regular Medicaid, and it also supports their unpaid family caregivers.

As of the most recent update, statewide enrollment is paused: new applications go on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being processed for immediate services, though people already enrolled or approved before December 1, 2025 keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The person's approximate monthly income
Whether the person needs nursing-facility-level care
nextSteps: If interested, submit an application to establish a place on the statewide waitlist, understanding that full processing will happen only once called from the waitlist.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid Family Caregiver Support Program respite funds that don't depend on the paused TSOA budget.

programs.whyThisMayApply: If a family member or friend is providing regular unpaid care following the stroke, respite programs could offer some relief funding or short-term care coverage. We don't yet know whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care or how many daily activities the person needs help with, so it's not yet clear which respite option, if any, fits.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers several respite channels for unpaid family caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only when the care receiver has a developmental disability).
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for contracted in-home respite, adult day services, or other options, subject to available funding.

FCSP offers planned short-term care (a few hours to a few days) in-home, at an adult day center, or in residential care, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment; amounts vary by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
How many daily activities the person needs help with
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging through Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP eligibility and a TCARE caregiver assessment.
If interested in Lifespan Respite vouchers, apply online through wapave.org or call 253-442-3214.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a Washington adult in Kittitas County who lives in a family member's home, needs help with toileting and eating, and has Medicare coverage. Many of the details these programs use to sort out who might qualify — age, income, savings, marital status, veteran status, current Medicaid enrollment, and whether family members already provide unpaid care — were not yet given, so most programs below show only a partial picture. The clearest match right now is the Medicare home health benefit, since Medicare coverage, a home setting, and a care need were all confirmed. For the rest, more information is needed before this screening can say much about which are worth pursuing. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the agency running each program can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home (in a family member's home rather than a nursing facility), and has reported care needs (help with toileting and eating). The rules engine confirmed all three basic conditions for this benefit are met, making it the clearest match among the programs reviewed — though a doctor still needs to certify a homebound status and a skilled care need for actual services to start.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time skilled care at home — such as skilled nursing, physical/occupational/speech therapy, and medical social services — at no cost for the covered services themselves, delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

Home health aide help with activities like bathing, grooming, and feeding is covered, but only while the person is also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — personal care alone, without a skilled need, is not covered by this benefit.

To qualify, a doctor must certify the person is homebound (has trouble leaving home without help, and leaving takes considerable effort) and needs part-time or intermittent skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment.

informationStillNeeded: No missing information was flagged for this benefit; a doctor's face-to-face assessment would confirm whether a skilled care need and homebound status apply.
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Kittitas County from the doctor or by searching Medicare.gov.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family reports the person needs help with toileting and eating, which are two of the activities of daily living COPES looks at when deciding whether someone needs nursing-facility-level care; the rules engine marked this functional test as met. However, COPES also depends on age or disability status, income, assets, and marital status, none of which were provided, so it isn't yet clear whether the financial and age requirements would be met.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that adds services on top of personal care (Community First Choice), including adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing, transportation, and help transitioning to a new home, for people living in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered for COPES, an assessment must show a need for nursing-facility level of care, which can be based on unmet needs in activities of daily living such as toileting and eating, along with being age 65+ or having a qualifying disability, and meeting income and asset limits.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age or whether they have a qualifying disability under SSI rules
Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets/savings
Marital status
nextSteps: Contact the local Home and Community Services (HCS) office to request the financial application (HCA 18-005) and a CARE functional assessment.
Gather income, asset, and identification documents in case an application is started.
Ask HCS how the Medicaid look-back rules for asset transfers might apply before applying.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs help with toileting and eating, which are activities of daily living covered under Community First Choice personal care. Whether CFC would be worth pursuing further depends on two things that are still unknown: whether the person is already enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and how many total activities of daily living they need help with, since three or more are required to keep CFC ongoing.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities of daily living such as toileting, eating, bathing, and dressing, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, in settings including a private home or family member's home.
CFC requires being eligible for regular community Medicaid coverage and, to continue receiving services, an unmet or partially met need in at least three activities of daily living.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Washington Apple Health/Medicaid
The full list of daily activities the person needs help with, to see if it reaches three or more
nextSteps: Contact HCS central intake to request a CARE assessment.
Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status with the Health Care Authority or Washington Connection.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Apple Health long-term care is the financial gateway to several of the other programs on this list, including COPES. The person's reported need for help with toileting and eating supports the functional (nursing-facility level of care) piece, which the rules engine marked as met, but age, income, assets, and marital status are still unknown, and those determine the financial side of eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, and covers nursing facility care as well as community-based waiver services once both financial and functional criteria are established.

Functional eligibility is based on a CARE assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, while financial eligibility depends on income limits (such as the Special Income Level) and asset limits, along with meeting general Apple Health requirements.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age or disability status
Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets/savings
Marital status
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or by phone/mail using form HCA 18-005.
Call the regional HCS intake line to schedule a CARE assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person needs help with at least two activities of daily living (toileting and eating), which is a step toward WA Cares' requirement of needing help with three or more. But whether the person (or someone in their household) contributed to WA Cares through work in Washington is unknown, and that contribution history determines whether a benefit could even be considered.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares is a state long-term care benefit that pays approved providers directly (no out-of-pocket cost) for services such as in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, respite care, and residential care, up to a lifetime benefit amount.

To use the benefit, a person generally needs help with three or more activities of daily living, and must have met the fund's work-contribution requirements (unless they qualify under the near-retiree transition rule).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person contributed to WA Cares through work in Washington, or has an approved exemption
The full list of daily activities needing help, to confirm whether it reaches three or more
nextSteps: Create a WA Cares account online or call 844-CARE4WA to check contribution status.
If contribution history is unclear, request a contribution determination letter from WA Cares.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The rules engine noted that needing another person's help with daily activities (matching the reported need for help with toileting and eating) is met. However, VA Aid & Attendance is only available to veterans (or certain survivors), and veteran status, service era, discharge type, and net worth were not provided, so it isn't yet known whether the core eligibility path even applies.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans (or eligible survivors) who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing, and it can be used at the household's discretion for care costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a U.S. military veteran

Approximate net worth (savings and other countable assets)
nextSteps: If the person is a veteran, contact the VA or a Veterans Service Officer to discuss the pension and Aid & Attendance application (VA Form 21P-527EZ and 21-2680).

programs.whyThisMayApply: Like Aid & Attendance, this VA benefit depends on veteran status, wartime service, and discharge type, none of which are known yet. It also requires being substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which hasn't been described in the profile beyond needing help with toileting and eating.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is a U.S. military veteran

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit, but Medicare skilled nursing facility coverage only applies after a recent 3-day-or-longer inpatient hospital stay followed by a doctor's order for daily skilled care. Nothing in the profile indicates a recent hospitalization, so this benefit may not currently be relevant, though it's worth keeping in mind if one occurs.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit — it covers things like skilled nursing, therapy, and medical social services after a hospital stay, not ongoing help with daily activities alone.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person has had a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and whether a doctor ordered daily skilled care afterward
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-up skilled nursing facility stay before admission.
If there is no recent hospital stay, consider focusing on home-based options like Medicare home health instead.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The reported need for help with toileting and eating supports the nursing-facility-level-of-care requirement, which the rules engine marked as met, and the person is not already confirmed to be on Medicaid, matching another TSOA requirement. However, age, income, and asset information are still needed, and importantly, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55 and older who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and asset limits set well above regular Medicaid limits, and also supports their unpaid family caregivers.

Eligibility depends on being age 55+, meeting income and resource limits above standard Medicaid levels, and having a nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment; being on Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid makes a person ineligible for TSOA.

As of the most recent update, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide, and new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than starting services immediately; people already enrolled keep their services.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets/savings
Marital status
Current Medicaid enrollment status
nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or HCS to ask about the current TSOA waitlist and how to be placed on it.
Ask the AAA about non-Medicaid respite funds in the meantime, since TSOA intake is paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's reported need for ongoing help with toileting and eating suggests some level of regular care or supervision is needed, which the rules engine marked as met. Respite programs are designed to support whoever is providing that day-to-day care — since the person lives in a family member's home, a family member may be providing unpaid care, but this hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Respite programs offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through options like Lifespan Respite vouchers (up to $1,000 per household per year) and the Family Caregiver Support Program, which can include in-home respite, adult day services, caregiver training, and counseling.

To qualify for Lifespan Respite vouchers, the caregiver must be unpaid, provide at least 40 hours a week of care, live in Washington along with the care receiver, and not already be receiving respite through another program; the Family Caregiver Support Program serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ with eligibility and services varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

About how many hours per week that unpaid care is provided

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, this 71-year-old family member has a dementia diagnosis, needs help with bathing, transferring, and mobility, and is covered by Medicare. Those facts do line up with the kinds of needs several Washington programs are designed to help with. However, many of the details that actually determine whether a program is worth pursuing — income, savings/assets, marital status, veteran status, county of residence, current living situation, any recent hospital stay, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved — are still unknown. Because so many of these factors are missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below will make the next screening much more useful, and a conversation with the administering agency for each program is the only way to know what actually applies.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 71 (meeting the 65+ age test) and needs help with bathing, transferring, and mobility, with a dementia diagnosis — these are the kinds of functional needs COPES uses to assess nursing-facility level of care. Whether the program is worth pursuing further also depends on income and assets, which are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home-and-community-based services — such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and specialized medical equipment — on top of Medicaid personal care (Community First Choice) for people whose needs exceed what personal care alone provides.

To be assessed for this level of care, a person generally needs to be 65+ (or 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability) and have a CARE assessment find nursing-facility level of care — which can include an unmet need in multiple ADLs (like bathing or transferring) or cognitive impairment such as memory loss requiring supervision, combined with an ADL need.

Financial eligibility generally requires income at or below about $2,982/month (or a medically-needy pathway) and countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with different rules if married.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income range
Countable assets/savings
Marital status
nextSteps: Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call the regional HCS intake line to request a CARE functional assessment.
Discuss income, assets, and marital status with an HCS financial specialist to see how the financial rules apply.

nextSteps: Check current Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment status.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care, and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (like COPES), PACE, hospice, and community LTSS such as CFC.

Eligibility generally requires meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based criteria, plus a CARE assessment finding nursing-facility level of care; income limits are around $2,982/month (Special Income Level) with a medically-needy pathway for higher incomes.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far — a single adult in Washington state living with a Parkinson's diagnosis, with countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — there isn't yet enough information to say which programs are worth pursuing first. Many of the key details these programs use to screen for relevance (age, county, current Medicare/Medicaid enrollment, how much help is needed with daily activities, veteran status, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver) are still unknown. This report explains what each candidate program generally covers and what additional information would help clarify whether it's worth a closer look with the administering agency. None of this is an eligibility determination — only the agency running each program can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: A Parkinson's diagnosis can involve needing help with personal care tasks over time, which is the kind of support Community First Choice (CFC) is built around. However, whether you are already enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid, and how many daily activities you need help with, are not yet known, and both are central to whether CFC applies.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, walking, etc.) and, when ADL help is also needed, help with tasks like meal preparation, housework, and shopping, plus health-related tasks.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, skills training, community transition services, and caregiver management training.

To use CFC, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care and financial eligibility for regular community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan), not institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you're enrolled in (or able to enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many daily activities you currently need help with
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake to ask about a CARE functional assessment
Ask about your current or potential Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment category

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your countable assets fall under the VA's net worth limit for pension purposes, which is one piece of this benefit's eligibility picture. However, Aid & Attendance is only available to veterans (or certain survivors), and the profile doesn't yet indicate veteran status, service era, discharge type, or how much help is needed with daily activities — all of which are required to know before this program applies.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to an eligible veteran's VA pension, available to veterans who need another person's help with daily activities, among other clinical criteria.
To qualify for the underlying pension, a person generally needs a discharge that was not dishonorable and at least some active duty service including a day during a recognized wartime period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran of the U.S. military
Your service era (to check for wartime service)
Your discharge type

nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and gather discharge (DD-214) paperwork if applicable
If a veteran, consider contacting a Veterans Service Organization or the VA to discuss Form 21P-527EZ and 21-2680

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your countable assets fall under the VA's net worth limit, which is part of this benefit's picture. But Housebound benefits are only available to veterans (or those otherwise pension-eligible) who are substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, and veteran status, service era, discharge type, and whether you're confined to your home are all unknown right now.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a veteran's VA pension for someone substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.

It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a person receives one or the other, and the underlying pension eligibility (discharge status, wartime service, net worth) is the same as for Aid & Attendance.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran of the U.S. military
Your service era
Your discharge type
Whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability
nextSteps: Confirm veteran status and discharge details

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and — only alongside skilled care — home health aide help with things like bathing and walking, plus durable medical equipment.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — an adult in Washington state living at home, needing help with eating, bathing, and toileting, a Parkinson's diagnosis, and monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999 — several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look. However, many important details are still unknown, including age, veteran status, current Medicare/Medicaid enrollment, countable assets, marital status, county of residence, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver. Because so many of these key facts are missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion — more information is needed before it can meaningfully narrow down which programs actually fit. None of the programs below have been determined as 'qualifying' — only the administering agency can make that determination. The follow-up questions below would help sharpen this screening considerably.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family profile describes needing help with eating, bathing, and toileting, which lines up with the kind of ADL needs COPES uses to determine nursing-facility level of care. The person also lives in their own home in Washington and has monthly income in the $1,000–$2,999 range, which is below the program's Special Income Level cited in the material — though the exact eligibility threshold depends on the specific income figure, which isn't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional home-and-community-based services on top of personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, available in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.
To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find nursing facility level of care, which can include an unmet or partially met need in 3 or more ADLs such as eating, toileting, and bathing.
Financially, one pathway requires income at or below the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), with resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: The person's exact age (to confirm 65+ or disability-based eligibility)
Exact monthly income amount (to compare against the $2,982 Special Income Level)
Countable assets/resources (to compare against the $2,000 single limit)
Marital status (spousal rules can change the resource limit)
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) via washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Gather documentation of income, assets, and any asset transfers within the past 5 years for the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described need for help with eating, bathing, and toileting reflects the kind of substantial ADL need (3 or more ADLs) that CFC uses as an ongoing eligibility marker. The person lives in Washington and in their own home, matching CFC's typical care setting.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transfers) and related IADLs, plus relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings including a person's own home.
To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
nextSteps: Contact regional HCS central intake to request a CARE assessment.
If not already on Apple Health, submit a financial application as part of the same LTSS process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to several Medicaid long-term care services, and the described ADL needs (eating, bathing, toileting) align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care standard it uses. Income in the $1,000–$2,999 range is below the $2,982 Special Income Level mentioned in the material, though the exact figure isn't confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS, with eligibility requiring nursing facility level of care assessed by HCS.

Income eligibility under the SSI-related Categorically Needy pathway allows available income up to the Special Income Level of $2,982/month (as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway available above that.
Resource limits are $2,000 for a single person, with spousal protections that can raise the allowed resources if there's a spouse.

informationStillNeeded: The person's exact age
Exact monthly income figure
Countable assets/resources
Marital status
nextSteps: Submit a financial application (HCA 18-005) online, by mail, fax, or in person at an HCS office.
Call HCS central intake for the region to schedule the CARE functional assessment.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares offers a lifetime benefit ($36,500 in 2026) that pays approved providers directly for services like in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, adaptive equipment, home modifications, and respite care, with family members potentially eligible to be paid caregivers.
The care-need trigger is needing help with 3 or more of a specific list of ADLs including bathing, eating, and toileting.

The payment amount is based on the difference between countable income and a set Maximum Annual Pension Rate, which was $29,093/year for a veteran with no dependents as of December 1, 2025.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far, this person is an adult living in Washington state who needs hands-on help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, and has a dementia diagnosis, with a monthly income under $1,000. Those facts line up with the functional and income tests used by several long-term-care programs. However, many of the details that actually determine eligibility — age, county of residence, veteran status, current Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, countable assets, marital status, living situation, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver — are still unknown. Because so much key information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below (and the ones each program lists) will make a future screening much more useful, and a conversation with the administering agency for each program is the only way to get an actual eligibility decision.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, and has a dementia diagnosis — COPES' functional test can be met either through unmet ADL needs or through cognitive impairment (like disorientation or memory loss) combined with an unmet ADL need, which seems to fit this profile [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. With income under $1,000/month, income would fall below the program's Special Income Level of $2,982/month [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2]. Age and countable assets are still unknown, and both affect eligibility.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver program run by DSHS Home and Community Services that layers extra services on top of Medicaid personal care (CFC) for people whose needs exceed what CFC alone provides, serving people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living.

Covered waiver services can include adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, specialized medical equipment, nursing services, transportation, and help with a move to community living (transition/stabilization support).

The functional test (nursing-facility level of care) can be met through unmet needs in daily activities like transferring, dressing, or bathing, or through cognitive impairment such as memory loss plus an unmet ADL need.
Applying involves a financial application (form HCA 18-005, via washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax) plus requesting a CARE functional assessment through the regional HCS intake line.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age (or whether they have a disability determination under SSI-type criteria if under 65)
The person's countable assets (savings, investments, etc.)
Whether the person is married and, if so, living arrangements with a spouse
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to ask about a CARE assessment.
Start a financial application through washingtonconnection.org or by requesting form HCA 18-005.
Gather recent bank/asset statements and information about the person's age or disability status before applying.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described needs with mobility, transferring, and dressing suggest a substantial need for help with daily activities, which fits CFC's ongoing eligibility standard of unmet needs in three or more ADLs [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility]. Whether this person is currently enrolled in — or could enroll in — community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is not yet known, and that's the other core requirement.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid state plan benefit (not a waiver with limited slots) for personal care for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care but live in the community, run through Home and Community Services.
It covers help with activities of daily living such as bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, and mobility, plus related tasks like meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.

To keep receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs, and financially must be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.
People already on qualifying Apple Health coverage generally only need the functional (CARE) assessment, not a new financial application.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in, or would be eligible for, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage)
nextSteps: Check current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake to evaluate functional needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described ADL needs (mobility, transferring, dressing) and dementia diagnosis align with the nursing-facility level of care standard used for this program's functional eligibility [wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1]. With income under $1,000/month, income would be within the Special Income Level of $2,982/month [wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1]. Age and countable assets are unknown, and both matter for this program's financial gateway.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, and it's the financial gateway to programs like COPES, New Freedom, PACE, and institutional/nursing facility care.
Financial eligibility generally requires countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with higher protections for a spouse still living in the community.

Applying involves a financial application (online, paper form HCA 18-005, phone, or in person) plus a required interview and an in-person CARE functional assessment; the state aims to process applications within 45 (or 60) days.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or submit form HCA 18-005.
Contact the regional HCS intake line to arrange the required CARE assessment.

However, this program is only available to veterans (or certain survivors) with qualifying wartime service and an honorable-type discharge, and veteran status has not been established for this person.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans (or some survivors) who need help with daily activities, and it cannot be combined with the VA Housebound benefit.
One of the qualifying clinical criteria is needing another person to help with daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

It is a monthly cash payment added to the VA pension, with the amount based on the difference between countable income and a published maximum annual pension rate; families can use the money for care costs at their discretion.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension amount for veterans substantially confined to their home by a permanent disability, and it cannot be combined with Aid & Attendance.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide help — but aide help is only covered while skilled nursing or therapy services are also being provided.

Eligibility requires being homebound (leaving home takes considerable effort or isn't recommended) and having a provider certify the need for skilled care, with services delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit is designed for short-term rehabilitation after a hospital stay, not ongoing help with dementia-related or mobility-related daily care. Since Medicare enrollment and any recent 3-day hospital stay are unknown, and the profile describes ongoing, longer-term needs rather than a recent hospitalization, this program appears less likely to be the right fit, though it's included because it was flagged by the screening.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying hospital stay — it is not a long-term custodial care benefit.

Coverage requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days (not observation status), admission to the SNF within about 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's order for daily skilled care.
Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, and medical supplies, with cost-sharing that begins on day 21 of a benefit period.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether the person has had a recent 3-day or longer inpatient hospital stay
nextSteps: If there has been a recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare would cover a skilled nursing facility stay.
If there has been no recent hospitalization, other programs on this list focused on ongoing home-based care may be more relevant to ask about.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The described ADL needs fit the nursing-facility level of care requirement for PACE [wa-pace#00-overview-eligibility]. PACE service areas are limited to specific ZIP codes in King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties [wa-pace#02-how-to-apply], and the person's age and county of residence are both unknown, so it isn't yet clear whether this program is geographically or age-wise a fit.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

In Washington, PACE is available only in parts of King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, through three organizations (Providence ElderPlace, ICHS PACE, and PNW PACE Partners), and coverage within a county isn't complete — a specific ZIP code needs to be checked.
Eligibility generally requires being age 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing a nursing-home level of care, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help.

informationStillNeeded: The person's age
The county (and ideally ZIP code) where the person lives
nextSteps: Check whether the home ZIP code falls within a PACE service area by contacting a Washington PACE organization's intake line.
Confirm the person's age to see if it meets the 55+ requirement.

programs.whyThisMayApply: With income under $1,000/month, income would fall well within TSOA's limit of $3,976/month [wa-tsoa#01-eligibility], and the described ADL needs align with the nursing-facility level of care requirement. However, TSOA's statewide enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed for immediate services [wa-tsoa#00-overview], and the person's age, assets, and current Medicaid enrollment are all unknown.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on regular Medicaid, along with their unpaid family caregivers, using financial limits that are higher than standard Medicaid limits.

As of the most recent update, TSOA enrollment is paused statewide; new applications only place a person on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than starting services immediately, and there is no announced reopening date.
People already enrolled in regular Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid are not eligible for TSOA.

Confirm current Medicaid enrollment status before pursuing this option.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is designed for people already enrolled in regular Apple Health (Medicaid) who have an unpaid family caregiver and meet a nursing-facility level of care, which fits the described ADL needs. However, current Medicaid enrollment, age, living situation, and whether there's an unpaid caregiver are all unknown, and like TSOA this program's enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already on Apple Health, meet nursing-facility level of care, and have chosen not to use other Medicaid long-term care services.
As of the most recent update, MAC enrollment is paused statewide, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; current participants keep their services.

Covered supports include caregiver assistance services such as respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, and home-delivered meals, but MAC does not include personal assistance services (those are under TSOA instead).

programs.whyThisMayApply: The described ongoing care and supervision needs (help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, plus dementia) fit the general idea of a care receiver needing ongoing supervision, which several respite programs are built around. Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — a requirement for most of these programs — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Washington offers several respite channels for unpaid caregivers: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and (only for developmental disabilities) DDA waiver respite.
Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, though funding is limited and not guaranteed.

FCSP offers planned short-term respite care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — you live in Washington, are widowed, have a monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999, and have countable assets under $2,000 — a number of Washington and federal programs could be worth a closer look. However, most of these programs also depend on things we don't know yet, like your age, whether you need help with daily activities, your veteran status, whether you're enrolled in Medicare or Medicaid, your county, your living situation, and whether you have an unpaid caregiver. Because so many key details are still missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a set of recommendations. Please review the follow-up questions below — answering them (or bringing them to a benefits counselor or the listed agencies) will make this screening much more useful.

report.overallSummary: Based on what's been shared so far, this adult in Washington state needs hands-on help with toileting, dressing, and eating, has a heart disease diagnosis, and has countable assets under $2,000. That combination of care needs and low assets lines up with the ADL and resource tests used by several Washington programs. However, many of the other facts these programs check — such as age, monthly income, current Medicare or Medicaid enrollment, county of residence, living situation, and whether an unpaid family caregiver is involved — are still unknown. Because so many key details are missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a conclusion. The follow-up questions below will help narrow down which programs are actually worth pursuing with an agency representative.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The need for hands-on help with toileting, dressing, and eating suggests a significant care need, and COPES uses an ADL-based assessment to decide if someone meets a nursing-facility level of care. Countable assets under $2,000 also matches the resource limit this program uses. Age and monthly income are still unknown, and both affect whether this program is worth pursuing further.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can add services on top of basic Medicaid personal care, including adult day care or adult day health, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To be considered, an assessment must find that the person needs help with 3 or more activities of daily living (like eating, toileting, or dressing) at a certain level, among other possible criteria for a nursing-facility level of care.
The resource limit checked for this program is $2,000 for a single person, which matches the assets reported.

The person's approximate monthly income

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with toileting, dressing, and eating fits the kind of substantial ADL need (3 or more ADLs) that Community First Choice is built around. Whether the person is already enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan coverage) is not yet known, and that is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, plus related tasks such as meal preparation and housework when ADL help is also needed.

It also covers relief care, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and training for family caregivers on managing personal care providers, and services can be delivered at home, in an adult family home, or in assisted living.
To keep receiving this benefit, a later reassessment must find an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.

nextSteps: Check current Apple Health (Medicaid) enrollment status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This program is the financial gateway to several Washington long-term care services, and the reported ADL needs (toileting, dressing, eating) and assets under $2,000 line up with two of its four eligibility checks. whatItCovers.text: Apple Health long-term care is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care and the financial pathway into programs like COPES, PACE, and other home and community-based waivers.
Financial eligibility depends partly on whether monthly income is at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway available for income above that.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares provides a lifetime benefit of $36,500 (2026), paid directly to approved providers, covering services like in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, respite care, and residential care.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington offers vouchers of up to $1,000 per household per year for unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours of care per week, usable for in-home respite, adult day services, and similar short-term relief.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus support groups, counseling, training, and equipment for unpaid caregivers of adults who need care, with amounts and rules varying by Area Agency on Aging.

nextSteps: Contact the local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about FCSP and Lifespan Respite options.
