report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared so far — an adult in Washington state who needs help with mobility, eating, and bathing, is divorced, and has countable assets under $2,000 — several programs may be worth a closer look. However, many of the details these programs actually use to sort people (age, income, veteran status, Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, county of residence, living situation, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved) are still unknown. Because so much key information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a set of recommendations. Answering the follow-up questions below will make the next screening much more useful, and only the administering agency for each program can confirm actual eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility, eating, and bathing, and your countable assets are under $2,000 — both line up with pieces of COPES' rules. COPES requires a functional assessment showing nursing-facility level of care, which often involves needing help with several ADLs like the ones you mentioned, and its resource limit is $2,000 for a single person.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Washington Medicaid waiver, run by DSHS Home and Community Services, that adds extra home-and-community-based services on top of Community First Choice personal care for people whose needs exceed what CFC alone provides.

It can cover adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, skilled nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation, among other services, in a private home, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

To move forward, an assessment must show a need for nursing-facility level of care — generally meaning an unmet or partially met need in multiple ADLs such as eating, bathing, or mobility — and the resource limit is $2,000 for a single applicant.

informationStillNeeded: Your age (COPES generally applies to people 65+ or 18-64 with an SSI-criteria disability)
Your monthly income (to check it against the $2,982/month Special Income Level)
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment
Submit the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax
Ask HCS how your specific income and age affect eligibility, since eligibility can only be confirmed by the agency

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with mobility, eating, and bathing suggests a substantial ADL need, which is part of what CFC looks at for ongoing services. Whether you're already enrolled in community Medicaid (CN/ABP) — a separate requirement — isn't yet known.

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Washington Medicaid state plan option for personal care for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community, covering help with ADLs like bathing, eating, dressing, and mobility, plus IADLs, relief care, and personal emergency response systems.
To continue receiving CFC at reassessment, a person must have an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Financially, CFC requires eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan community Medicaid coverage, not institutional financial rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in (or could enroll in) Apple Health community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
nextSteps: Check your current Apple Health/Medicaid enrollment status
If not enrolled, ask HCS about applying for community Medicaid alongside a CARE assessment
Request a CARE assessment through regional HCS central intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described needs with mobility, eating, and bathing, plus assets under $2,000, touch on two of the pieces Apple Health LTC looks at — functional need and resources — but age and income are still unknown, and those matter too.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, serving as the financial gateway to programs like COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS, administered by the Health Care Authority and DSHS Home and Community Services.
Functional eligibility for HCS clients is based on the CARE assessment finding nursing facility level of care, and resource limits are $2,000 for a single person.
Income eligibility runs through the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), with a Medically Needy pathway for people above that level.

informationStillNeeded: Your age (to check the 65+ or 18-64-with-disability pathway)
Your monthly income (to check it against the Special Income Level)
nextSteps: Apply online at washingtonconnection.org or submit the HCA 18-005 paper application
Complete the required interview with an HCS financial specialist
Request the CARE functional assessment through regional HCS intake

programs.whyThisMayApply: placeholder

Needing help with mobility, eating, and bathing (three ADLs) matches the WA Cares care-need trigger, which requires help with 3 or more of the counted activities. Whether you've contributed enough through work to qualify for a benefit is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares is Washington's public long-term care insurance program, funded by a payroll premium, offering a lifetime benefit (currently $36,500 for 2026) that pays approved providers directly for services such as in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, respite care, and residential care.

The care-need trigger is needing help with 3 or more of seven counted ADLs — bathing, bed mobility, eating, medication management, mobility, transferring, and toileting — expected to last at least 90 days.
To receive benefits, a worker generally must have contributed for a set number of years, unless they qualify under the transition pathway for people born before 1968.

informationStillNeeded: Your work history and years of contribution to WA Cares
Whether you have an approved WA Cares exemption
nextSteps: Create a WA Cares account online or call 844-CARE4WA to check your contribution status
If eligible on contributions, ask about the care-needs assessment process
Contact a local Area Agency on Aging for help navigating the application

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with daily activities like bathing and eating, which fits the kind of clinical need A&A looks for. However, whether you are a veteran, your service dates, and your discharge type — all required for the underlying VA pension — are unknown, so this program's relevance can't be judged yet.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans (and some survivors) who need help with daily activities, added on top of the base Veterans Pension.
One clinical criterion is needing another person's help to perform daily activities such as bathing, feeding, or dressing.

The underlying pension requires a discharge that was not dishonorable, service including at least one day during a recognized wartime period, and income/net worth within limits (net worth limit $163,699 effective December 1, 2025).
The benefit is a monthly cash payment the household can use at its discretion for care costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran of the U.S. military
Your dates and era of service
Your discharge type
nextSteps: Gather military discharge paperwork (DD-214) if applicable
Contact a Veterans Service Officer to discuss potential eligibility
If eligible on service history, ask about VA Form 21-2680 for the clinical assessment

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit requires being substantially confined to home by a permanent disability, which hasn't been described in your profile, and — as with Aid & Attendance — veteran status, service era, and discharge type are unknown, so this program's relevance can't be assessed yet.

whatItCovers.text: The Housebound benefit is an increased monthly VA pension payment for veterans who are substantially confined to their home because of a permanent disability.

It requires meeting the same underlying pension rules as Aid & Attendance — discharge status, wartime service, and income/net worth limits — plus either a 60%+ additional disability rating or being permanently confined to home.
It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a claimant gets one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are a veteran of the U.S. military
Your dates and era of service
Your discharge type
Whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability
nextSteps: Gather military discharge paperwork (DD-214) if applicable
Contact a Veterans Service Officer to discuss whether Housebound or Aid & Attendance fits better
Discuss homebound status with your doctor if applicable

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described needs with mobility, eating, and bathing suggest a personal or skilled care need, which is part of what this benefit looks at. Whether you're enrolled in Medicare and whether you live at home (rather than a facility) are still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide care (only alongside skilled nursing or therapy) at no cost for the covered services.
Eligibility requires homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and care ordered by a provider and delivered by a Medicare-certified agency.
It does not cover 24-hour care, home meal delivery, or custodial/personal care when that is the only care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare
Whether you currently live at home (not in a nursing facility)
nextSteps: Confirm your Medicare enrollment status
Ask your doctor whether a home health referral and face-to-face assessment would apply to your situation

programs.whyThisMayApply: This benefit depends on a recent qualifying hospital stay, which hasn't been mentioned in your profile, and Medicare Part A enrollment is also unknown, so it's unclear yet whether this applies to your situation.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying hospital stay, for up to 100 days per benefit period.

Eligibility requires a medically necessary inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, admission to the SNF generally within 30 days of leaving the hospital, and a doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed.
Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, and therapy services, with no daily cost for days 1-20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21-100.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in Medicare Part A
Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days
nextSteps: Check your Medicare Part A enrollment and benefit period status
If a hospital stay occurs in the future, ask hospital discharge staff whether it would qualify

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with mobility, eating, and bathing points toward a possible nursing-facility level of care, which PACE requires. However, your age and county of residence — both required to check service-area and age eligibility — are unknown.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community, through an interdisciplinary team.

Eligibility requires being age 55 or older, living in a PACE organization's service area, needing nursing-home level of care as certified by the state, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help.
Washington PACE coverage is currently limited to King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties, and coverage within a county is not complete, so a specific ZIP code must be checked.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your county of residence, to check whether it's in a PACE service area
nextSteps: Confirm which county and ZIP code you live in
If in King, Pierce, Spokane, or Snohomish County, contact the relevant PACE organization's intake line
Ask about the state's nursing-facility level of care certification process

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your resources under $2,000 are well within TSOA's limits, and your described ADL needs may support a nursing-facility level of care finding. However, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, and your age, income, and Medicaid enrollment status are still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people age 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, along with their unpaid caregivers, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants go on a first-come, first-served waitlist, and there is no announced reopen date, though current participants keep their services.
Financial limits (as of 1/1/2026) are income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 for a single person.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Your monthly income
Whether you are currently enrolled in CN/ABP Apple Health Medicaid
nextSteps: Understand that new applications currently only join a statewide waitlist due to the enrollment pause
Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the waitlist and any available non-Medicaid respite funds in the meantime

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described ADL needs may support a nursing-facility level of care finding, which MAC requires, but this program is also paused statewide, and several required details — your age, current Medicaid enrollment, living situation, and whether you have an unpaid family caregiver — are unknown.

whatItCovers.text: MAC supports the unpaid family caregivers of people age 55+ who are already on Apple Health Medicaid, meet nursing-facility level of care, and have chosen not to use other Medicaid LTSS.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopen date; current participants keep their services.

Covered categories include caregiver assistance services like respite care, housekeeping, adult day care, training and education, and specialized medical equipment; personal assistance services are not available under MAC.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Whether you're currently enrolled in CN/ABP Apple Health Medicaid
Whether you live in your own home or another person's home (not a licensed facility)
Whether you have an unpaid family caregiver age 18 or older
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to ask about the current waitlist
Confirm current Apple Health Medicaid enrollment status

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing ongoing help with mobility, eating, and bathing points to an ongoing care need, which several respite programs are designed to support. Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care — a requirement for most respite options — is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Respite care programs in Washington offer short-term relief for unpaid family caregivers through three main channels: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers, the Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP), and DDA waiver respite (only for developmental disabilities).

Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year, delivered through contracted agencies for in-home respite, adult day services, or other options, though awards are limited by funding and priority need.
FCSP offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, plus support groups, counseling, and caregiver training, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

Lifespan Respite requires the unpaid caregiver to provide 40+ hours per week of care or supervision, with both caregiver and care receiver living in Washington and unable to afford respite on their own.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and about how many hours per week
nextSteps: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to discuss respite options
If applicable, have the unpaid caregiver complete the Lifespan Respite Washington application or request an FCSP screening

What is your approximate monthly income? (This affects Medicaid-related programs and TSOA.)

Are you enrolled in Medicare (Part A and/or Part B)?

Have you had a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently that required inpatient admission?

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — you are 91, live in your own home, need help with dressing, mobility, and eating, have heart disease, monthly income under $1,000, and countable assets somewhere between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington programs may be worth a closer look, especially those tied to age, care needs, and low income. However, a number of details that these programs actually check (your exact asset total, marital status, whether you're already enrolled in Medicare and/or Medicaid, which county you live in, whether you have an unpaid family caregiver, and any recent hospital stay) are still unknown. Because so many of these specific eligibility factors are missing, please treat this screening as a starting point rather than a final answer, and use the follow-up questions below to get a more accurate picture before contacting any agency. Nothing here is an eligibility determination — only the administering agency can confirm eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 91, you are well past the age-65 mark this waiver looks for, and needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating reflects the kind of daily-activity needs its functional assessment considers. Your reported monthly income (under $1,000) is well below the program's income threshold. Your countable assets fall somewhere in a $2,000–$85,000 range, which is too wide to tell whether you're at or below the $2,000 single-person resource limit this program uses.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers additional waiver services on top of personal care, including adult day care/adult day health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, environmental modifications, and transportation, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered, a CARE assessment must find that the person needs nursing-facility level of care — for example, an unmet or partially met need in three or more activities of daily living such as eating, transfer, or mobility.
Applying involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a request for a CARE assessment through regional HCS intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Needing help with dressing, mobility, and eating touches three activities of daily living, which lines up with the ongoing-eligibility rule this program uses. whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, and mobility, along with related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, for people living at home or in certain community settings.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your age (91) and reported need for help with dressing, mobility, and eating align with the kind of nursing-facility-level-of-care assessment this program uses, and your monthly income under $1,000 is well within its income standard. whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care and also serves as the financial gateway to community programs like COPES, PACE, and Community First Choice.
